Consequently, an agency seeking to replicate the structure using the best practices of an external agency may not operate in a manner that meets its own community's needs for public safety. However, by embracing the contingency perspective, law enforcement agencies can tailor their RTCC structures to the unique challenges they face and optimize their effectiveness in maintaining public safety.

Within the institutional perspective, police agencies are found to be more reliant on social pressures for conformity and legitimacy than on technical pressures for economic performance. For example, diffusion studies often demonstrate a bell-shaped curve for the adoption of innovations over time, with innovators, mainstream adopters, laggards, and resisters contributing to the curve (Grubler, 1996). Innovators, who are considered risktakers with ample resources to absorb setbacks, are responsible for the initial adoption period by experimenting with innovations and demonstrating their utility.

Conversely, police agencies that refuse to conform to changes in the policing landscape may be viewed as laggers or resisters. In other words, if they fail to consider implementing a real-time crime center, a rapidly diffusing police innovation across the country, they may be deemed irrational in their decision-making. The institutional perspective can be further evidenced by agencies that have embraced the RTCC and who often cite some aspect of social and institutional pressures from surrounding agencies as their motivation for adoption. For instance, the Police Chief of New Haven recently acknowledged that the agency is modeling its crime center after those used in Hartford, Bridgeport, and Waterbury, all of which have been highly successful (Mirmina, 2023).

From a rational standpoint, it is postulated that police organizations exhibit rational behavior by outlining objectives and constructing a framework for decision-making along with strategies to achieve said objectives. Nevertheless, this perspective carries inherent limitations. The goals established by law enforcement agencies may not always be straightforward, as they might encounter obstacles due to limited knowledge on how to effectively navigate structural changes, while resource constraints may impede their endeavors. For instance, an agency might introduce an RTCC to bolster and streamline the achievement of a specific policing model (such as community-based or crime-controloriented). However, the ever-evolving landscape may necessitate continuous adaptation and adjustment of best practices associated with operating such a unit.

# Multi-Perspective Framework

In light of the aforementioned observations, and to address the original research question, it can be argued that each distinct viewpoint presented in the previous section possesses its own inherent value throughout the various phases of adopting and implementing RTTCs.7 This assertion is best demonstrated by referring to Figure 2, where in the initial stage of adoption, external institutional influences can act as a compelling catalyst. While not necessarily mandatory, the anecdotal success of RTCCs in neighboring jurisdictions, empirical evidence demonstrating increased crime reduction or clearance rates, and the visible display of community support all contribute to the concept's legitimacy, thus promoting acceptance among other agencies. Moreover, the possibility of collaborating with partner agencies to expand network coverage and enhance the ability to identify and apprehend transient offenders or suspects may also emerge as an institutional pressure, resulting in an agency adopting additional technologies that are consistent with other systems to improve efficiency and provide officers with real-time information on demand (Avalos, 2016).

![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/6057a4fc90e3788e4cd31aa3a7cec3dc8cc549780b3815a8b057d3a97c1999c3.jpg)

Figure 2. Multi-perspective framework of RTCC adoption and implementation

The second stage of the RTCC implementation process aligns with the fundamental assumptions of the contingency perspective. In this stage, the organization has already adopted an RTCC unit, but the structural and operational arrangements are contingent upon several factors. The availability of financial resources and grant funding, as well as the degree of stakeholder involvement, are among the most commonly considered factors. These elements significantly impact the development of standard operating procedures for the unit, staffing decisions, and the quality and quantity of technology acquired. Additionally, they influence the likelihood of future expansion of the RTCC unit.

The ultimate stage of the process is an ongoing one, in which the decision-making process concerning the optimal operation of the unit and the procurement of technology may evolve to fulfill the goals established by the department and adjust to changing societal circumstances over time. Whether it be to meet heightened demands of producing postincident investigative products or aid in other tasks that fall under the umbrella of the investigative RTCC unit, changes are likely to occur and should be anticipated. This is also true for any changes made to the budget restrictions upheld by the department.

# Preliminary Typology of Existent RTCC Models

This section will expound upon the various types of RTCCs that have been deployed throughout the United States. To fully comprehend the nuances of these models, extent literature and open-source information will be utilized to develop a typology that aptly captures their salient characteristics. Essentially, a typology serves as a system of classification that arranges objects or concepts based on shared features or attributes (Nichols, 2007). In policing research, typologies have been employed to classify a diverse range of phenomena, such as cybercrime (Phillips et al., 2022), organized crime (Le, 2012), domestic violence (Johnson, 2008), and offender characteristics (Kooyman, 2012), among others. By aggregating criminal activities into categories, researchers can discern similarities and differences in offender behavior, motivations, and victim profiles.

Additionally, typologies can be instrumental in designing policing strategies and interventions. For instance, a typology of domestic violence offenders may enable law enforcement officers to detect early warning signs and devise preemptive measures to prevent further harm to victims. Similarly, in the context of RTCC implementation, a typology can provide a foundation for agencies that are considering future adoption or seeking direction in constructing an infrastructure. It can also assist in facilitating appropriate evaluations that are consistent with the intended objectives of the RTCC of interest.

# Method Used to Develop Typology

The development of this typology is an expansion of a prior inquiry conducted on the diffusion of RTCCs at a national level (Przeszlowski et al., 2022). The study referenced surveyed police agencies across the country to assess the nature of their RTCC implementation. The results indicated that 1) RTCCs are proliferating at a rapid pace but are still in their early innovation/adoption phase, 2) there is no single model of their use, 3) most employ a diverse range of technologies and information sources, and 4) most enable the sharing of information with partners in real-time.

Within the previous study, various databases were examined, encompassing Google, Google News, Google Videos, YouTube, the International Association of Crime Analysts (IACA), and LinkedIn, to accumulate an all-inclusive inventory of existing RTCCs. A forum was created on the IACA platform to facilitate the exchange of information among crime analysts regarding their real-time crime centers. Additionally, searches on LinkedIn provided insights into the deployment of these centers and individuals who work within them. Here, the results of the search identified 61 RTCCs or their equivalent.

For each of the 61 RTCCs identified, contact information was obtained by searching publicized reports and contacting the police departments directly. The survey consisted of 25 questions designed to gather information on three substantive areas, including organizational characteristics, organizational structure, and operational procedures. The survey was administered through an online platform using Qualtrics software, which is a powerful online survey tool suitable for academic research. The survey contained both closed and open-ended questions and was distributed in May 2020. To improve response rates, two rounds of electronic reminder letters via email in three-week intervals, with a final round of targeted electronic requests sent in August 2020. At the end of the data collection period, 44 of the 61 identified RTCCs responded to the Qualtrics survey request, resulting in a response rate of 72 percent.

Here, in pursuit of developing the typology, a comparable approach was adopted. The data collection process concluded in March 2023, revealing a staggering expansion, with a total of 103 police agencies that have established operational RTCCs. Although an additional survey was not disseminated, pertinent information from available the previously mentioned sources was extracted (i.e., intended goals, structural characteristics, etc.) to create a classification system. The ensuing section outlines the findings.

# Findings

As depicted in Table 2, the extant literature on the internal composition of RTCCs can be organized into three comprehensive categories, forming a preliminary typology. This typology classifies RTCC attributes according to command structure (centralized authority, regional authority, or autonomous), configurations (centralized, decentralized, or cloud-based), territory (jurisdiction, district, or digital), and premise (crime-control, crime-reduction, or incident-based), thus grouping them by type (traditional, community, or virtual).

# Traditional Model

In the traditional model, a single command and control center assumes the responsibility of overseeing all operations. This model is distinguished by a hierarchical structure, where power and decision-making responsibilities are centralized under a central authority. This centralized RTCC model is typically deployed in densely populated urban centers, where there is a high incidence of criminal activity and a pressing need for a coordinated response (Guerette, et al., 2022), The centralization of the RTCC model provides a superior level of control and management efficacy. This approach allows for the prompt deployment of resources, streamlined communication between relevant agencies, and a well-organized decision-making process. The success of the centralized RTCC model can be attributed to its ability to leverage cutting-edge technology and data-driven decisionmaking, enabling it to respond effectively to crime-related incidents.

Table 2. Real-time crime center typology

|     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Type | Command | Configurations | Territory | Premise | Example |
| Traditional | Central Authority | Centralized; Single Control Center | Jurisdiction | Crime- Control | Miami Police Department |
| Community | Regional Authority | Decentralized; Multiple Control Centers | District | Crime- Reduction | Chicago Police Department |
| Virtual | Autonomous | Cloud-Based; Digital | Beat | Incident- Based | Chula Vista Police Department |

Note. This is a preliminary typology.

To some agencies, this model may prove impractical or unfavorable for several reasons including resource constraints, dependence on technology, and limited flexibility. For example, the cost of establishing and maintaining a centralized RTCC can be prohibitive (e.g., NYPD’s RTCC cost 11 million dollars to implement, recurring costs not included), particularly for small law enforcement agencies with limited budgets (NYC Global Partners, 2010). Secondly, centralized RTCCs depend heavily on technology for the gathering, analysis, and dissemination of crime data. Any failure or malfunction of the technology infrastructure can disrupt the flow of information and adversely impact decision-making (Bureau of Justice Assistance, 2021). Thirdly, the rigid hierarchical structure can lead to delays in responding to rapidly evolving situations. This limitation can be especially problematic in situations that require quick action, such as active shooter incidents (Mastrofski & Willis, 2010).

# Miami Police Department Real-Time Crime Center

Miami is a dense urban area of about 36 square miles. It has a population of roughly 450,00 individuals from various backgrounds. According to the US Census, demographically it is comprised of seventy percent $( 7 0 % )$ Hispanic, sixteen percent $( 1 6 % )$ African American/Black, and fourteen percent $( 1 4 % )$ White Non-Hispanic population. The city has continuously grown as an urban area and has enjoyed a resurgence as a metropolitan hub with one of the highest tourist-to-local ratios with nearly 1,600 visitors to every 100 residents. Yet, poverty levels remain higher than the national average. Like most major urban cities, crime tends to cluster in several concentrated neighborhood areas. It is there where the RTCC technologies had been predominantly deployed. The City of Miami Police department was established in 1896 and has since grown to become one of the largest police departments in the southeastern United States, with over 1,300 sworn officers and over 400 civilian personnel.

The MPD's RTCC serves as the hub of the department's technological infrastructure, providing critical intelligence and investigative support to its officers and detectives. Located within the central station, the MPD's RTCC is characterized by a hierarchical structure with a clear chain of command and decision-making power vested in a central authority. The center is responsible for managing all RTCC operations, including active monitoring, responding to crime-related incidents in real-time, providing, postincident investigative support and serving as a command center (Guerette et al., 2022). To this end, the RTCC is manned by a team of nine (9) highly proficient detectives, who monitor live feeds from cameras and sensors distributed across the city, as well as social media, facial recognition, and other online platforms.

Primary Technologies: CCTV, ShotSpotter (gunshot detection system), Clarity/Vigilant (automated license plate readers), Faces/Clearview (facial recognition software).

# Community-Based Model

In contrast to the centralized RTCC model, the Community RTCC model is highly decentralized, with multiple smaller centers distributed across a geographic area. Each center has its own command and control center and operates independently of each other. This RTCC model emphasizes engagement with the community to prevent crime and works closely with community groups and organizations to identify and address local crime issues (e.g., hosting community events). By involving the community, the RTCC can gather valuable information about local criminal activities and take preventive measures to address them. Community involvement also fosters trust and collaboration between law enforcement and citizens, creating a safer and more secure environment for all.

This model also leverages technology to enhance policing efforts, but with a different objective in mind. It employs advanced analytics and data-driven decisionmaking to identify crime patterns and predict potential criminal activity. By utilizing such technologies, the RTCC can proactively target and prevent criminal activities, ultimately reducing the crime rate in the community (Hollywood et al., 2019). Moreover, having more decentralized units and less camera feeds to monitor may prove to be advantageous (Piza et al., 2019). Despite the advantages of this RTCC model, it also presents some challenges. One of the primary challenges is the need for effective communication and coordination among the different centers. Additionally, this RTCC model may require greater investment in technology and resources to achieve the same level of efficacy as a centralized model.

# Chicago Police Department Strategic Decision Support Centers

Chicago, a diverse and vibrant city located in the Midwest region of the United States, boasts a population of 2.7 million people living within its 234 square miles. In accordance with the US Census, the city's demographics are diverse, with a population that is $31 %$ White, $30 %$ Black, $2 9 %$ Hispanic or Latino, and $6 %$ Asian, among other ethnic groups. The Chicago Police Department (CPD), with over 13,000 sworn officers, is among the largest police forces in the country and is responsible for serving 22 districts. On average, each CPD district is responsible for providing law enforcement services to about 125,000 people over an area of 11 square miles, although population and district sizes vary significantly across the city (The Civic Federation, 2022).

Since 2017, the Chicago Police Department has implemented a distinctive type of RTCC known as Strategic Decision Support Centers across 13 of its 22 districts (Hollywood, et al. 2019). The SDSCs are small conference rooms equipped with a variety of technologies, as well as three wall monitors, workstations, and a conference table. They are typically staffed with two to four individuals per shift, including both sworn officers and civilian analysts. Analysts generally work during day shifts, while each SDSC is supervised by a sergeant or lieutenant.

The SDSCs not only provide a workspace for briefings and collaborative efforts beyond the individual work of the SDSC staff but also serve as a central hub for data analysis, surveillance, and information sharing. By leveraging cutting-edge technologies, such as license plate readers, gunshot detection systems, and predictive analytics, the SDSCs help the CPD identify crime hotspots and make data-driven decisions to reduce crime in the city. The SDSC model prioritizes not only technology but also community engagement and collaboration, with the CPD closely collaborating with community groups and organizations to identify and address local crime issues (Hollywood et al., 2019).

Primary Technologies: CCTV, Genetec Citigraf (public safety mapping display), CVRM (predictive policing software), Link Chart (social network analysis).

# Virtual Model

A virtual real-time crime center is a cloud-based and fully digital model that functions as a centralized hub for gathering, analyzing, and disseminating critical data and intelligence in the law enforcement domain (Merchant, 2020). This innovative model, unlike the traditional physical facilities housing RTCCs, can operate entirely online, accessible from any location with an internet connection or through a mobile data terminal. The virtual RTCC offers an economical solution that can be accessed remotely, making it an appealing option for small to mid-sized law enforcement agencies. Furthermore, it provides an efficient and scalable solution that can improve public safety and security in communities where resources are scarce (Flock Safety, 2022).

The primary goal of the virtual RTCC is to enhance situational awareness and improve decision-making at the beat level by providing real-time information on crime trends, incidents, and threats to patrol officers in the field. This type of RTCC leverages advanced data analysis and visualization tools to discern patterns, relationships, and anomalies in crime data, thereby enabling more effective resource allocation, operational planning, and tactical response. By facilitating data-driven policing strategies and empowering officers with relevant and timely information, virtual RTCCs represent a significant step forward in the quest to improve public safety and bolster community-police relations.

# Chula Vista Police Department Real-Time Crime Center

Chula Vista is a city located in the southern region of San Diego County in California, United States. The city covers an area of approximately 52 square miles and has a population of around 270,000 people, according to the latest U.S. Census Bureau (2022) estimates. The city's population is diverse, with a Hispanic or Latino population of around $59 %$ and a non-Hispanic white population of around $2 7 %$ . The city also has significant Asian and African American populations, each accounting for around $5 %$ of the population. The Chula Vista Police Department (CVPD) is the law enforcement agency responsible for maintaining public safety and order within the city. The CVPD has approximately 260 sworn officers and 100 civilian staff, who serve the community through various divisions, including patrol, investigations, traffic, and community services.

The Chula Vista Police Department has adopted an advanced cloud-based RTCC model to boost its policing efforts. The CVPD's RTCC system leverages the CopLogic Platform, a cloud-based software that provides officers with real-time crime data and intelligence from any device with an internet connection, such as smartphones or mobile data terminals. The CopLogic Platform includes a Live 911 software, which is a significant feature. This software allows patrol officers to monitor incoming emergency calls in realtime, providing critical information about the incident's nature and location before they reach the site (Solis, 2020). This information comprises details such as the caller's address and phone number, any relevant comments or notes from previous calls, and previous incidents at the same location. The Live 911 software also equips officers with a map of the incident location, facilitating speedy and efficient navigation to the scene.

The CVPD's cloud based RTCC model extends beyond Live 911 software, enabling officers to access a wealth of crime data and intelligence such as real-time traffic data, crime reports, and predictive analytics. This capability empowers officers to make wellinformed decisions and adopt a proactive approach to policing. Moreover, since 2018, the CVPD has also implemented a “Drone as First Responder Program” started using drones to help with investigations and provide better situational awareness for officers responding to calls (Cohen, 2022). This initiative became the pioneering program in the nation to secure Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) endorsement, which permits law enforcement to observe anything beyond their visual range from aerial viewpoints. Access to drone information is further accessible to patrol.

Primary Technologies: CCTV, Drones (unmanned aerial systems), Live911 (public safety mapping display), Vigilant (automated license plate reader).

# Chapter Four: The Influence of RTCC Technologies on the Case Processing and Outcomes of Homicides and Aggravated Assaults

# Introduction

The primary objective of this chapter is to examine the influence of police technologies on the outcomes of homicide and aggravated assault incidents. In particular, the research assesses the extent to which RTCC technologies facilitate the investigative case processing of such violent crimes, and how they affect the clearance of cases, including whether they enhance the efficiency of investigators in resolving these cases expeditiously. The research questions guiding this study are therefore three-fold: (1) “What is the impact of RTCC technologies on the clearance of homicide and aggravated assault incidents compared to all other violent crimes?” (2) “To what degree have RTCC technologies been integrated into the case processing of this specific category of crime?” and (3) “Does RTCC support improve the time that it takes to clear these cases?”

This chapter will commence by presenting a literature review pertaining to the examination of homicides and aggravated assaults, and the utilization of technology in their investigation. A subsequent section will detail the analytical approach taken and provide a comprehensive overview of the data collection process and the measures involved within the analysis. Once all analytical approaches are exhausted, a supplemental analysis will be presented to augment the findings and provide additional insight into the impact of the technologies assessed. Limitations and policy implications for this and all subsequent studies will be located in chapter eight.

# Review of Literature

To establish a foundation for this assessment, two relevant bodies of literature will be utilized. The first relates to the factors found to influence the solvability of homicides and aggravated assaults, and the second pertains to the role of technology in the investigation of these crimes.

Factors Known to Impact Case Clearance

As compared to other forms of violent crime, the study of factors influencing the case clearance of homicides and aggravated assaults have been investigated to a greater extent (Jarvis, Mancik, & Regoeczi, 2017; Scott & Wellford, 2021). This research is largely motivated by several things. Firstly, homicides and aggravated assaults are regarded as the most heinous and brutal crimes, and their resolution is a paramount objective for law enforcement agencies (Doucette, Green, Dineen, et al., 2021).8 Secondly, these incidents frequently have a higher likelihood of being reported to law enforcement than other criminal activities, which increase the chances of an investigation taking place (Gramlich, 2021). Thirdly, the nature of these incidents, such as the existence of tangible evidence and witness accounts, can offer investigators with more leads and evidence to work with, leading to an increased likelihood of case clearance (Cook, Braga, Turchan, & Barao, 2019). Lastly, solving these types of crimes can bring a sense of closure to the victim's family and the community, which is a significant impetus for law enforcement agencies to focus on these cases (Riedel & Jarvis, 1999).

# Homicide Case Clearance

Wellford and Cronin's (1999) scholarly inquiry unveiled a multitude of investigation-related factors that influenced the probability of arrest. Certain factors were within the purview of the police, while others were not. Within the realm of policecontrolled factors, the initial actions taken by first responders, the promptness of detective arrival, the thorough documentation of the crime scene, and the level of collaboration with the crime and medical examiner's laboratory were all found to be significant. In terms of personnel, the researchers found that assigning more than one investigator to a case improved clearance rate, as did training detectives on proper investigative techniques. Factors outside of police control included the victim's race, the presence of witnesses, the type of weapon used and the whether the weapon was located at the scene of the crime.

Subsequent research has divulged that specific investigative pursuits, such as the gathering of supplementary evidence, the cultivation of cooperative informants, and the execution of search warrants, heighten the likelihood of resolving homicide cases (Alexander, 2012; Wellford et al., 2019). Furthermore, incidents involving firearms are relatively less likely to be cleared compared to those that entail the use of weapons like knives and blunt instruments, which require closer physical proximity (Addington, 2006; Litwin, 2004). Various investigations, for instance, those conducted by Lee (2000), Litwin (2004), and Regoeczi, Jarvis, and Riedel (2008), have also identified specific characteristics of individual cases, such as the demographics of the victim (age, race, and gender), which influence the probability of case resolution. In general, victim race holds less importance than the severity of the offense and the presence of evidence, and research on homicide clearance rates has produced mixed results concerning the gender of the victim. While some scholars suggest that homicide clearance is equally probable for male and female victims (Addington, 2006; Litwin and Xu, 2007; Puckett and Lundman, 2003) others assert that homicide clearance is more likely for female victims than for males (Lee, 2005; Regoeczi, Jarvis & Mancik, 2020; Regoeczi, Jaris & Riedel., 2008).

The intricate relationship between physical evidence and the effectiveness of criminal investigations is a matter of considerable complexity, as posited by Jarvis and Regoeczi (2012). While a majority of scholars argue that forensic examination of evidence recovered from the initial crime scene carries minimal statistical significance for clearance rates (McEwen, 2010; McEwen & Regoeczi, 2015; Schroeder & White, 2009), the delayed processing of such evidence could potentially undermine its value. More recent research conducted on homicides in Boston has discovered a robust statistical association between various investigative measures, including pre- and post-arrest forensic tests, and the resolution of such cases (Braga, Turchan, & Barao, 2019).

# Aggravated Assault Case Clearance

The available research on the factors that impact the successful resolution of aggravated assaults is somewhat scant. Investigations into the factors associated with the clearance of such assaults have predominantly relied on data from the National IncidentBased Reporting System (NIBRS), which fails to offer any meaningful insights into the nature of the investigation or the pertinent organizational features, implying that incidentrelated variables are the chief predictors (Roberts and Lyons, 2009; Taylor et al., 2009; Overstreet, McNeeley, & Lapsey, 2021). For instance, Roberts and Lyons (2009) utilized NIBRS data from 2005 to predict the time required to clear aggravated assault cases, discovering that the racial identity of both the victim and the offender influenced the case's resolution, while age and gender were indicators of the time necessary to achieve a favorable outcome. Similarly, other studies have found that cases involving younger victims or minority groups may have lower clearance rates, and assaults committed by African American offenders are less likely to be cleared than those perpetrated by white offenders (Taylor, Holleran & Topalli, 2009). Stolzenberg and D'Alessio's (2004) research has also revealed that African American females are more prone to being arrested in connection with aggravated assault incidents than their white counterparts.

While prior research has shed some light on the clearance of aggravated assaults, its methodological limitations have hindered the incorporation of a comprehensive range of explanatory variables. To address this gap, Baskin and Sommers (2012) undertook an analysis of official record data from five U.S. jurisdictions (namely, Los Angeles County, Indianapolis, Evansville, and South Bend) to identify the factors that impact the charging and conviction of aggravated assault. Their findings indicate that extralegal factors, such as victim demographics and suspect-victim relationships, significantly impact the probability of arrest. Specifically, arrests were more likely in cases involving white victims, black or Latina female suspects, and intimate relationships. Legal variables, such as the presence of victim accounts or crime scene evidence, also increased the likelihood of arrest. However, the study discovered that physical evidence was only examined before arrest in a meager four percent of cases.

Moreover, Scott and Wellford's (2021) investigation delved into the organizational and investigative characteristics that predicted the successful resolution of aggravated assault cases, while controlling for factors at the incident level. The study's principal findings suggest that the relationship between investigative effort and case clearance is significantly influenced by the organizational practices of an agency, including the number of best practices implemented. However, this correlation does not consistently result in favorable outcomes for case clearance. Rather, the likelihood of case clearance is more significantly influenced by case-specific factors, such as the recovery of a weapon, the presence of a cooperative witness, and incidents involving white victims. Additionally, after considering all variables in their models, the study found no significant connection between crime scene processing and case clearance. Instead, cases with physical evidence collected during the initial police response phase were nearly $50 %$ less likely to result in clearance. Ultimately, the authors underscore the pressing need for further investigation into this particular type of crime.

# Impact of Technology on Homicide and Aggravated Assault Investigations

Merely a handful of investigations have delved into the influence of innovative police technology on resolving violent crimes. Instead, examinations of measures associated with homicide clearance typically center on the organizational, investigative, and case dimensions, as per a review of 46 studies on violent crime conducted by Wellford et al. (2019). The limited amount of research in police technology may stem from various factors, such as insufficient funds for technology development and implementation, inadequate comprehension of the potential benefits of technology in policing, and the intricacy of integrating technology with preexisting policing practices. For instance, while advanced forensic evidence (such as cellphone records, CCTV, or DNA) is acknowledged as crucial during the prosecutorial stage of case processing, investigators often encounter frustration due to their limited expertise in handling the vast quantities of data produced by these technologies, as well as the resource constraints that come with them (Gibsb Van Brunschot, et al., 2022).

Research endeavors investigating the role of technology generally focused on its potential to function as a preventive mechanism or crime deterrent and have evaluated its ability to reduce criminal activities in specific areas. For instance, Caplan, Kenndey, and Petrossian (2011) conducted an assessment of the impact of police-monitored CCTV cameras on shootings, auto thefts, and thefts in Newark, NJ, and found that the benefits were minimal. Furthermore, they noted that the benefit was more significant for auto thefts than for shooting incidents and that the cameras were not as effective in discouraging street crimes.

Similarly, Piza and colleagues (2014) discovered that the integration of proactive CCTV monitoring with direct police patrol leads to greater crime control benefits than solely relying on CCTV deployment. In their study, they evaluated the effects of this combination and determined that it surpasses the efficacy of using CCTV as a stand-alone measure. This intervention was particularly beneficial for street and disorder crimes. More recently, Gerell (2021) investigated whether the implementation of police operatorcontrolled CCTV cameras in a nightlife area would result in a decrease in assaults. Contrary to their initial hypothesis, the outcome revealed a noticeable increase in reported assaults in the area where actively monitored cameras were employed to assist law enforcement in their crime-fighting efforts.

In a quasi-experimental evaluation study conducted by Wheeler and Phillips (2018), the efficacy of roadblocks and automated license plate readers (ALPRs) in reducing crime rates in high-crime areas of Buffalo, NY was investigated. The Buffalo Police

Department (BPD) implemented high-visibility roadblocks in various locations throughout the city during a two-month period in 2013, known as Operation Strikeforce, and used ALPR to identify vehicles associated with warrants, revoked licenses, or stolen status. Only those vehicles flagged as problematic were stopped. Overall, the evaluation showed mixed results in deterring part one violent crimes and reducing traffic accidents, where part one violent crimes (which included homicides and aggravated assaults) showed a statistically significant decrease, while accidents showed a statistically significant increase. This inquiry added to the findings of two preceding empirical investigations that employed ALPRs at crime hot spots. Notably, Lum and colleagues (2011) study revealed no noteworthy effects, whereas Taylor et al.'s (2012) research documented a deterrent impact for longer-term ALPR interventions.

In an attempt to determine impact on case clearance, Doucette and colleagues (2021) conducted a rigorous investigation to evaluate the impact of ShotSpotter technology on case clearance rates. Their study utilized a pooled, cross-sectional time-series analysis to thoroughly examine the influence of this technology on murder arrests, firearm homicides, and weapons arrests across sixty-eight metropolitan areas across the United States between 1999 and 2016. The authors posited that the broad adoption of ShotSpotter technology in diverse jurisdictions would lead to a decline in homicide rates while concurrently yielding a rise in firearm crime arrests. This expectation was rooted in the capacity of the technology and the information offered by the vendor. However, the findings indicated no significant differences in homicide rates, murder arrests, and weapons arrests between counties with ShotSpotter technology and those without it, even after controlling for multiple demographic variables and state firearm laws. Nevertheless, the authors acknowledged a limitation of their study, namely the inability to attribute the results to the exact census tract covered by ShotSpotter, which may have introduced some bias and limited the accuracy of the evaluation of the technology's impact on crime clearance rates.

Despite the insufficient research on the use of ShotSpotter and case clearance, it is important to acknowledge that, as with other forms of technology, law enforcement personnel widely acknowledge the effectiveness of gunshot detection software in investigations. This technology has demonstrated considerable positive outcomes such as reducing response times of officers to the scene of an incident (Mazerolle et al., 1998), facilitating the collection of crucial evidence such as identifying the location of gunfire (Choi, Librett, & Collins., 2014), and generating comprehensive reports on gun-related crimes (Carr & Doleac, 2016).

# Impact of Real-Time Crime Centers

Currently, assessments of RTCCs are restricted to the Strategic Decision Support Centers implemented by the Chicago Police Department and the centralized Real-Time Crime Center employed by the Miami Police Department. 9 The former evaluation revealed some indications of success in reducing homicides and aggravated assaults in districts where SDSCs were deployed (Hollywood et al., 2019), whereas the latter study did not focus on homicides and aggravated assaults exclusively but instead found support for improved crime clearance rates across all categories of violent crime which also included sexual assaults, robberies, and domestic violence incidents (Guerette et al., 2022).

# Current Study

Considering that this data is derived from a broader empirical investigation on the influence of real-time crime centers on case processing and outcomes of violent crime incidents (Guerette, et al., 2022) there is a basis to argue that these violent crimes, when assessed individually, could also profit from the assistance of these investigative entities. Additionally, informed by the clearance rates of this category of crime on a national scale, it is anticipated that there is a higher probability of clearing homicide and aggravated assault cases when compared to all other forms of violent crime (Li & Lartey, 2022). Thus, the following hypotheses are advanced.

\[H1\] Compared to all other forms of violent crime, homicide and aggravated assault incidents will have an improved likelihood of case clearance, controlling for the support of RTCC technologies.

\[H2\] RTCC-technologies will be firmly integrated in the case processing of homicides and aggravated assaults.

\[H3\] RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated assault incidents will experience longer time to case clearance rates as a result of increased investigative effort.

For the purpose of this study homicides and aggravated assaults will be classified as commensurate offenses due to their shared characteristic of involving intentional violence against another person. 10 While homicide refers to the act of killing another individual, aggravated assault pertains to a severe physical attack that inflicts serious harm or injury to another. This classification may arise when an individual, who intends to commit homicide, instead inflicts severe harm on the victim but does not succeed in ending their life. Similarly, when an individual commits aggravated assault, the severity of the injury inflicted may lead to the victim's death, resulting in a homicide charge. Furthermore, both these offenses may entail similar factors, such as the use of weapons, the intent to harm, and the presence of anger or aggression. The gravity of the wounds incurred by the target can also ascertain whether the transgression is classified as an aggravated assault or a homicide. Thus, for some of these reasons, scholars have progressively gravitated toward viewing these classifications of crimes as interconnected, particularly with the incorporation of firearms in the perpetration of the offense (Hipple, 2022).

# Analytical Approach

This study employed four distinct analytical techniques and two forms of analytical software programs to examine the influence of RTCC technologies on the case clearance of homicides and aggravated assaults. First, a set of nested multivariate logistic regression models were employed to determine the likelihood of case clearance for aggravated assault and homicide incidents compared to all violent crime incidents and controlling for RTCC support. The multivariate model was adjusted for various solvability factors or case characteristics that could potentially impact the clearance probability of cases outside the scope of the technologies employed within the unit. Second, module network analysis was used to empirically examine the extent to which RTCC technologies influence the investigative process of these unique crime types. Network analysis involves a set of integrated techniques that aim to depict the relational properties of individual elements and analyze the structures that emerge from the recurrence of these relations (Chiesi, 2001;

Marsden, 2016). Kaplan Meier survival analysis was used to determine differences in the time to case clearance for RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated assault cases compared to the control sample and finally, due a suboptimal model fit, Cox regression with hazard model outputs was used to determine differences in the time to case clearance for RTCCassisted homicide and aggravated assault incidents compared all other forms of violent crime assisted by RTCC technologies. To conduct the logistic regression and survival analyses, Statistical Package for the Social Science (SPSS) was utilized, whereas an opensource online program, Jeffrey’s Amazing Statistics Program (JASP) was used to perform modular network analysis.

# Source of Information

The dataset utilized for this study was acquired through a collaborative Smart Policing Initiative with the City of Miami Police Department, which concluded in October of 2022. This section will detail the process involved in gathering this information, with emphasis on the measures included for analyses that follow.

# RTCC Internal Incident Log

The MPD RTCC kept an internal log within their unit as a method of performance measurement. The significance of the internal log lies in its dual role: firstly, as an in-unit record that enabled RTCC personnel to document their daily undertakings and their efforts toward providing requested incident support, and secondly, as a basis for the development of an extended log that facilitated the eventual evaluation of the unit's performance. The incidents recorded within the internal RTCC log served as a foundation for the extended log, which in turn provided essential data for the initial outcome evaluation and ultimately served as a valuable resource for the present dissertation.

The RTCC unit personnel (e.g., detectives, sworn personnel) were tasked with recording all activity that occurred during operating hours within this established internal log. This included any occurrences that were brought to the attention of the RTCC staff through either dispatch or officers in the field, and whether material assistance was provided by the RTCC. This also included incidents that were identified by RTCC detectives through active monitoring and cases in which the RTCC was asked to assist in the post-incident investigative process.

When an incident was brought to the attention of RTCC personnel during the time of initial response, information sequencing occurred in one of two ways. RTCC personnel were either informed of the incident through dispatch or solicited by officers in the field to provide support. When a call was dispatch-initiated, the incident number was generated through the computer-aided dispatch system and was entered into the log as an identifier by RTCC personnel. When a call was raised by patrol, either the same process occurred, or patrol advised the RTCC detectives of a generated case number. Any immediate information obtained from dispatch or officers in the field was also entered into the log at that time (e.g., location, vehicle, weapon used, etc.) along with an indication of the information sources utilized and any additional notes pertaining to the incident.

For post-investigative support purposes there were two procedures for obtaining support from the RTCC. For requests related to video retrieval, an online video request form was submitted by the lead detective which was routed to the RTCC. This form was reviewed by the RTCC Sergeant who either approved or denied the request based on whether the RTCC had resources that covered the area of the incident in question. If the request was denied, the incident was not recorded in the log. For those that were approved and processed, the case number was recorded in the log. The second procedure related to all other requests that were not video related. In those instances, detectives sent an email request to the RTCC unit for specific support. For occurrences that were identified through active monitoring, RTCC personnel simply recorded the activity in the log. For these instances, there were no formal case numbers that were developed and required no added data validation.

# Time Period of Internal Log Data

The assembly of the internal RTCC log began in January of 2020. The internal RTCC consisted of 3,636 entries, of which 2,228 involved an incident resulting in an assigned case number. Once duplicate case numbers were removed, 1,871 unique incidents remained, and select violent crime and property crime types were extracted for the purpose of eventual evaluation. Despite the date of assembly, the oldest incident that the RTCC provided post-incident investigative support to transpired on January 16th, 2019. 11

# Validity Check

On a weekly basis, the internal RTCC log was reviewed and validated. Any missing information was also added at this time. For instances in which the RTCC personnel are unable to obtain an identifier, MPD’s crime analyst tool was utilized to locate the appropriate case number. This tool can extract all incidents that occurred in a specified area and timeframe. Once an incident or case number was identified, the Department’s records management system, PremierOne was utilized. The record management system stores information pertaining to the type of incident (i.e., signal number), the location of the incident, whether an arrest was made and any additional case details. This information was extracted and added in the log.

Three other sources were used to validate the information in the log. First, the RTCC personnel indicated the information technologies used to assist the individual or unit in the notes section of the database when initially logging the incident. It was then ensured that those details were properly coded within the relevant measures. Second, case files within PremierOne were reviewed to identify instances when the RTCC provided support and the types of information received. Each case entry was then compared to the record management system information to ensure proper coding. Third, request emails sent directly to the unit detectives were reviewed to ensure that the log properly documented all the applicable information technologies used to support the investigation.

# Data and Measures

The internal log included sixteen distinct measures that were determined in collaboration with the MPD command staff to capture the RTCC's activities while responding to or assisting with incidents. These measures aided in identifying incidents and recording relevant numerical and dichotomous measures. For example, the log recorded the case number, date, time, type of incident signal, location, NET area, unit assisted, and type of service provided (real-time, post-incident, active monitoring). Numerical measures such as arrests and charges (felony or misdemeanor) were also included, as were dichotomous measures such as the recovery of weapons or narcotics and involvement of a vehicle. The RTCC resources accessed were coded categorically, and each resource used for each incident (e.g., CCTV, Clearview, ShotSpotter) was likewise recorded. Lastly, personnel provided their personal identification number and described their assistance in a column designated for additional notes.

# Treatment Group

The purpose of the expanded database was to capture additional measures for all violent and property crime incidents which received support from the RTCC. The incidents recorded within the expanded RTCC database had all received real-time or post-hoc deployment of information from the RTCC to responding officers or detectives. The extended database allowed for more in-depth analysis in the assessment of selected outcomes for incidents treated by the RTCC compared to non-RTCC incidents. The procedure for incident collection and recording within the extended RTCC database was as follows. With each identified case number, exhaustive steps were taken to acquire the additional information needed to build the extended database and to ensure that measures within the database were coded with certainty. These steps involved accessing the police department’s record management system, PremierOne, reviewing reports of investigations (301 reports), querying the department-wide email system, attending bi-weekly violent crime meetings, and reading through OneNote documents. The following illustrates the systematic process developed in the expansion of the log.

Each incident number within the extended database was first queried within PremierOne to access the case folder devoted to the incident. The initial report uploaded within the system allowed the research partner to obtain information on the time of occurrence, the amount of manpower assigned to the case, and the overall description of the incident (i.e., what transpired, number of victims, suspects, and witnesses). The initial report also provided some detail on the traditional investigative methods utilized (e.g., whether CSI was requested). This information was often submitted into the system by dispatchers or patrol officers who first arrived on the scene.

Next, reports of investigation (or 301 reports) were accessed. These reports were often found attached to the PremierOne case files and provided a more detailed account of the incident through subsequent investigation. These reports were typically submitted by detectives and approved by their superiors. In the instance that a 301 report was not attached to the case file, a secondary search was conducted within the department-wide email system. Email queries also produced additional information that was not always readily available within the RMS system. These queries verified items such as: whether information flyers were produced on the subject, victim, or vehicles involved, whether felony apprehension team packets were submitted, whether arrest forms were uploaded, and whether the National Integrated Ballistic Information Network (NIBIN) was utilized. Finally, the email queries also drew out RTCC personnel end-of-shift reports which documented their involvement in note-worthy incidents.

Additional steps were taken for classified cases such as homicides. To capture all the resources employed within the investigation of these incidents, the research partner attended bi- weekly violent crime meetings and read through the subsequent OneNote documents that were assembled as part of the information-sharing process developed within the agency. These documents captured information regarding new investigative leads, any additional witnesses or victims that may have surfaced, the use of cellphone and search warrants, and any additional intelligence information or activities that may have developed over the course of the investigation.

# Time Period of Treatment Group

The data collection for the extended RTCC analytical database occurred simultaneously with the internal RTCC incident log. Data collection was concluded on April 30th, 2022, to allow for the assembly of the non-RTCC treated control sample. The violent crime incidents recorded within the extended RTCC database are comprehensive. That is, all homicides, aggravated assaults, robberies, sexual assaults, and domestic violence incidents that were included and validated within the internal RTCC log, were also captured within the extended database. Selected property crimes (e.g., burglaries, larceny thefts, vandalism) were likewise included within the extended database The extended analytical database consisted of a total number of 762 incidents, of which 648 were violent crimes. The time period of cases within the expanded RTCC database was naturally the same as that in the RTCC incident log, from January 16th, 2019, to April 30th, 2022.

# Measures for Treatment Group

The extended RTCC analytical database included 41 additional measures beyond those contained in the RTCC data log. Of these measures, roughly 80 percent were coded dichotomously. These measures consisted of all unique technology and information sources accessed (e.g., CCTV, LPR, facial recognition), as well as traditional investigative methods utilized (witness statements, information flyers, line-ups). These were dichotomously coded in one of two ways: 1) in a manner that detailed the access and use of the selected technology/information source or method $\\mathrm { \[ 1 = y e s }$ it was utilized, $0 = \\mathtt { n o }$ it was not utilized); or 2) in a manner that depicted is evidence was recovered (e.g., $1 =$ yes there was evidence from the CCTV, or $0 = \\mathtt { n o }$ , CCTV was accessed and reviewed but there was no evidence). Numeric measures detailed the number of officers, victims, witnesses, and suspects involved in the processing of the case, as well as the hours and days it took to clear all applicable cases. The detailed coding arrangement for these measures, and all others, can be found in Appendix A.\
\
# Control Group\
\
A control sample of similar cases not receiving RTCC support was randomly drawn to compare and isolate the impact of RTCC assistance on case outcomes. These non-treated calls for service were those dispatched to officers who responded to incidents through traditional operating procedures and without the assistance of the centralized RTCC unit. The procedure for incident collection and recording within this control sample entailed the following. To ensure equivalency between the RTCC-treated sample and the control sample, a proportionate stratified random sampling procedure was used. First, the frequency of cases in the RTCC sample within neighborhood enforcement areas (NETs) and for each violent crime type was calculated.\
\
These two criteria, crime type and NET area, were used to stratify the groups from within which the control sample would be drawn so that the proportion of cases in the control sample would be the same for these two strata. Thus, the RTCC sample and the control sample had the same number of cases for each NET area and for each crime type. This ensured equivalency on these two dimensions and effectively controlled for the influence of neighborhood effects and crime-type effects on case outcomes. The distribution of frequencies and tests for equivalence between the RTCC and control sample are reported in Appendix B. To generate a sampling frame from which the control sample could be randomly drawn from within each stratum, an automated pull was queried through the RMS system for each unique crime type and specified NET area. Once the sampling frame was generated a three-step process was followed. First, the incidents from the expanded RTCC analytical data log were extracted from the automated pull to ensure that no RTCC-assisted cases were drawn in the process of random selection.\
\
Second, a random integer set generator was utilized to identify the incidents selected for the control log (Randomness and Integrity Services, n.d.). One set was generated with a specified number of unique random integers. The integers were assigned a value between 1 and the total number of incidents generated from the automated pull for the specific crime signal and NET area. The randomness from the selected generator stems from atmospheric noise, which for many purposes is better than pseudo-random number algorithms traditionally used in computer programs (Marangon, Vallone, & Villoresi, 2014). Finally, the number produced by the generator was matched with the number assigned to each incident within the specified automated pull. The matched incident was extracted and placed into the control log. This process was repeated for each signal type and NET area in the log.\
\
# Time Period of Control Sample\
\
Data collection for the control analytical sample began in May 2022 and concluded in July 2022. To support the proposed analytical design, the control log consisted of 762 non-RTCC treated cases. These incidents were equivalent to the RTCCtreated group as it concerns the crime type/signal and associated NET areas (see\
\
Appendix B). 12 The sampling period from which the control cases were randomly drawn was the same as that of the RTCC log and the RTCC expanded analytical sample. That is, January 16th, 2019, to April 30th, 2022.\
\
# Measures for Control Group\
\
The exact same measures included within the RTCC analytical sample were replicated within the control group to meet the conditions of the experimental design (see Appendix A).\
\
# Findings\
\
Table 3 presents a comparative overview of the measures employed in the analyses to evaluate the influence of RTCC technologies on the case processing and outcomes of homicide and aggravated assault incidents, as compared to other forms of violent crimes. Here the other forms of violent crime included within the model consist of misdemeanor assaults $\\scriptstyle ( \ n = 7 4$ ) robberies $\\scriptstyle ( \\mathtt { n } = 4 6 6$ ), domestic violence $\\scriptstyle ( \\mathrm { n } = 9 0 )$ ), and sexual assaults incidents $\\mathrm { ( n = 5 6 ) }$ ). For all continuous measures in the table, independent samples t-tests were used, equal variances not assumed, to ascertain differences between groups. In contrast, for dichotomous measures, Fisher's Exact Test was implemented. This test is used when the sample size is small, and the assumptions of the chi-squared test are not met. The complete model of violent crimes $\\mathrm { ( N = } 1 2 9 6 )$ ) will be presented first, followed by separate analyses for homicides $\\mathrm { ( N = } 1 2 3$ ) and aggravated assaults $( \\mathrm { N } = 4 9 6 )$ .\
\
# Full Violent Crime Model\
\
For the two outcome measures, a significantly greater proportion of cases were cleared within the RTCC-assisted sample $\\mathrm { N } = 2 3 4$ , 36 percent) compared to the control sample, with only 198 cleared cases $( 3 1 % )$ . Alternatively, RTCC-assisted cases had a significantly longer time to clearance, with an average of 43 days to case clearance $\\mathrm { S D } =$ 112.4) compared to the control sample average of just 19 days $\\mathrm { ( S D = 5 4 . 5 ) }$ ). Among the measures used in the analysis to adjust for victim and crime investigation characteristics, there were several significant differences between the two samples. Among victim characteristics, both victim age and whether the victim was black were significantly different, although, in frequency, the differences were small. The RTCC-assisted sample had an average victim age of 36 $\\mathrm { S D } = 1 4 . 4 ) $ , while the control sample was slightly older, with an average victim age of 38 $\\mathrm { S D } = 1 5 . 8 $ ). Among the RTCC sample, roughly half of the victims were black $\\mathbf { \\eta } \_ { \\mathrm { n } } = 3 2 2$ ), while 46 percent of the control sample victims were black $( \\mathtt { n } = 2 9 0 )$ .\
\
Within the measures comprising human evidence, three of the five were significantly different between the two groups. There were statistically significant greater proportions of lineups $\\mathrm { \\Delta } n = 1 4 5$ vs. $\\mathbf { n } = 1 0 9$ ) and flyers $\\mathbf { n } = 2 1 3$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 6 3$ ) used, and the presence of external CCTV evidence $( \\mathrm { n } = 3 1 4 ~ \\mathrm { v s . ~ } \\mathrm { n } = 7 9 )$ within RTCC-assisted cases compared to the control sample. There were no significant differences in the presence of victim and witness interviews. For crime scene-based evidence, five of the six measures were significantly different between the samples. There was significantly more firearm present ( $\\mathrm { \\Delta n } = 3 3 7$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 2 1 8$ ) and vehicles used $\\mathrm { n } = 3 7 7$ vs. ${ \\bf n } = 2 1 0$ ) in the commission of a crime, and accordingly, greater use of NIBIN ballistics tracing ${ \\bf \\langle n = 1 9 3 }$ vs. $\\mathtt { n } = 5 7$ ), processing of gunshot residue $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\mathrm { n } = 8 0$ vs. $\\mathrm { n } = 3 1$ ), and greater use of the CSI unit $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\mathrm { n } = 3 5 0$ vs. $\\mathrm { n } = 2 4 6$ ). Lastly, there were also significantly greater numbers of search warrants $\\mathbf { \\bar { n } = }$ 51 vs. $\\mathtt { n } = 1 5$ ) and cell warrants $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \_ { \\mathrm { n } } = 3 9$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 1 2$ ) used, and more officers utilized (mean $= 4 . 0 6$ vs. mean $= 3 . 8 2$ ) on RTCC-assisted cases compared to the control sample.\
\
# Homicides\
\
As it concerns homicide incidents, for the two outcome measures, $34 %$ of cases were cleared within the RTCC-assisted sample and $2 7 %$ of control sample cases were cleared, with no significant difference between the two. However, RTCC-assisted homicide cases had a significantly longer time to clearance, with an average of 40 days to case clearance $\\mathrm { ( S D = 4 0 . 5 ) }$ compared to the control sample average of just 9 days $\\mathrm { S D } =$ 15.3). Further, only four additional measures proved to be significantly different between the two samples. Three of these measures were present within the human evidence category, including the use of an information flyer $( \\mathtt { n } = 2 4 \ \\mathrm { v s . } \ \\mathtt { n } = 7 )$ and suspect lineup (n $= 1 9$ vs. $\\mathrm { n } = 7$ ), and also the use of external CCTV footage ${ } ^ { ( \\mathrm { n } = 3 3 }$ vs ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 1 4$ ), and one within the category that comprises crimes scene evidence, with that being latent/DNA $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } = }$ 12 vs. ${ \\tt n } = 2 4$ ).\
\
# Aggravated Assaults\
\
The outcomes measures for aggravated assault incidents did not show any notable disparities between the two samples. This suggests that both samples had a similar quantity of incidents cleared by arrest, and that time to clear these incidents did not take significantly longer in one sample compared to the other. All remaining categories produced at least one measure that proved to be significantly different between the samples examined. In terms of victim characteristics, there were more black victims in the RTCC-assisted sample $\\mathbf { \\bar { n } = }$ 132) compared to the control sample $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } } = 9 9$ ).\
\

# Case Processing\
\
Module network analysis helped to determine the influence of RTCC technologies on the case processing of homicide and aggravated assault incidents. The network modules here were constructed with an EBICglasso estimation procedure with a nonparanormal transformation. EBICglasso is a powerful statistical approach for conducting network analysis on high-dimensional datasets and this method excels at accurately estimating the conditional dependence relationships between variables, which is a crucial aspect of network analysis (Foygel & Drton, 2010). A notable advantage of this approach is its ability to efficiently determine the sparsity pattern of the graphical model, allowing for identification of the most relevant connections while discarding insignificant ones. Additionally, EBICglasso is capable of handling scenarios where the number of variables greatly exceeds the number of observations, a common situation in genomics and other fields that rely on high-throughput data (Williams, Rhemtulla, Wysocki, & Rast, 2019).\
\
Here, the analyzed data was drawn from the expanded data set derived from the RTCC log and the stratified randomly drawn control sample as discussed in the previous “Data and Measures” section. Here the entire sample was used $\\mathrm { N } { = } 1 2 9 6$ ).The analysis utilized measures from the data set which coded whether various evidential sources were used in the investigation of the violent crime incident regardless of whether usable evidence was obtained from the evidence sources. This is because the purpose of the analysis here was to determine how various technology or information-based evidence sources were being used by officers and detectives, exclusive of whether they yielded usable evidence for prosecutorial purposes. These evidence sources were grouped into four types and included RTCC technologies (CCTV, LPR, social media, facial recognition, and ShotSpotter), crime scene evidence (NIBIN, GSR, CSI, Latents/DNA), human-based evidence (victim interviews, witness interviews, media flyers, lineups, and external CCTV) and warrant based evidence (search warrants, cell warrants).13An additional crime type variable was intentionally coded to represent investigations of homicides and aggravated assaults, with the aim of examining the role of police technology in investigating these crimes. Here, victim characteristics and other solvability factors were not included as they were outside of the scope of the analysis.\
\
Beyond these distributions presented in Table 4, the findings from the network analysis indicate several important things. First, the network of RTCC-supported cases and the randomly drawn control cases had similar levels of sparsity within the network.\
\

\
Where the RTCC-assisted sample had a sparsity measure of .441 compared to that of .303 of the control sample. This measure quantifies the difference between a fully connected network, where all nodes have a relationship with one another, and the actual number of relationships observed in the analyzed network. A lower sparsity metric suggests greater interconnectedness among nodes, while a higher metric indicates weaker connectivity. In this case, the network is moderately dense, as supported by the number of non-zero edges present in the matrix.\
\
Despite the greater number of evidential nodes present in the RTCC network $( { \\mathfrak { n } } =$ 18), the level of connectedness with other evidential nodes remains about the same as in the control sample $( \\mathtt { n } = 1 3 )$ ). This implies that the integration of RTCC technologies into case processing has been proportionate to other evidential nodes, indicating a successful implementation despite the Miami RTCC being a new system as of 2019. This contradicts previous research suggesting that police agencies are resistant to incorporating innovative technologies into traditional practices and structures (Ioimo & Aronson, 2004; Manning, 2003; Manning, 2008). In this case, technology is seen as beneficial to officers' work rather than a hindrance, leading to expected positive outcomes and outputs (Brunschot, Abela, Witt & Hak, 2022; Lum, Koper, & Willis, 2016).\
\
Second, RTCC technologies appear to occupy a substantial role in the processing of homicide and aggravated assaults to a greater extent than traditional evidential sources. Presented in Figure 3, all RTCC evidence nodes appear to have many connections with other traditional evidence sources. Two of the RTCC evidence sources, however, appear to have greater influence on the investigation of the particular crimes of interest. According to the weight matrix (Table 4), the gunshot detection system, ShotSpotter (influence of .049) and the LPR (.028) appear to have stronger connections to the node representing homicides and aggravated assaults as compared to other traditional nodes, such as the CSI unit (.025) and NIBIN (.015).\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/14742d74e8de3a0a998bb3dba64ec873607b9c3f622702c874bb2f968e562b37.jpg)\
\
Figure 3. RTCC-assisted module network analysis for homicide and aggravated assaults incidents\
\
These findings are perhaps as expected, as more than half of the incidents within the sample either utilized a firearm or had a firearm present during the commission of the crime. Two of the measures within this network, suspect flyers (-.008) and cell warrants (- .010), showcased a negative relationship with the crimes of interest, implying that 1) their connection to the node relied on the presence of other connections, and 2) the presence or absence of these measures would not affect the strength of the other connections.\
\
Within the control sample (Figure 4.) RTCC technologies were removed from the network plot to determine which sources outside the scope of the RTCC maintained strength within the investigative process regardless of the integration of the unit. Here, we see that crime scene evidence sources played the greatest role in the investigation. As seen in the weight matrix (Table 4) the ballistics tracing NIBIN measures had the greatest influence on the investigation of these crimes (.053), followed by the deployment of the CSI unit to the scene of the crime (.034).\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/307d1ed71bb6c8f15c58f89e28ec5b31729b4ae2eebd886ac7dff5a414b6b17f.jpg)\
\
Figure 4. Non-RTCC-assisted module network analysis for homicide and aggravated assaults incidents\
\
Once again, given the high incidence of firearm use, these findings are expected. Moreover, within the MPD standard operating procedures, the CSI unit is tasked with collecting firearm casings when there are ten or more present at the scene of the incident (whereas patrol collects the casings if there is less than ten). These casings then get sent to the property unit before being transferred to the internal gun crime processing unit which houses the ballistics tracing mechanisms. Additionally, three of the measures within this network, suspect flyers (-.015), suspect lineups (-.006) and cell warrants (-.017), exhibited a negative relationship with the crimes of interest. This indicates that these measures' association with the node was reliant on the presence of other connections, and their absence would not weaken the other connections' strength in the network.\
\
# Case Clearance\
\
Table 5 presents the analysis results aimed at examining whether RTCC technologies influenced the case clearance of homicides and aggravated assault incidents. Although Table 3. shows that RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated assault incidents were not cleared more frequently than the control sample, these numbers alone do not consider the differences in the case processing of these crimes. Therefore, to accurately determine the impact of RTCC technologies on clearance rates for these incidents, the multivariate logistic regression analysis controlled for such differences.\
\
Here, missing data was identified in 52 total cases and deleted using the listwise deletion method. Specifically, $3 . 5 %$ $( { \\bf n } = 4 6 )$ of all cases had missing victim gender information and $3 . 9 %$ $( \\mathtt { n } = 5 0 $ ) had missing victim race information. Moreover, two cases or . $2 %$ of the sample had missing data regarding whether the CSI unit was deployed to the scene of a crime. As for victim age, fifty-five incidents were missing data, but this information was transformed and replaced with the series mean. The Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients were statistically significant in all models, with the exception of Model One, indicating good model fit. Within the significant models Nagelkerke pseudo R-square indicated an increasing ability of the models to predict the model variance ranging from a low of . $8 %$ in Model Two, to a high of twenty-three percent in Model Six. All independent measures used in these models were examined for multicollinearity using the variance inflation factor (VIF) with no multicollinearity revealed. All VIF factors ranged from a low of 1.034 to the highest at 2.067.14\
\
Model One only included homicide and aggravated assault incidents. Here, we see that without the presence of any other covariates, the odds of these crimes being cleared, as compared to all other crimes included in the model, were not significant. In Model Two, all violent crimes that received RTCC-support, were included within the model to determine the influence of technology on case clearance. These crimes established significance within the model, indicating approximately $3 6 %$ increased odds of case clearance $( \\le . 0 5 )$ . Here, we see that the crimes of interest improved slightly in likelihood of case clearance but did not reach levels of significance.\
\
Homicide and aggravated assault incidents remained insignificant in Model Three, in which victim characteristics were subsequently introduced. Of all covariates included within the model, RTCC-assisted violent crimes and whether the victim was male also proved to be significantly related to whether a case was cleared. Here, violent crime cases receiving RTCC technology support being $3 8 %$ more likely to be cleared than those without RTCC support and male victims resulted in $34 %$ lower odds of case clearance. Human evidence sources were added to Model Four. Within this model, all violent crime RTCC-assisted cases maintained significance $47 %$ more likely to be cleared), as did whether the victim was a male, which still resulted in roughly $33 %$ lower odds of case clearance. All human evidence sources proved to be of statistical significance, with suspect lineups being the most impactful. In fact, when a lineup was present, the odds of the case being cleared were five times greater than when there was no lineup, while controlling for other factors (£ .001). External CCTV, or private surveillance CCTV did not result in increased odds, but rather the presence of this form of evidence was associated with 58 percent decreased odds in case clearance.\
\
Model Five incorporated evidence from the crime scene, revealing that incidents of homicide and aggravated assaults remained insignificant. Further, cases involving RTCCassistance had a $47 %$ greater likelihood of being cleared $\\left( \\le ~ . 0 5 \\right)$ . Of the victim characteristics, a male victim resulted in $2 6 %$ lower odds of case clearance $( \\le . 0 5 )$ . Among the five human evidence elements, all five remained significant, with suspect lineups having the greatest impact on case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } = 5 . 6 7 9 ; \\leq 0 0 1 ) $ ). External CCTV, once again, depicted lesser odds of case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 4 2 3 ; \\leq . 0 0 1 ) $ . As for crime scene variables, only the presence of a firearm during the commission of the crime was significant, revealing $4 8 %$ lower odds of case clearance.\
\
In Model Six, all variables, including those related to warrant evidence and manpower, were included in the final analysis. Here, the crimes of interest remained insignificant. A male victim sustained statistical significance, revealing an unchanged $2 6 %$ lower odds of case clearance (£ .05). Among all the variables, the support of RTCC technology, witness interviews, lineups, suspect flyers, and search warrants significantly increased the odds of case clearance. RTCC-assisted violent crime cases had $64 %$ better odds of case clearance, and the use of a suspect lineup remained the strongest indicator in the model $( \\mathrm { O R } = 5 . 4 1 6 ; \\leq . 0 0 1 )$ . External CCTV presence $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 4 0 3 ; \\leq . 0 0 1 ) $ and the use of a firearm $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 5 1 1 ; \\leq . 0 0 1 )$ ) significantly decreased the likelihood of the case being cleared, controlling for all other solvability factors.\
\
