# Chapter Eight: Discussion, Limitations and Conclusion\
\
# Introduction\
\
The primary aim of this dissertation was to address a significant gap in the current literature regarding the implementation and use of real-time crime centers. The research concentrated on investigating the disparities in the adoption and implementation of RTCCs on a national scale, their effects on the investigative procedure, including case processing and outcomes within the context of a prominent law enforcement organization in southern Florida, and their impact on public sentiment. This dissertation did not attempt to challenge the findings of the former evaluations that have been published, such as the CPD assessment of the crime reduction impact of district-level SDSC’s, but rather aimed to contribute to contribute to the limited body of knowledge that currently exists.\
\
To accomplish the objectives of this study, a comprehensive approach was employed, involving an extensive review of existing literature to develop a typology capable of capturing the nuances of all RTCCs across the nation. Moreover, a quasiexperimental design and network analysis were implemented to evaluate the impact of these centers on both violent and property crimes within the jurisdiction of the City of Miami. Subsequently, a randomized controlled trial was conducted to assess public attitudes and opinions concerning the utilization and implementation of police technologies. The following sections provide a thorough discussion of the findings and limitations of each analytical chapter, followed by an in-depth examination of the policy implications that emerge from the research. Ultimately, the research concludes by proposing further investigations in this area of study.\
\
# Discussion of Multi-Perspective Theory Application and Typology\
\
Chapter three of the dissertation utilized three different organizational perspectives, namely the institutional, contingency, and rational perspectives, to provide a framework for understanding the process of adopting and implementing real-time crime centers at a national level. The principal objective of the chapter was to ascertain which of the perspectives was most applicable in comprehending the factors that motivated agencies to adopt and implement an RTCC and how this decision impacted the procurement of technologies and establishment of best practices.\
\
The chapter drew heavily from the research conducted by Willis, Mastrofski, and Weisburd (2007), who used a comparable approach to investigate the organizational transformations associated with the implementation of COMPSTAT in three different police agencies. The authors maintained that the adoption of specific structures is an expected reaction for organizations that operate in a highly developed institutional environment. They also concluded that institutional theory could provide a better explanation for COMPSTAT's internal contradictions than the rational model alone.\
\
The findings here slightly departed from the approach taken by Willis, Mastrofski, and Weisburd (2007) by proposing that each perspective - institutional, contingency, and rational - has inherent value during various stages of RTCC adoption and implementation. Therefore, a multi-perspective approach was formulated to capture the nuances across different RTCC units and the inherent differences their operational structures.\
\
For instance, it was conceptualized that during the initial stage of adoption, external institutional influences may act as compelling catalysts for a neighboring agency to adopt an RTCC unit. In contrast, during the second stage of the RTCC implementation process, the fundamental assumptions of the contingency perspective aligned better with the structural and operational arrangements of RTCCs that are dependent on factors like funding, stakeholder investments, or community needs. The ultimate stage of the implementation process was deemed ongoing, where the decision-making process concerning the optimal operation of the unit and the procurement of technology can evolve to meet the goals set by the department, as well as adapt to changing societal circumstances over time, aligning with the rational perspective.\
\
Collectively, these perspectives informed the development of the RTCC typology. This resulted in the identification of three primary models - the traditional, community, and virtual models - that exist in the law enforcement domain. The traditional model is characterized by a centralized command and control center responsible for all operations, whereas the community model is decentralized, with multiple smaller centers distributed across a geographic area to cater to specific needs. The virtual model, on the other hand, is fully digital and cloud-based, functioning as a hub for gathering, analyzing, and disseminating critical data and intelligence throughout the established chains of command.\
\
It is important to note that each RTCC model has unique benefits and challenges, and law enforcement agencies should carefully consider their specific needs and circumstances when deciding which model to adopt. For example, while traditional RTCCs may have a more established command structure, they may face difficulties in coordinating with fulfilling the demands of the agency. As indicated in the final MRTCC evaluation report (Guerette et al., 2022), there were some officers that felt that the access to technologies were too restricted and limited to the RTCC unit only. Detectives working within specialized, undercover units, such as the narcotics or gangs’ division, stated that they would benefit more from the technologies if they had their own personal access, and that they often did not want to share classified information with the detectives in the RTCC. This ties into some research that discusses the benefits and drawbacks of centralized investigative units overall (Feeley, 1973; Horvath et al., 2001; Simon, 1947)\
\
While community based RTCCs may possess a more profound comprehension of issues specific to their locale, coupled with the ability to allocate ample time and resources towards predictive analytics, there are still evident limitations that must be acknowledged. For instance, these RTCCs may encounter inefficiencies when attempting to coordinate responses during large-scale incidents or threats, owing to their relatively smaller size and scope of operation. Furthermore, they may lack the centralized resources and sophisticated technological infrastructure of traditional RTCCs, which could inhibit their ability to respond to emergencies in a timely and effective manner. In contrast, virtual RTCCs may provide cutting-edge technological features; nevertheless, their efficacy is contingent upon the existence of a dependable and secure communication infrastructure. Furthermore, the potential for the misapplication of technological resources may exacerbate the chain of command issues. Lastly, monitoring individual accountability and assessing the effectiveness of the technologies within a larger jurisdiction could pose greater challenges in terms of documentation and evaluation.\
\
Nevertheless, an understanding of the inherent benefits and limitations of each realtime crime center model is imperative for law enforcement agencies in making informed decisions regarding the establishment and evaluation of their units. This is vital in order to effectively combat crime in real-time and produce rigorous evaluation reports. Moreover, a deeper understanding of existing RTCC frameworks can prevent an agency from implementing a unit that is incompatible with its long-term vision of organizational success, thus contributing to sustained success in crime prevention efforts.\
\
# Case Processing and Outcomes\
\
This dissertation has highlighted the significance of assessing crimes on an individual basis rather than within a generalized framework. Depending on the distinct attributes associated with each category of crime, specific technological approaches may be more advantageous than others. Therefore, for crimes that involve personal motives or crimes of passion, the availability of solvability factors may inherently vary from those that do not result in harm to a victim. For example, in instances of larceny-theft, the utilization of CCTV coverage or facial recognition may be more beneficial, whereas conventional forms of evidence such as DNA or latent fingerprints may prove more valuable in cases involving homicide or aggravated assault incidents.\
\
Further, upon considering the theoretical frameworks expounded in chapter two, it becomes evident that the findings of this dissertation offer stronger backing for the tenets of the solvability perspective than for those of the devaluation thesis. Although only gender demonstrated statistical significance as a demographic factor, it conflicted with the devaluation thesis by indicating that incidents involving male victims led to poorer case outcomes. Consequently, the findings fail to substantiate the assertion of the devaluation theory, which posits that police discretion in resolving incidents of violent and property crime is influenced by extralegal factors like the victim's demographics and socioeconomic status, resulting in a lower probability of cases being cleared when victims belong to nonwhite, female, or young categories (Litwin, 2004; Riedel, 2008). This was not observed in the current study.\
\
Given the severity of homicides and aggravated assaults and their significant impact on society, these crimes are considered high-profile. As a result, certain factors can affect the clearance rates of these crimes in ways that differ from other types of crimes. Puckett and Lundman's (2003) investigation revealed that law enforcement agencies devote more resources, time, and effort to homicide cases because they are more visible. In addition, Corsianos (2003) found that public outrage and media attention surrounding homicide cases contribute to their increased scrutiny. Consequently, there is increased pressure on law enforcement agencies to solve these cases, resulting in more comprehensive investigations that involve extensive forensic analysis, witness interviews, and other demanding procedures (Cook et al., 2019).\
\
It is apparent that RTCC technologies have been fully incorporated into the case processing of violent crime types. The network analysis of the module revealed a robust association between the homicide and aggravated assault node and two specific RTCC technologies, ShotSpotter, and LPR system. The frequency of firearms involved in such violent incidents within the dataset may explain the influence of RTCC technologies that are optimized for firearm detection and tracking in these cases. Furthermore, the CSI unit and NIBIN were found to be crucial outside of the influence of RTCC technologies. This discovery is not surprising since the MPD recently established an in-house gun crime center that enables the rapid processing of firearm casings and return of investigative leads to officers.27\
\
As it concerns the case clearance model, we see that the influence of the RTCC technologies on the clearance of these crimes is limited. Rather, the case characteristics within the model prove to be better indicators of case clearance in both the initial output and the supplementary analysis conducted, thus supporting the solvability perspective covered in chapter two. Nonetheless, there are certain findings that warrant acknowledgement. For instance, this study found that consistent with prior research, male victims and the involvement of firearms were linked to decreased likelihood of case clearance.\
\
In terms of gender, there are various explanations that could account for these findings. Firstly, biases and stereotypes may propagate the notion that men are less vulnerable to abuse than women, resulting in cases of male victimization receiving less attention (Scott-Storey et al., 2023). Secondly, law enforcement agencies and the public may be less aware of the extent of violence against men compared to women, leading to fewer resources being allocated to identifying perpetrators (Cook, 2009). Additionally, certain areas of Miami, particularly those with high rates of gun violence, are notorious for being heavily influenced by gangs (predominantly male-dominated), where a culture of silence among members is deeply ingrained, and male victims may receive less public support (Bjerregaard & Smith, 1993; Leverso & O'Neill, 2022). Consequently, incidents of homicide or aggravated assault may receive minimal cooperation from witnesses, with individuals being less willing to provide their accounts of the events.\
\
When it comes to firearms, the findings are not surprising, as it is widely documented that compared to crimes committed with sharp or blunt instruments that require closer proximity to the victim, firearm-related crimes often lack physical evidence such as latent prints or DNA and often lack the component of victim identification (Braga, Turchan, & Barao, 2019; Cook & Nagin, 1979). Additionally, investigating these crimes can be challenging since many firearms may not be registered or obtained legally, making them challenging to trace back to their owner and challenging for law enforcement agencies attempting to link the firearm to the suspect. For instance, in 2019, the Department of Justice (DOJ) conducted a survey that revealed that around 43 percent of criminals acquired their firearms from the black market, 6 percent obtained them via theft, and 10 percent purchased them from a retail source (McKay, 2020).\
\
Moreover, the logistic regression model revealed a significant decrease in the probability of case clearance with the inclusion of external or private closed-circuit television surveillance. In the investigative process, detectives in the RTCC typically engage in obtaining external CCTV footage through their specialized video retrieval specialists, particularly when their internal cameras are ineffective or do not provide adequate coverage. To access these external surveillance feeds, the police agency often enters into private-public partnerships or a memoranda of understanding with local businesses or establishments. Alternatively, if such partnerships do not exist, cooperative witnesses or individuals may submit privately owned surveillance footage. However, cooperation may depend on the prevailing street culture, and uncooperative individuals may require detectives to obtain a warrant.\
\
As per Cook and Berglund (2021), the widespread availability of video and other digital resources has brought about a distinctive set of difficulties for investigators. Although law enforcement officers can readily access video footage from city cameras, it is generally not the case for recordings from private cameras. The process of identifying the sources of potentially valuable footage and obtaining it from proprietors can be timeconsuming and frequently unfruitful. As a result, the temporal dimension and promptness of video surveillance acquisition are frequently cited as barriers to the successful procurement of video evidence (Morgan & Dowling, 2019).\
\
Suspect lineups and public information flyers are perhaps the best predictor of case clearance in the investigation of homicides and aggravated assaults. The utility of suspect lineups as a valuable tool in the investigative process stems from several factors (Davis, Gibson & Solomon, 2014; Higginson, Eggins & Mazerolle, 2017). Primarily, visual identification of suspects through such lineups can greatly aid investigators in the strenuous task of sifting through potential suspects and apprehending the true culprit. Moreover, properly executed lineups can heighten the possibility of witness identification of the culprit, thereby providing significant evidentiary value in building a compelling case against the suspect. The corroborative role of suspect lineups, particularly when used in conjunction with other forms of evidence, such as physical or forensic evidence, can significantly bolster the overall strength of the case. 28\
\
Similarly, providing the general public with information flyers that either offer monetary compensation for the reporting of relevant information to law enforcement authorities or provide details about the identity of the suspect(s) can be an effective means of enhancing case clearance rates. These flyers can offer a promise of anonymity to the informant, which can encourage them to come forward with vital information that could be crucial in solving the case. The dissemination of such flyers can also serve as a deterrent to potential suspects and encourage them to surrender or seek legal assistance before being apprehended by law enforcement agencies. Nonetheless, there is no empirical research to support this claim, beyond this study.\
\
Lastly, in relation to the time required to clear homicide and aggravated assault cases, it is apparent that when not accounting for case-level characteristics, the duration for cases receiving RTCC-support does not significantly differ from cases without support. The same holds true when examining RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated assaults compared to all other RTCC-assisted violent crimes, while controlling for case-level characteristics. Notably, when a firearm is involved, it takes investigators longer to clear a crime due to additional stages in the investigative proceedings. Additionally, the lengthier time to clearance may also be attributed to the increased likelihood of obtaining search or cell phone warrants, which may further prolong the time required to acquire sufficient evidence for a conviction.\
\
# Discussion of Robbery Incidents\
\
Robbery, unlike other forms of violent crime such as homicide or assault, is generally categorized as an instrumental crime. This type of criminal behavior is primarily motivated by achieving a specific and tangible goal, such as acquiring property, rather than being an expression of emotion or impulse (Cohn & Rotton, 2003). Despite its significance in criminology, the literature on robbery has received considerably less attention compared to other forms of violent crime. Additionally, the available literature on robbery outcomes has mostly focused on crime prevention, neglecting the aspect of crime clearance. To bridge this gap, chapter five aimed to investigate not only the established factors that influence case clearance in robbery cases but also the impact of real-time crime center technologies on these outcomes.\
\
As demonstrated by the module network analysis, the ShotSpotter gunshot detection system become a critical factor in the investigation of robbery incidents within the MPD RTCC setting. When examining individual cases, it was found that the use of firearms as a means of intimidation, either before or after the commission of the incident, was prevalent. It is also possible that during the process of data collection, a case initially reported to dispatch as a robbery may have been subsequently reclassified as an aggravated assault over the course of the investigation. Nonetheless, it is crucial to recognize that if the perpetrator did not cause any bodily harm and only stole property, the crime would still be classified as a robbery according to the established procedures of the MPD.\
\
The LPR system was also influential in the case processing of robbery incidents, as it was in homicide and aggravated assault incidents. Interestingly, the utilization of vehicles during the commission of robberies was more prevalent than in the combined instances of homicides and aggravated assaults, although it reduced the chances of case clearance. Willis, Koper, and Lum (2018) suggested that law enforcement officials could enhance their use of the LPR system beyond merely identifying stolen vehicles by exploiting it as a valuable tool for collecting a diverse range of evidence that would bolster witness accounts. Furthermore, detectives in Miami have noticed a surge in robbery incidents reported fraudulently to facilitate insurance claims. Consequently, the LPRs may have been employed more extensively in these cases to mitigate the effect of such fraudulent claims.\
\
Once more, the findings suggest that the RTCC treatment group did not experience a notable improvement in the time to clearance, as previously hypothesized. It could be argued that this result is due to the RTCC's acquisition of additional investigative leads and the implementation of further steps to gather evidence, which may have prolonged the successful resolution of cases. Additionally, compared to homicides and aggravated assaults, robbery incidents tended to require a longer clearance time on average. Beyond the significant variables of the victim's gender and the dissemination of a public information flyer, it is evident that the implementation of a lineup also resulted in longer clearance times for robbery cases. This observation aligns with the nature of a robbery, as victims typically have more direct contact with their perpetrator during the crime, resulting in the theft of items in their presence.\
\
Finally, the supplementary analysis, which only accounts for only the robbery treatment control sample, puts forth a number of propositions. Firstly, it indicates that RTCC technologies have a disproportionately positive effect on incidents of robbery in comparison to cases of aggravated assault and homicide. Given, a recent assessment of sexual assault incidents and homicides which a markedly negligible impact of Miami RTCC technologies (Przeszlowski, Guerette, & Sudderth, 2023), this may suggest that the presence of robberies may have significantly influenced the broader outcomes of the Miami RTCC evaluation which was concluded in the preceding year (Guerette, et al., 2022).\
\
Here, the supplemental analysis also revealed that the clearance of robbery incidents was more likely to occur through the use of public information flyers or lineups rather than through victim or witness interviews. Although this finding deviates from previous research, it is worth noting that prior studies have often failed to consider additional variables that could impact these results due to over reliance on NIBRS data (Roberts & Lyons, 2009; Overstreet, McNeeley, & Lapsey, 2021).\
\
# Discussion of Property Crime Incidents\
\
The findings of property incident study are multi-fold. Notably, it was discovered that police personnel and investigators are significantly less likely to clear property crimes compared to violent crimes, even with the influence of RTCC technologies. This result is not unexpected, as the clearance of crimes with a discernible victim is often prioritized over property crimes in law enforcement practices (Braga and Dusseault, 2018; Cook et al., 2019). Moreover, while the current study did not specifically examine the workload of investigators handling burglary and larceny theft incidents, it is assumed, based on precedent research, that these investigators are often overburdened compared to those assigned to homicide or assault cases (Roberts & Roberts, 2016; Worrall, et al., 2004).\
\
Nonetheless, the highly connected network analysis model demonstrated that RTCC technologies have become a nearly standard practice in property crime investigations, as compared to all other forms of violent crime. However, this raises the question as to why the impact of technology is not more pronounced in the case clearance rates of property crime incidents. Some plausible explanations are the following. Particularly in scenarios of burglary or home invasion, investigators frequently depend on the surveillance recordings obtained from private security cameras mounted outside residential dwellings or commercial establishments. Victims of such crimes may be more inclined to assist law enforcement personnel and provide such footage, especially in comparison to incidents that are of a more personal nature. However, as noted by Brookman and Jones (2021), despite being the most frequently used forensic tool in identifying offenders of these types of crimes, the utilization of CCTV in investigations presents various challenges, such as inadequate video quality for suspect identification. Thus, any footage received from external or private CCTV surveillance may not be useful, which leads to the subsequent point of consideration.\
\
At the time of data collection, the command staff responsible for overseeing the property crime detectives at MPD mandated that all CCTV images be forwarded to the RTCC, irrespective of their resolution or potential usage facial recognition software. Accordingly, The RTCC detectives, as per their established procedural norms, were dutybound to subject these images to the software, even if their quality fell short of optimal standards. Consequently, the RTCC team occasionally found itself flooded with image requests from the property unit detectives. So, while this approach could potentially facilitate the identification of more leads and contribute towards the investigation of property crimes, its efficacy in producing a significant impact thus remains uncertain.\
\
Furthermore, in regard to the timeframe required for clearing property crimes, it is apparent that no substantial distinction exists in the duration between the control and treatment cohorts, nor when contrasted with other violent crimes handled by the RTCC. This observation has consistently emerged throughout the dissertation's findings, leading to the proposition that the use of multiple sources of evidence in an investigation neither abbreviates nor elongates the time required for case resolution. In this context, it is apparent that incidents with public information flyers, which frequently necessitate the use of evidence acquired from RTCC CCTV or external CCTV, are associated with longer clearance times. Although it may seem logical that access to tools that facilitate the identification of suspects would expedite the resolution of crimes, the retrieval of video footage and the subsequent need for additional assistance from the RTCC can sometimes be impeded by “red tape” or bureaucratic procedures (Cook and Berglund, 2021). This tendency is particularly noteworthy for property crimes, which may receive lower priority in the triage of crime reports and requests at the RTCC unit.\
\
# Discussion of Public Perception Study\
\
The diminishing confidence in the police, as revealed by the Gallup survey, highlights a significant shift in public perception and underscores the evolving dynamics between law enforcement agencies and the communities they serve (refer to chapter seven). This decline may indicate a growing concern or dissatisfaction among Americans with regards to certain aspects of policing, potentially stemming from high-profile incidents, inappropriate use of technology, or systemic issues that have garnered widespread attention in recent times. As a result, it becomes crucial for policymakers, law enforcement authorities, and society at large to address these concerns and work towards rebuilding trust and ensuring effective and accountable policing practices.\
\
Taken together, the findings of chapter seven present a compelling picture. First, it seems that the stimuli utilized in the study had a discernible impact on perceptions of police technology use and deployment. In comparison to the positive treatment group, the treatment group exposed to negative stimuli exhibited significantly lower scores on their perception of police transparency and effectiveness. This suggests that when individuals are presented with less favorable information regarding technology use and deployment, such as excessive officer discretion, they may perceive agencies as failing to meet their personal standards for acceptable behavior.\
\
To delve deeper into the impact of the stimuli, additional analyses were conducted. These analyses sought to examine not only the differences in pre- and post-test scores of public approval between all treatment groups and the control group, but also to evaluate the mean differences in these scores compared to the control group that did not receive any stimuli. Surprisingly, the findings revealed that individuals who received only a neutral description of police technologies scored significantly higher on the post-test assessing their approval of police technology than the control group. Furthermore, the mean scores were notably higher than those of the groups that were exposed to positive or negative manipulation.\
\
The findings suggest several significant implications for law enforcement. One crucial aspect is the need for agencies to educate the public about the technologies they use in order to increase public acceptance and approval. It is essential to tailor communication to the specific jurisdiction that an agency serves because each agency operates differently, as documented in chapter three. For instance, consider facial recognition and biometric fingerprint data mechanisms. CVPD is more advanced and progressive than MPD in their deployment of these technologies in their encounters with the public. For example, while MPD only uses facial recognition within the RTCC setting, CVPD allows personnel to use the tool in patrol settings via an application installed on a cellphone or tablet (Strain & Garske, 2013). Additionally, while MPD documents and analyzes latent/DNA data through their CSI unit, other agencies, such as the Anaheim Police Department, have portable fingerprint scanners for patrol officers (Peralta, 2016). It is important to note that national news or press releases may convey that these practices are standard throughout all jurisdictions, which is not yet the case.\
\
Furthermore, the findings of the ordinal regression analysis suggest that the critical factors influencing the acceptance or rejection of police technology are heavily influenced by individuals' perceptions of legitimacy and effectiveness. Law enforcement agencies can utilize this valuable insight by carefully considering their technology acquisitions. For instance, Strom's (2017) research demonstrates that agencies often adopt new technological innovations based on the decisions of executive staff and funding availability, rather than a comprehensive policing strategy. Frequently, an agency member witnesses a technology demonstration elsewhere and perceives a pressing need for the same product within their agency, which aligns with the institutional perspective discussed in chapter three.\
\
Consequently, the agency seeks to justify the technology's purchase to meet its perceived needs, even though the purchase itself is essentially a solution in search of a problem to solve. Such thinking is counterproductive and may lead agencies to procure unsuitable technology that fails to meet their actual needs, resulting in unfulfilled objectives in the long run and increased concerns from stakeholders and the public (Scism & Gallegos, 2020). As a result, the agency's effectiveness and legitimacy may suffer, ultimately resulting in reduced public confidence in police practices.\
\
# Limitations\
\
With respect to chapter three, the theories employed in formulating a comprehensive framework for understanding the variations in RTCC models at the national level were not empirically tested, but rather explored to determine their intrinsic value in the context of these units. Moreover, the typology presented herein ought to be considered preliminary but well-informed, given some of the following reasons. First and foremost, the typology was developed utilizing data obtained from a diffusion of innovation study and survey that was disseminated to sixty-one police agencies across the country. The objective was to attain a more profound understanding of their operational and organizational frameworks. Additionally, leveraging my expertise as a criminologist and having worked as a research assistant for multiple years with the Miami Police Department, I accumulated substantial knowledge not only from that centralized unit but also from external agencies, including the Chicago Police Department, who collaborated with the Miami RTCC on several projects and working groups.\
\
Regarding the chapters pertaining to case processing and outcomes, a significant limitation arose regarding the ability to determine the admissibility of evidence obtained by RTCC detectives and external unit investigators. Specifically, the coding of variables was based solely on whether these personnel utilized sources of evidence or technologies, without considering whether such evidence ultimately proved to be valuable in the investigation process. For instance, although the dataset included CCTV footage, the actual evidentiary value of the footage was not taken into account. Additionally, insufficient sample sizes did not allow for the independent consideration of homicide incidents, aggravated assaults, and the crimes assessed under the property crime model.\
\
Moreover, questions may be raised as to the selection of the control group throughout the studies that consisted of a quasi-experimental design. Despite being selected at random, there may be apparent potential selection effects as there are significant differences between the treatment and control groups. For example, within the violent crime models, the treatment group had many more incidents involving firearms and vehicles routed through the RTCC, as compared to the control group which may have, to some extent contributed to the clearances depicted, specifically as it pertained to robbery incidents.\
\
Finally, as it concerns the public perception study, the results of the assessment may have proved different if the survey was administered in person, as compared to through an online survey marketing tool. Despite the online marketing tool taking all the precautionary steps to avoid “professional survey takers,” the true extent to which this is avoidable is not documented (Andrade, 2022). Nonetheless, the stratification utilized and the RCT design, did alleviate some of the concerns traditionally encountered with this type of research.\
\
# Future Research\
\
In order to advance the field, future research must strive to address the aforementioned limitations inherent in the current study. Furthermore, although the current investigation did not examine the nuances of the individual investigative units responsible for case processing, such as those handling assaults versus robberies, it is imperative to conduct further investigation in this area to achieve a more complete understanding of the RTCC's utilization. This is particularly relevant given the notable disparities in case processing observed between property crimes and violent offenses within this dissertation.\
\
There may also be certain police agency educational components that warrant further investigation or insight. Specifically, as it pertains to questions about how the agencies that house real-time crime centers or comparable innovative technological advancements share their information with the populace. For example, perhaps, certain agencies have a better educational or community-oriented community embedded within their framework and are viewed more favorable by the public. Empirical assessments of this sort, with emphasis on the community-relation unit within police agencies has not garnered much attention, despite playing a significant role.\
\

APPENDIX C\
\
RTCC Perception Survey \[Consent for Pollfish\]\
\
SQ1.The following survey contains questions regarding police technology. Are you willing to take part in the following survey? Your responses are anonymous.\
\
\[Consent for FIU\]\
\
Please take a few minutes to complete the following survey. The survey consists of 29 questions and should take no more than 15 minutes of your time. The purpose of the survey is to better understand the nature and use of real-time crime center (RTCC) surveillance technologies and their impact on public perceptions in the United States. The survey responses will be used solely for research, and all information obtained herein will be kept completely confidential. Results of all surveys will only be reported in the aggregate, meaning responses will not be attributable to any single individual. Your participation is completely voluntary, and you may choose not to participate at any time throughout the course of the survey.\
\
Q1.What is your gender?\
\
Q2.What best describes your political affiliation?\
\
Q3.Science and technology are making our lives, healthier, easier, and more comfortable.\
\
Q4.Have you been a victim of crime in the past three years?\
\
Q5.Have you ever been arrested for a crime?\
\
Q6.Have you had a personal interaction with a police officer in the past three years?\
\
Q7.If yes, how would you best describe that interaction with the police officer?\
\
Q8.On a scale of 1-10 how common is crime in the neighborhood that you live in?\
\
\[Pre-Test\]\
\
Q9.Overall, on a scale of 1 to 10, indicate your approval of police use and deployment of technology.\
\
Q10.I trust that police agencies deploy police technologies in accordance with datadriven decision-making.\
\
Q11.I support the use of police technology as it is deployed in a manner that best serves the community.\
\
Q12.Police agencies are transparent as it concerns the use and deployment of police technology.\
\
Q13.I consider myself familiar with the technologies used by police agencies.\
\
Q14.Police use of technology is effective in reducing crime and victimization.\
\
Q15.Police use of technology has a positive impact on the efficiency and effectiveness of police practices.\
\
\[Stimuli Here\]\
\
Q16.Police technology is used and deployed in a legitimate manner.\
\
Q17.Police technology is used and deployed in a transparent manner.\
\
Q18.Police technology is used and deployed in an effective manner.\
\
Q19.Police agencies utilize and deploy technology in a manner that serves the best interest of the public.\
\
Q20.I trust police officer decision-making as it pertains to the use and deployment of innovative technology.\
\
Q21.I consider myself informed on the vast array of technology utilized by police officers and law enforcement personnel.\
\
Q22.Police agencies are transparent about their use of innovative technologies.\
\
Q23.The use of innovative technology within police agencies has the potential to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of police practices dramatically.\
\
Q24.Police technology is effective in reducing crime rates and improving public safety.\
\
\[Post-Test\]\
\
Q25.Overall, on a scale of 1 to 10, indicate your approval of police use and deployment of technology.\
\
Q26.I would like more technologies deployed by police in my neighborhood and places where I work.\
\
Q27.I would like less, or no technologies deployed by police in my neighborhood and places where I work.\
\
Q28.I support police use of technologies with proper oversight and procedure in my neighborhood and places where I work.\
\
Q29.I support the absolute and unregulated use of technology by police agencies in my neighborhood and places where I work.\

# APPENDIX D\
\
# Vignettes Deployed as Stimuli Within the Three Respective Treatment Group\
\
Positive Vignette\
\
Police agencies across the country are beginning to implement units known as real-time crime centers. These units are equipped with innovative technologies such as closed-circuit television (CCCTV), facial recognition software, license plate readers, and gunshot detection systems, amongst others. These technologies are deployed to high-crime areas based on analysis of historical crime data. Agencies are careful to not violate citizen privacy rights. Police agencies often publish information on the use of technologies on social media or other outlets and ensure that the technologies are deployed in ways that are open and able to be seen by community members. Real-time crime centers offer assistance to responding officers, provide information to officers and detectives after incidents have occurred and can monitor live community events (such as protests of entertainment locations). The primary purpose of real-time crime centers is to improve public safety and reduce criminal activity. For example, surveillance cameras combined with gunshot detection systems can alert patrol officers immediately after shots are fired, allowing them to respond to incidents faster. In most cases, officers are able to either assist victims and/or arrest the offender who committed the crime more quickly.\
\
Police agencies across the country are beginning to implement units known as real-time crime centers. These units are equipped with innovative technologies such as video cameras in neighborhoods, facial recognition software, license plate readers, and gunshot detection systems, amongst others. These technologies are deployed within high-crime, minority communities and based on police discretion. They are also used to monitor live community events such as protests and entertainment locations. There are no ways to ensure that certain technologies such as facial recognition software and searches of social media platforms are done in a manner that ensures citizen privacy rights. Police agencies often do not publish information, nor do they make known how they use these technologies. For operational security, police have decided to not reveal when, where and why these technologies are being used. There is no evidence that these systems are effective in reducing crime or improving community safety.\
\
Neutral Vignette\
\
Police technology: Police technology refers to the broad range of electronic devices, computer equipment and specialized software used to perform police work. As technology has evolved, so has the reliance on technology by police officers and law enforcement officials to prevent crime and apprehend criminals. Closed-circuit television (CCTV), facial recognition software, gunshot detection systems, and automated license plate readers (ALPRs) are all examples of innovative technologies acquired by police agencies nationally. (1) CCTV (surveillance cameras): Typically used to identify suspects in offenses that are committed within view of the camera. (2) Automated license plate readers (ALPRs): Computer-controlled camera systems attached to either police cars or street polls that capture all license plate numbers that come into view, along with the location, date, and time. (3) Gunshot detection system (ShotSpotter): ShotSpotter consists of audio sensors implemented throughout a targeted area (generally on top of buildings of similar locations) that detect the sound of gunfire and identify the location of an incident. If it is confirmed to be gunfire, the relevant information (including time, place, and a recording of the incident) is sent to local police so that they can quickly go to the scene. (4) Facial recognition software: Allows officers to submit images of people’s faces, taken in the field or lifted from photos or video, and instantaneously compares them to photos in government databases – mugshots, jail booking records, driver’s license.\
\

### MCP Insights

# The Case for Real-Time Crime Centers

Posted on June 11, 2024 by
[Jack Dougherty](https://resources.missioncriticalpartners.com/insights/author/jack-dougherty)

Play

The Case for Real-Time Crime Centers

AI-generated audio

5:33

When a law-enforcement organization is contemplating the implementation of a [real-time crime center (RTCC)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_Time_Crime_Center), it is best to begin with an understanding of what an RTCC is — and isn’t.

RTCCs primarily exist to provide law-enforcement officials and other personnel with enhanced situational awareness to better inform their decision-making when a significant incident has occurred. This could involve determining what response to deploy, sharing intelligence concerning what responders might encounter, and providing tactical instructions that will guide their response to make them more effective and keep them safer. RTCCs have been referred to as “ [tactical dispatch centers](https://www.nena.org/page/TacticalDispatch)” for good reason.

All of this is accomplished through aggregation software that equates to an RTCC’s central nervous system. The software collects all sorts of data feeds and then parses the data so that it is manageable, contextual, and actionable. The data feeds are generated by public and private video systems, gunshot identification/location systems, law-enforcement records-management systems, and more. Sans parsing, the amount of information available to RTCC personnel would be overwhelming and thus unproductive. The parsing can be done manually or automatically, with the latter approach driven by configurations set by the law-enforcement organization.

RTCCs often are confused with intelligence centers, which gather information to give law-enforcement officials a clearer picture of the threat environment in which their department and its personnel are operating, and in some cases to enable them to engage in predictive policing. For example, crime statistics might indicate that a particular area of a city has seen a significant increase in car-jackings, burglaries, and snatch-and-grab thefts, or a particular street corner has emerged as the place for illegal drug transactions.

Another example of intelligence gathering concerns the monitoring of social-media feeds. An early example of how social media can be leveraged occurred in 2012 in conjunction with the Super Bowl played in Indianapolis. An intelligence center was established near the stadium, where platforms such as [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/), [Twitter](https://x.com/), [MySpace](https://myspace.com/), and [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/) were monitored for six weeks leading up to the event. A total of 65 million postings, tweets and blogs were parsed, using 500 keywords and several algorithms that had been developed for this purpose.

The effort uncovered no red flags, which gave public-safety officials a much greater comfort level given that about 1 million people were about to descend on the downtown area where the stadium is located for a variety of fan-related events in the days leading up to the big game.

So, an intelligence center enables law-enforcement officials to prepare for what might happen. In contrast, an RTCC helps officials react more effectively to an incident that is happening — it’s all about detection and apprehension in real time — which is event-driven at its core.

Here’s an example. Let’s say that an armed robbery occurred at a bank and the perpetrators already have fled the scene. There is a lot of situational awareness that can be shared with the responding officers while they are in transit. Here are just a few of the data feeds that an RTCC could leverage in real time to support the response:

- The bank’s security camera system
- GPS trackers hidden in the money stacks taken by the robbers
- Security cameras of nearby businesses, residences, and schools
- Municipal and/or county fixed camera systems
  - For example, the city of Chicago maintains a video-surveillance system, managed by its Office of Emergency Management, that consists of more than 50,000 cameras mounted in fixed locations and on public-transit trains and buses
- Helicopters and unmanned aerial vehicles (i.e., drones)
- Vehicle-mounted cameras
- Cameras worn by offers in foot pursuit
- Facial-recognition systems
- [Automatic license plate reader (ALPR) systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automatic_number-plate_recognition)
- Smartphones
- [Computer-aided dispatch systems](https://www.missioncriticalpartners.com/computer-aided-dispatch-software/) that capture caller details of potential witnesses
- Law-enforcement records-management systems

Any one of these data feeds could be integral to apprehending the suspects, but let’s zoom in on [ALPR systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automatic_number-plate_recognition) with a real-life example. In 2015, a Virginia news reporter and her cameraman were gunned down while doing a live interview. The state police transmitted the license-plate number of the rental car that the suspect was driving. Shortly after, a trooper typed the number into her mobile data terminal and received a hit from the ALPR system — the suspect’s vehicle had passed by just a few minutes earlier.

The trooper immediately began her pursuit, catching up to the suspect about four minutes later. Six minutes after that, the suspect’s car veered into an embankment and troopers found him with a self-inflicted gunshot wound. He died a couple of hours later.

That’s a pretty powerful example of the impact an RTCC can have on law-enforcement response.

A future blog will explore typical challenges that emerge when implementing an RTCC and key considerations that every law-enforcement organization — regardless of size are scope — should contemplate in doing so. So, stay tuned!

_Jack Dougherty is MCP’s manager of public-safety applications. Email him at [JackDougherty@MissionCriticalPartners.com](mailto:JackDougherty@MissionCriticalPartners.com)._

# Does the Rapid Deployment of Information to Police Improve Crime Solvability? A Quasi-Experimental Impact Evaluation of Real-Time Crime Center (RTCC) Technologies on Violent Crime Incident Outcomes Expand - [https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.24274521](https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.24274521) Copy identifier URL to clipboardIdentifier Info Cite [Download(3.31 MB)](https://tandf.figshare.com/ndownloader/files/42609081) ShareEmbed journal contribution posted on2023-10-09, 19:20authored byRob T. Guerette, Kimberly Przeszlowski

Despite advances in police practices, national case clearance rates of violent crimes are at an all-time low. One recent trend in American policing involves the rapid deployment of various technology-derived information sources to police prior to, during, and immediately after their initial response to crime incidents. Housed within centralized local-level police department units, these often called “real-time crime centers” (RTCCs) have capitalized on technological innovations to harness a host of information with the intention of improving the ability of police to manage crime. To date, however, very little is known about their ability to improve police practice or their impact on crime solvability. This study entails the first to assess the impact of RTCC technologies on violent crime incident case clearances and time-to-clearance using a multi-method, quasi-experimental design. Results indicated after controlling for neighborhood, crime type, and case-level characteristics, RTCC-assisted cases had 66% better odds of being cleared compared to a randomly drawn control sample of cases not receiving RTCC support. Further, after controlling for case-level characteristics, no significant differences were found between the clearance times of RTCC-assisted and control sample cases. Implications for police practice are discussed.

## History ## Related Materials 1. 1\. DOI - Is supplement to [Does the Rapid Deployment of Information to Police Improve Crime Solvability? A Quasi-Experimental Impact Evaluation of Real-Time Crime Center (RTCC) Technologies on Violent Crime Incident Outcomes](https://doi.org/10.1080/07418825.2023.2264362) ## [Usage metrics](https://info.figshare.com/user-guide/usage-metrics-and-statistics/) **0** **0** **0** [![Justice Quarterly](https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/876az-branding-figshare/tandf/logo.png)](https://tandf.figshare.com/rjqy) ## Categories - [Cell Biology](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:category:%20%22Cell%20Biology%22) - [Environmental Sciences not elsewhere classified](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:category:%20%22Environmental%20Sciences%20not%20elsewhere%20classified%22) - [Immunology](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:category:%20%22Immunology%22) - [Biological Sciences not elsewhere classified](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:category:%20%22Biological%20Sciences%20not%20elsewhere%20classified%22) - [Science Policy](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:category:%20%22Science%20Policy%22) - [Mental Health](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:category:%20%22Mental%20Health%22) ## Keywords [Information technology](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22Information%20technology%22) [real-time crime centers](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22real-time%20crime%20centers%22) [investigations](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22investigations%22) [CCTV](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22CCTV%22) [police technology](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22police%20technology%22) [decision support centers](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22decision%20support%20centers%22) [crime case clearances](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22crime%20case%20clearances%22) [data systems](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22data%20systems%22) [crime solvability](https://tandf.figshare.com/search?q=:keyword:%20%22crime%20solvability%22) ## Licence [CC BY](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/) ## Exports Select an option RefWorksRefWorks BibTeXBibTeX Ref. managerRef. manager EndnoteEndnote DataCiteDataCite NLMNLM DCDC

[Capturing killers: the construction of CCTV evidence during homicide investigations](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/348935841_Capturing_killers_the_construction_of_CCTV_evidence_during_homicide_investigations)

Article

Full-text available

- Jan 2022

- [![Fiona Brookman](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Fiona Brookman](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Fiona-Brookman)
- [![Helen Jones](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Helen Jones](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Helen-Jones-26)

Drawing upon quantitative and qualitative data gathered during a four-year ethnographic study of 44 British homicide investigations, this paper advances the sparse literature on how closed-circuit television (CCTV) contributes to criminal investigations and the risks associated with its use. Based on insights gleaned from interviews with homicide detectives, analysis of case files and observations of live homicide investigations, we examine how CCTV is used during homicide investigations focusing principally on two key investigative moments – identifying and charging suspects. Our quantitative data indicate that CCTV is used more frequently than any other kind of forensic science or technology to both identify and charge suspects. Nevertheless, our qualitative data reveal numerous challenges associated with how CCTV footage is recovered, viewed, shared, interpreted and packaged for court. We reveal the individual and organisational processes and workarounds that have emerged in a socio-technical landscape that lacks clear standards and principles. We discuss the implications of these findings for practice and policy and their relevance to questions about the socially constructed nature of forensic scientific knowledge (First published online 31 Jan 2021).

[The Value of CCTV Surveillance Cameras as an Investigative Tool: An Empirical Analysis](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/316344070_The_Value_of_CCTV_Surveillance_Cameras_as_an_Investigative_Tool_An_Empirical_Analysis)

Article

Full-text available

- Sep 2017
- Eur J Crim Pol Res

- [Matthew P. J. Ashby](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matthew-P-J-Ashby-2056562692)

There has been extensive research on the value of closed-circuit television (CCTV) for preventing crime, but little on its value as an investigative tool. This study sought to establish how often CCTV provides useful evidence and how this is affected by circumstances, analysing 251,195 crimes recorded by British Transport Police that occurred on the British railway network between 2011 and 2015. CCTV was available to investigators in 45% of cases and judged to be useful in 29% (65% of cases in which it was available). Useful CCTV was associated with significantly increased chances of crimes being solved for all crime types except drugs/weapons possession and fraud. Images were more likely to be available for more-serious crimes, and less likely to be available for cases occurring at unknown times or in certain types of locations. Although this research was limited to offences on railways, it appears that CCTV is a powerful investigative tool for many types of crime. The usefulness of CCTV is limited by several factors, most notably the number of public areas not covered. Several recommendations for increasing the usefulness of CCTV are discussed.

[Detective effort: what contributes to arrests during retrospective criminal investigations?](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/312141128_Detective_effort_what_contributes_to_arrests_during_retrospective_criminal_investigations)

Article

Full-text available

- Jan 2017

- [![Seth Fallik](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Seth Fallik](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Seth-Fallik)

Clearance rates among retrospective criminal investigations have been a cause for concern in the last 20 years. Unfortunately, little is known about how detectives contribute to case outcomes. To explore this topic, 184 detectives during a 60 day period completed time-task logs that were paired with incident report data. Through a unique temporal sampling procedure, 564 index offenses were disaggregated into subsamples based on the case’s suspect status at the time the case was assigned. The curvilinearity of these estimates was tested and recalculated into binary terms when quadratic relationships were observed. Continuous and re-estimated quadratic variables were then evaluated for multivariate suitability and regressed to predict a cases likelihood of arrest. The results of these analyses found that (a) detectives continue to exercise great investigative discretion once cases are assigned; (b) suspect status influences arrests; (c) detective time spent interviewing the suspect was the most consistent predictor of arrests; (d) the influence of individual investigative activities on arrests tended to vary by the suspect status; (e) detective effort matters most among cases without a known suspect; and (f) more time performing investigative activities is not always positively associated with arrests. These findings are discussed as they relate to organisation issues, such as supervision, case management, and resource allocation within the context of criminal investigations. In doing so, this quantification of detective effort contributes to policy and practice debates on detective caseloads and seeks to improve the efficacy of criminal investigations.

This research examines the effectiveness of field mobile computing in law enforcement through the theory of task-technology fit. Field mobile computing has been at the forefront of police technology implementations throughout the United States for the past several years. This study measures how well these systems match tasks the field officers must perform and measures the effectiveness of field mobile computing as implemented in a medium-sized Southwestern police department. The authors found statistical support indicating that inquiries to local, state, and national criminal information databases increased with the implementation of mobile computing. The authors also found that field officers recognize the potential benefits mobile computing affords them and that the amount of time required for completing reports increased with the implementation of field mobile computing. Last, administrators, detectives, and records personnel all experienced statistically significant improvements in the tasks they performed, directly resulting from the implementation of mobile computing.

- [![Mindy S. Bradley-Engen](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Mindy S. Bradley-Engen](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Mindy-Bradley-Engen)

We examine the differences in the sentencing of those who plead guilty and those convicted by jury trial among defendants convicted of serious violent offenses. Drawing from a focal concerns and court communities perspective on court decision making, we develop several hypotheses about jury trial penalties for serious violent offenders, and how such penalties may vary by offense characteristics, defendant characteristics, and court contexts. Our hierarchical models using Pennsylvania sentencing data from 1997 to 2000 reveal that defendants are substantially penalized if they exercise their right to a jury trial and then lose. Furthermore, this jury trial penalty is not evenly assessed, but depends on the seriousness and type of offense, defendant criminal history, and court contextual characteristics such as caseload, court community size, local violent crime rates, and the size of local black populations.

- [Jeffrey W. Spears](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Jeffrey-W-Spears-77903907)

Previous research testing the sexual stratification hypothesis has demonstrated that the defendant's race interacts with the victim's race to produce harsher sentences for blacks who sexually assault whites. Research also has demonstrated that victim characteristics affect outcomes of sexual assault cases. We use data on defendants bound over for trial in Detroit Recorder's Court to build on and extend this research. We examine the effect of the racial makeup of the offender/victim pair on a series of sexual assault case outcomes, and we test for interaction between offender/victim race, the relationship between the victim and the offender, and evidence of risk-taking behavior by the victim. Our results show that sexual assaults involving black men and white women are not always treated more harshly than other types of assaults. We conclude that the sexual stratification hypothesis must be modified to account for the role of factors other than the racial composition of the offender/victim pair.

[Exploring the Use of DNA Evidence in Homicide Investigations](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/247748931_Exploring_the_Use_of_DNA_Evidence_in_Homicide_Investigations)

Article

Full-text available

- Aug 2009
- Police Q

- [David A. Schroeder](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/David-A-Schroeder-2219866610)
- [![Michael D. White](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Michael D. White](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Michael-White-2)

Since the 1960s there has been a consistent gradual decline in national homicide clearance rates and the trend continued throughout the 1990s and into the 21st century, despite the development of DNA evidence. Although the media portrays DNA testing as an investigative “super weapon” for police, there is little empirical research examining its actual use by detectives or its impact on case clearance. This article examines New York Police Department (NYPD) case files for Manhattan homicides between 1996 and 2003 to investigate how often detectives used DNA evidence in the course of their investigations, as well as how its use influenced the likelihood of case clearance. Results suggest that DNA evidence was rarely used by NYPD detectives and that it was not related to case clearance. These findings suggest that NYPD detectives used DNA evidence as a “tool of last resort,” relying on it only when all other investigative means had been exhausted. The authors conclude that the explanation for these findings is complex and that the diffusion framework may be helpful in understanding detectives’ use of DNA evidence in New York and elsewhere.

[The Imprisonment Penalty Paid By Young, Unemployed Black and Hispanic Male Offenders](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/229617724_The_Imprisonment_Penalty_Paid_By_Young_Unemployed_Black_and_Hispanic_Male_Offenders)

Article

Full-text available

- Mar 2006
- CRIMINOLOGY

- [![Cassia Spohn](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Cassia Spohn](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Cassia-Spohn)
- [David Holleran](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/David-Holleran-83010211)

A recent study of sentencing decisions in Pennsylvania (Steffensmeier et al., 1998) identified significant interrelationships among race, gender, age, and sentence severity. The authors of this study found that each of the three offender characteristics had significant direct effects on sentence outcomes and that the characteristics interacted to produce substantially harsher sentences for one category of offenders—young black males. This study responds to Steffensmeier et al.'s (1998:789) call for “further research analyzing how race effects may be mediated by other factors.” We replicate their research approach, examining the intersections of the effects of race, gender, and age on sentence outcomes. We extend their analysis in three ways: We examine sentence outcomes in three large urban jurisdictions; we include Hispanics as well as blacks and test for interactions between ethnicity, age, and gender; and we test for interactions between race/ethnicity, gender, and employment status. Our results are generally—although not entirely—consistent with the results of the Pennsylvania study. Although none of the offender characteristics affects the length of the prison sentence, each has a significant direct effect on the likelihood of incarceration in at least one of the jurisdictions. More importantly, the four offender characteristics interact to produce harsher sentences for certain types of offenders. Young black and Hispanic males face greater odds of incarceration than middle-aged white males, and unemployed black and Hispanic males are substantially more likely to be sentenced to prison than employed white males. Thus, our results suggest that offenders with constellations of characteristics other than “young black male” pay a punishment penalty.

Prior theory and research on sentencing oversimplify the role of race, gender and age in judicial decision making. In this article we present a “focal concerns” theory of judicial decision making to frame hypotheses regarding the effects on sentencing of these social statuses, both singly and in combination. Analyzing statewide sentencing outcomes in Pennsylvania for 1989–1992, we find that, net of controls: (1) young black males are sentenced more harshly than any other group, (2) race is most influential in the sentencing of younger rather than older males, (3) the influence of offender's age on sentencing is greater among males than females, and (4) the main effects of race, gender, and age are more modest compared to the very large differences in sentencing outcomes across certain age-race-gender combinations. These findings demonstrate the importance of considering the joint effects of race, gender, and age on sentencing, and of using interactive rather than additive models.

Despite constant calls for reform, policing in the United States and Britain has changed little over the past thirty years. In Policing Contingencies, Peter K. Manning draws on decades of fieldwork to investigate how law enforcement works on the ground and in the symbolic realm, and why most efforts to reform the way police work have failed so far. Manning begins by developing a model of policing as dramaâa way of communicating various messages to the public in an effort to enforce moral boundaries. Unexpected outcomes, or contingencies, continually rewrite the plot of this drama, requiring officers to adjust accordingly. New information technologies, media scrutiny and representations, and community policing also play important roles, and Manning studies these influences in detail. He concludes that their impacts have been quite limited, because the basic structure of policingâofficer assessments based on encounters during routine patrolsâhas remained unchanged. For policing to really change, Manning argues, its focus will need to shift to prevention. Written with precision and judiciously argued, Policing Contingencies will be of value to scholars of sociology, criminology, information technology, and cultural theory.

[Evidence-Based Policing: Translating Research into PracticeTranslating Research into Practice](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/373346314_Evidence-Based_Policing_Translating_Research_into_PracticeTranslating_Research_into_Practice)

Book

- Aug 2023

- [Cynthia Lum](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Cynthia-Lum-77068829)
- [Christopher S. Koper](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Christopher-S-Koper-28903048)

Today's police agencies are in a period of both crisis and reform as they try to improve their ability to deliver public safety to citizens in ways that are effective, legitimate, and sustainable. Evidence-based policing offers one such solution—an approach which emphasises the value that research can bring to police officers and, by extension, the public they serve. However, evidence-based policing is not just about the process of understanding and evaluating police practices. It is also about translating and using that knowledge in daily police activities. This book examines the scientific evidence for the effectiveness of various police practices and provides tools to help turn research into practice. Part I gives a practitioner's definition of evidence-based policing, a primer on how to judge and interpret research findings, and a review of the Evidence-Based Policing Matrix, a tool for translating research on police crime control interventions. In Part II the chapters review the breadth of knowledge about policing interventions for people, places, communities, and technology, focusing on how to optimize operations based on this information. Tools and ideas that can assist in implementing evidence-based practices into patrol, investigations, supervision, management, crime analysis, and leadership are provided in Part III. Finally, in Part IV the chapters contain conversations with researchers about how they might continue to work with police agencies to advance evidence-based policing.

The challenges accompanying the investigation of homicide have been observed for some time. Although police today can gather evidence in ways not imagined decades ago, the use of forensic science and technology (FST) have created challenges and consequences for modern-day homicide investigations. While ‘digital footprints’ are increasingly expected in court proceedings, the provision of and analysis of FST data falls to the police who face resource shortages and other challenges. We surveyed homicide investigators across Alberta to examine their perceptions of FST and the implications of FST for their work. Participants revealed that data volume, lack of expertise and resource constraints result in frustration with FST and the demands it creates. At the same time, most participants pointed to civilianization as the means through which technology can provide full advantage to homicide (and other) investigations.

The newfound ability to deliver information to police in rapid timeframes has resulted in Real-Time Crime Centers (RTCCs) across the United States. Despite their emergence, little is known collectively about them. This study appraised the nature of RTCCs through a national survey of 44 police agencies. Findings revealed that (1) RTCCs have recently begun to diffuse rapidly but are still in an early innovation/adoption phase, (2) there is no single model of their use, (3) most procure a wide variety of technologies and information sources, and (4) most allow for information to be shared with partners in real-time.

[More and better video evidence for police investigations of shootings: Chicago's area technology centers](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/352997841_More_and_better_video_evidence_for_police_investigations_of_shootings_Chicago's_area_technology_centers)

Article

- Jul 2021
- POLICING

- [![Philip J. Cook](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Philip J. Cook](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Philip-Cook-6)
- [Anthony Berglund](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Anthony-Berglund-2197651103)

Purpose
The purpose of this study is to describe the creation, implementation, activities and rationale for the Area Technology Centers (ATCs), an innovation adopted by the Chicago Police Department’s (CPD’s) Bureau of Detectives (BoD) in 2019 for the purpose of supporting investigations of crimes of serious violence by deploying specialized teams of officers to gather and process video and digital evidence.

Design/methodology/approach
This case study utilizes historical information and descriptive data generated by a record-keeping system adopted by the ATCs.

Findings
The ATCs were developed as a collaboration between the CPD and the University of Chicago Crime Lab (a research center). The start-up was funded by a gift from the Griffin Foundation. Detectives have made extensive use of the services provided by the ATCs from the beginning, with the result that homicide and shooting investigations now have access to more video and digital evidence that has been processed by state-of-the-art equipment. The CPD has assumed budget responsibility for the ATCs, which is an indication of their success. The ATC teams have been assembled by voluntary transfers by sworn officers, together with an embedded analyst from the University of Chicago.

Practical implications
The ATC model could be adopted by other large police departments. The study finds that ATCs can be effectively staffed by redeploying and training existing staff and that their operation does not require a budget increase.

Social implications
By arguably making police investigations of shooting cases more efficient, the ATCs have the potential to increase the clearance rate and thereby prevent future gun violence.

Originality/value
The ATCs are a novel response to the challenges of securing and making good use of video and digital evidence in police investigations.

[Effective police investigative practices: an evidence-assessment of the research](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/352691368_Effective_police_investigative_practices_an_evidence-assessment_of_the_research)

Article

- Jun 2021
- POLICING

- [Heather Prince](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Heather-Prince-2184375065)
- [Cynthia Lum](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Cynthia-Lum-77068829)
- [Christopher S. Koper](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Christopher-S-Koper-28903048)

Purpose
Detective work is a mainstay of modern law enforcement, but its effectiveness has been much less evaluated than patrol work. To explore what is known about effective investigative practices and to identify evidence gaps, the authors assess the current state of empirical research on investigations.

Design/methodology/approach
The authors assess the empirical research about the effectiveness of criminal investigations and detective work in resolving cases and improving clearance rates.

Findings
The authors’ analysis of the literature produced 80 studies that focus on seven categories of investigations research, which include the impact that case and situational factors, demographic and neighborhood dynamics, organizational policies and practices, investigative effort, technology, patrol officers and community members have on case resolution. The authors’ assessment shows that evaluation research examining the effectiveness of various investigative activities is rare. However, the broader empirical literature indicates that a combination of organizational policies, investigative effort and certain technologies can be promising in improving investigative outcomes even in cases deemed less solvable.

Research limitations/implications
From an evidence-based perspective, this review emphasizes the need for greater transparency, evaluation and accountability of investigative activities given the resources and importance afforded to criminal investigations.

Originality/value
This review is currently the most up-to-date review of the state of the research on what is known about effective investigative practices.

[Impact of ShotSpotter Technology on Firearm Homicides and Arrests Among Large Metropolitan Counties: a Longitudinal Analysis, 1999-2016](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/351252211_Impact_of_ShotSpotter_Technology_on_Firearm_Homicides_and_Arrests_Among_Large_Metropolitan_Counties_a_Longitudinal_Analysis_1999-2016)

Article

- Apr 2021
- J URBAN HEALTH

- [![Mitchell Doucette](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Mitchell Doucette](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Mitchell-Doucette-2)
- [Christa Green](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Christa-Green-2203985008)
- [![Jennifer Dineen](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Jennifer Dineen](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jennifer-Dineen)
- [Kerri M Raissian](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Kerri-M-Raissian-2193983067)

Over the past decade, large urban counties have implemented ShotSpotter, a gun fire detection technology, across the USA. It uses acoustic listening devices to identify discharged firearms' locations. We examined the effect of ShotSpotter with a pooled, cross-sectional time-series analysis within the 68 large metropolitan counties in the USA from 1999 to 2016. We identified ShotSpotter implementation years through publicly available media. We used a Poisson distribution to model the impact of ShotSpotter on firearm homicides, murder arrests, and weapons arrests. ShotSpotter did not display protective effects for all outcomes. Counties in states with permit-to-purchase firearm laws saw a 15% reduction in firearm homicide incidence rates; counties in states with right-to-carry laws saw a 21% increase in firearm homicide incidence rates. Results suggest that implementing ShotSpotter technology has no significant impact on firearm-related homicides or arrest outcomes. Policy solutions may represent a more cost-effective measure to reduce urban firearm violence.

(Felony investigation decision model—An analysis of investigation elements of information. Government Printing Office, Washington, DC: U.S, 1977). Despite the passage of time, Eck’s (Solving crimes: The investigation of burglary and robbery. Library of Congress, Washington, DC, 1979) variables analyzed here were also found to be associated with higher clearance rates of burglary in Greater Manchester in 2008/9—however, not as robustly when burglaries with violence are analysed separately. Forensic evidence—footprints and fingerprints, for example—were not found to be associated with higher clearance rates.
