# Time to Case Clearance\
\
The analyses to determine whether RTCC-assisted cases improved the time that it took to clear cases are reported in Figures 5 and 6, and Table 6. The simple frequency of days to case clearance reported in Table 3 suggests that RTCC assistance did not improve the speed at which arrests were made in homicide and aggravated assault incidents. However, this does not account for the various victim characteristics and case processing characteristics that could influence the speed at which cases are cleared which are outside the scope of RTCC influence.\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/4c2e0afefefaec58a90d4f2baf9fc472829691898b56160ac259ca588116b16e.jpg)\
\
Figure 5. Cumulative rate of case clearance RTCC-assisted homicides and aggravated assaults vs. control sample\
\
It may be possible that when accounting for fundamental that exist within different crime incidents, the difference in the time that it takes to clear cases is adjusted. To determine this, first, a Kaplan-Meier survival analysis was performed to identify the trajectories in the time- to-case clearance within the two samples. Here, only the treatment and control groups for aggravated assaults and homicides were included within the analysis. The total number of cleared cases between the two samples was 198, of which 100 were cleared in the RTCC-assisted sample, and 98 in the control. The outcome of that analysis is reported in Figure 5. This analysis does not adjust for case characteristics and only plots the proportion of cases cleared within each sample across the period of days present within the observation period. Here we do not see a clear difference in the trajectory of RTCC-assisted cases and the control sample, with control sample cases being cleared at a similar rate as those which were assisted by RTCC technologies. The Mantel-Cox Log Rank comparison indicates that these trends were not significantly different ( $\\chi 2 = 2 . 7 3 6$ , 1 df, $p = . 0 9 8 )$ .\
\
A Cox Proportional Hazard regression model was utilized to address the dissimilarities in victim and case processing characteristics between two samples. This analysis was initially conducted solely on homicide and aggravated assault cases $\\mathrm { N } = 1 9 8$ ) due to their distinct characteristics. However, the sample size was limited, and the resulting Chi-Square coefficient did not attain statistical significance, indicating a suboptimal model fit. Consequently, all cleared violent crimes were incorporated in the analysis and further stratified by technology support to examine whether the time to clearance varied for RTCCassisted homicides and aggravated assaults in comparison to other forms of RTCC-assisted violent crimes.\
\
The comparison of the trend line of that model is presented in Figure 6, and the measures included in the model and coefficients are reported in Table 6. Each of the victim characteristics, human evidence, crime scene evidence, warrants, and manpower measures were included to consider their impact on the time taken to clear cases. With this adjustment and holding constant the effect of all other measures, no significant difference was observed in the days to case clearance between the RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated cases and all other RTCC-assisted violent crimes $( \\mathrm { B } = . . 0 5 6 , \\mathrm { S E } = . 1 0 4 , p =$ .590, n.s.).\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/28e110d5bc278f1a6d3ab47a0a74a5ab44561a64afa9db1c3ba79446664050a6.jpg)\
\
Figure 6. Cumulative rate of case clearance for RTCC-assisted homicides and aggravated assaults vs. other forms of violent crime adjusted at mean for case characteristics\
\
Among the remaining measures in the analysis, four were significantly related to the time that it took to clear cases. Of the victim characteristics, the only significant predictor was whether the victim was male. When the victim was male the case had significantly greater odds $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 5 3 0 ) $ ) of being cleared before female victims. Among human evidence measures, whether a public information flyer was disseminated was associated with 39 percent $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 6 1 2 ) $ ) lower odds of being cleared before those cases without a flyer. Within the crime scene group, whether a firearm was present during the commission of the crime lowered the odds of being cleared before incidents that did not utilize a firearm, with $2 9 %$ lower odds $( p = \\leq . 0 5 )$ ). Finally, within the warrant and manpower measures, the presence of a cell phone warrant lowered the odds of a case being cleared as compared to incidents that did not require the obtaining of a cell phone warrant $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 6 0 0$ ; $p = \\leq . 0 5$ ). The model Chi-Square coefficient was statistically significant indicating a good model fit ( $\\chi ^ { 2 } = 7 3 . 3 5 7 ,$ , 17 df, $p = < . 0 0 1 )$ ).\
\

# Supplemental Analysis\
\
A supplemental analysis was conducted to further augment the results of the former nested regression model (see Table 7). The research questions posed here was the following: “Do RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated assault crime incidents have greater rates of case clearance compared to homicide and aggravated assault cases that did not receive RTCC support?”\
\
\[H4\] RTCC-assisted homicide and aggravated assault crime incidents will have greater rates of case clearance as compared to the control cases that did not receive RTCC support.\
\
Here, only RTCC-assisted homicides and aggravated assaults were used in the model and compared to those that did not receive RTCC-support. Within this model, missing value analysis resulted in the following missing variables: victim sex 20 incidents $( 3 . 2 % )$ ; victim race 24 incidents $( 3 . 9 % )$ , and CSI 2 incidents $( . 3 % )$ . Data was deleted using the listwise deletion method, resulting in 594 total incidents remaining in the analysis, or 26 removed incidents. As for victim age, twenty-seven incidents were missing data, but this information was transformed and replaced with the series mean.\
\
The Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients suggested models 3, 4 and 5 were statistically significant indicating good model fit, and within those models Nagelkerke pseudo R-square indicated an increasing ability of the models to predict the model variance ranging from a low of $21 %$ in Model Three, to a high of twenty-five percent in Model Five. All independent measures used in these models were examined for multicollinearity using the variance inflation factor (VIF) with no multicollinearity revealed. All VIF factors ranged from a low of 1.083 to the highest at 2.184.\
\
Despite the exclusion of all violent crimes, RTCC-assisted homicides and aggravated assaults did not reach statistical significance in any of the five models, indicating that the use of technologies housed within the investigative unit do not increase the odds of case clearance for these two crime types. Rather, as seen in the final model, whether a victim or witness interview took place, whether an information flyer was deployed, and whether a suspect lineup was conducted, all significantly improved the odds of a case being cleared. Within this analysis, external CCTV and the use of a firearm continued to depict lower odds of case clearance, $64 %$ and $59 %$ respectively.\
\

# Chapter Five: The Influence of RTCC Technologies on the Case Outcomes of Robbery Incidents\
\
# Introduction\
\
Whereas the former chapter assessed homicides and aggravated assaults, the primary objective of this chapter was to examine the influence of police technologies on the outcome measures of cases that involve robbery incidents. In particular, the study sought to evaluate the impact of RTCC technologies on case clearance rates, ascertain their efficacy in facilitating investigative procedures, and investigate whether cases receiving RTCC assistance demonstrates accelerated clearance rates. The research questions guiding this study were therefore three-fold: (1) “What is the impact of RTCC technologies on the clearance of robbery incidents?” (2) “To what extent have RTCC technologies been integrated into the case processing of this particular crime type?” and (3) “Does RTCC support improve the time that it takes to clear these cases?”\
\
The opening section of this chapter presents a review of the relevant literature pertaining to robberies and the role of technological advancements in their investigation. A subsequent section will detail the analytical approach taken and provide a brief overview of data collection process and the measures involved within the analysis. Once all analytical approaches are exhausted, a supplemental analysis will be presented to augment the findings and provide additional insight into the impact of the technologies assessed. Limitations and policy implications for this and all subsequent studies will be located in chapter 8.\
\
# Review of Literature\
\
Although research on the investigation of robbery incidents is comparatively limited when contrasted with other types of violent crimes, there exist two pertinent and distinctive bodies of scholarship that warrant discussion. The first pertains to the factors that impact the solvability of robbery cases, while the second concerns the empirical literature on the use of technology in the investigation of such crimes. In the following sections, a comprehensive review of both is presented.\
\
# Factors Known to Impact Case Clearance\
\
Despite its frequent occurrence and grave nature as a violent interpersonal crime, scholarly inquiry on robbery has been comparatively sparse. National data reveals that the clearance rate for robberies is merely $2 7 %$ , which is significantly lower than the clearance rates for homicides and assaults, standing at $50 %$ and $47 %$ , respectively (Li & Lartey, 2022). Although the average robbery clearance rate in the United States has remained stagnant over the past four decades, there has been variation among law enforcement agencies, with some consistently surpassing the national average in long-term robbery clearance rates, while others falling below it. Scholars have put forth diverse explanations for these disparities (Scott, et al.,2019).\
\
The low clearance rates of robberies have been attributed to the distinctive features of this crime and the variables that impact its solvability. Previous research has emphasized the difficulty in identifying suspects in robbery cases, given that they are often committed by individuals leading a high-risk lifestyle (Cook, 1987; Wright & Decker, 1997). Additionally, scholars have pointed out that insufficient resources, outdated technologies, and limited time and motivation on the part of law enforcement officers to investigate these incidents have all contributed to the low clearance rates (Horvath, Meesig, & Lee, 2001).\
\
The understanding of case clearance in the domain of robberies has remained limited, prompting scholars to adopt a broad approach to comprehensively present legal and extralegal factors known to affect the case clearance of homicides and aggravated assaults. However, the application of the same variables to their models has yielded inconsistent results. While some studies indicate that extralegal variables have no effect on case clearance (Roberts, 2008), others suggest that such variables may have an impact, particularly when the victim is female or white (Snyder, 1999). Additionally, it has been observed that the seriousness of the offense is a critical factor in determining case clearance likelihood (Stolzenberg & D’Alessio, 2004), as well as the existence of a victim-offender relationship (Eitle, Stolzenberg & D’Alessio, 2005).\
\
The impact of forensic evidence on the successful resolution of robbery cases is often recognized as a crucial factor. Nevertheless, scientific evidence is utilized in only a small fraction of violent crime cases and even when it is present, its contribution to case outcomes is typically negligible. Contrarily, non-scientific evidence like eyewitness identification has been found to be a stronger predictor of conviction. Nonetheless, Baskin & Sommers (2010) study revealed that the presence of guns and crime scene evidence, as well as the victim's and witness's statements, increased the likelihood of clearance in robbery cases. The collection of physical evidence at the scene resulted in an arrest in $4 8 %$ of cases compared to only $14 %$ of cases where such evidence was absent. However, the examination of physical evidence before arrest happened in just $1 6 %$ of cases, and about $2 5 %$ of arrests resulted from direct arrest techniques. Moreover, a systematic review by\
\
Higginson, Eggins, and Mazerolle in 2017 showed that the presence of crime scene technicians at robbery crime scenes significantly enhanced the likelihood of case clearance by three times and the probability of an arrest by almost double.\
\
In a more recent study, Scott and colleagues (2018) compared the aforementioned factors and other relevant characteristics to determine the difference in case clearance amongst high and low performing agencies. The findings revealed that high performing agencies prioritize investigating robberies and assign all cases to a detective who acts as a first responder to the crime scene. They also employed centralized organizational structures, required specialized training for detectives, and provided adequate resources, such as support from forensic units, intelligence, crime analysis, and fugitive task forces. Conversely, low performing agencies reported higher caseloads per detective and complaints about resources. It should be noted that robbery units typically experience higher case or workloads as compared to other violent crime units, such as those comprising homicide investigators (LoFaso, 2020).\
\
In the same study, the authors additionally collected data on over 350 robbery incidents across seven of the eight agencies included in the preliminary analyses. Utilizing logistic regression analysis, the researchers sought to identify factors linked to successful robbery clearance, discovering that the early collection of physical evidence and the presence of an investigative supervisor during initial response significantly augmented the likelihood of resolving the crime. Moreover, collecting physical evidence later in the investigation was associated with substantially enhanced case clearance rates. In contrast, investigators following up with the victim decreased the likelihood of clearance, potentially due to their preference for tackling more complex cases.\
\
A discussion on the role of technology in robbery investigations can be quite complex. Intuitively, technology has the potential to greatly benefit the investigation of robberies by providing police with new tools and methods for collecting and analyzing evidence, identifying suspects, and tracking their movements. For example, forensic technologies such as, ballistics testing, and digital forensics can help police connect suspects to crime scenes and build stronger cases against them. Moreover, as a form of street crime, the commission of robbery incidents in external environments and in the purview of CCTV cameras, could further assist investigators in corroborating victim/witness testimonies and help investigators piece together events leading up to the event. Social media analysis and LPR can further help assist in identifying the whereabouts of the perpetrator and property stolen at the scene of the robbery. However, despite these potential benefits, the use of police technology has not necessarily led to an improvement in case clearance rates for robberies.\
\
There are several reasons for this. First, the sheer amount of data that police must sift through in order to identify suspects can be overwhelming, particularly in the age of big data (Gibs Van Brunschot et al., 2022). While technology has made it easier to collect and store data on potential suspects and crime scenes, it has not necessarily made it easier to analyze and interpret this data in a timely and effective manner. Second, technology can sometimes create false leads and waste valuable investigative resources. For example, Hill and Mac (2023) have pointed out that facial recognition technology has been subjected to criticism for its ability to potentially misidentify innocent individuals as suspects, ultimately leading law enforcement agencies to pursue incorrect investigative leads.\
\
Finally, the effectiveness of police technology in solving robberies can be limited by broader systemic issues such as underfunding, understaffing, and lack of training. Even the best technology cannot compensate for these fundamental issues.\
\
Perhaps, the only study that assessed the impact of police technology on the case clearance of robbery incidents in specific, found that license plate readers are a promising tool in law enforcement for investigating this crime type. In their study, involving the largescale deployment of 100 fixed LPRs throughout Charlotte-Mecklenburg Police Department’s jurisdiction, Koper & Lum (2019) found that the technology was associated with an increase in the number of robbery investigations that resulted in an arrest or prosecution. The LPR technology significantly increased the number of investigative leads, which in turn increased the likelihood of a successful outcome for robbery investigations.\
\
The study also found that the effectiveness of LPR technology was influenced by other factors, such as the level of training and experience of the officers using the technology, the quality of the LPR system, and the availability of other resources for investigations. Overall, the study suggests that large-scale deployment of LPR technology can be a valuable tool for law enforcement agencies in investigating robbery cases but caution that it should not be viewed as a ‘cure-all’ for all criminal investigations.\
\
The employment of CCTV surveillance in the investigation and case clearance of violent crime incidents has also attracted considerable attention in recent years. However, it is important to acknowledge that most studies examining the impact of CCTV tend to view it as a tool for deterrence and primarily focus on its potential for reducing crime, which is not the intention of the present study. Of those trying to achieve the same objective, Morgan and Dowling (2018) conducted a study in the New South Wales region of Australia, which revealed that requesting surveillance footage from rail network authorities resulted in an astounding $1 8 %$ increase in the clearance rate of police investigations into robberies. Furthermore, Ashby's (2018) analysis of the British rail network showed a marked difference in the clearance rates of robbery incidents with and without access to relevant CCTV footage. The findings demonstrated that CCTV surveillance significantly increased the clearance rate of robberies by more than two-fold, underscoring the invaluable role of this technology in criminal investigations.\
\
# Impact of Real-Time Crime Centers\
\
Thus far, only two evaluations of RTCCs have been conducted. As it pertains to the crime reduction potential of Chicago SDSC’s on the robbery incidents throughout select districts, the evaluation found clear support. In all evaluated models, a statistically significant decrease in crime was observed. The SDSC systems were identified as "critical enablers" for SDSC operations due to their ability to provide real-time situational awareness of incidents and support a framework for long-term decision-making (Hollywood et al., 2019). The second evaluation of MPD’s RTCC did not provide insight into the impact of technology on robbery but rather, the researchers found support for improved crime clearance rates across all categories of violent crime which also included homicides, assaults, sexual assaults, and domestic violence incidents (Guerette et al., 2022). The same data and quasi-experimental design utilized in the latter evaluation will be replicated here, but with a particular emphasis on the robberies.\
\
# Current Study\
\
Considering that this data is derived from a broader empirical investigation on the influence of real-time crime centers on case processing and outcomes of violent crime incidents (Guerette, et al., 2022), it can be inferred that robbery cases receiving RTCC support are more likely to be cleared, involve higher levels of technological integration in their case processing, and not experience any delays in case processing time. (i.e., time to arrest) as a result of investigative effort exerted and the subsequent actions taken in that process. Nonetheless, in contrast to other forms of violent crime such as homicides, assaults, and sexual assaults, robbery incidents may have lower clearance rates according to national data, even without taking into account any other factors. As a result, the following hypotheses are proposed to address the original research questions.\
\
\[H1\] Compared to all other forms of violent crime, robbery incidents will have a lower likelihood of case clearance, controlling for the support of RTCC technologies.\
\
\[H2\] RTCC-technologies will be firmly integrated in the case processing of robbery incidents.\
\
\[H3\] RTCC-assisted robbery incidents will experience longer time to case clearance rates as a result of increased investigative effort.\
\
# Analytical Approach\
\
This study employed four distinct analytical techniques and two forms of analytical software programs to examine the influence of RTCC technologies on the case clearance and processing of robbery incidents. First, a set of nested multivariate logistic regression models were employed to determine the likelihood of case clearance for robbery compared to all other forms of violent crime incidents and controlling for RTCC support. The multivariate model was adjusted for various solvability factors or case characteristics that could potentially impact the clearance probability of cases outside the scope of the technologies employed within the unit. Second, module network analysis was used to empirically examine the extent to which RTCC technologies influence the investigative process of these unique crime types. Finally, Kaplan Meier survival analysis and Cox regression with hazard model outputs was used to determine differences in the time to case clearance for RTCC-assisted robbery incidents compared to the control sample, holding all other case characteristics constant. To conduct the logistic regression and survival analyses, SPSS was utilized, whereas an open-source online program, JASP was used to perform modular network analysis.\
\
# Data and Measures\
\
The analysis relied on data from the expanded RTCC incident log analytical database which contained additional measures of violent crime incident characteristics and sources of evidence used by responding officers and detectives. For a detailed overview of the data collection process, please refer back to the “Data and Measures” section in chapter four. Here, the violent crime of interest is robbery. Within the following analyses, all but two of measures were coded dichotomously, with 1 indicating it was present within the investigation of the case and 0 indicating its absence. Two measures, victim age and the number of officers responding, were coded as continuous measures. There were two dependent measures in the outcome analysis, whether the case was cleared by arrest (coded as 1 indicating an arrest was made and 0 one was not) and the number of days from the time the incident occurred to the time the arrest was made. Days to arrest was coded as a continuous measure.\
\
# Findings\
\
Table 8 presents a comparative overview of the measures employed in the analyses to evaluate the influence of RTCC technologies on the case processing and outcomes of robbery incidents, as compared to other forms of violent crimes. Here the other forms of violent crime included within the model consist of homicides $( { \\bf n } = 1 2 3$ ), aggravated assaults $( \\mathtt { n } = 4 9 6 )$ ), misdemeanor assaults $( \\mathtt { n } = 7 4 )$ ), domestic violence $( \\mathtt { n } = 9 0 )$ ), and sexual assaults incidents $( { \\tt n } = 5 6 )$ ). For all continuous measures in the table, independent samples t-tests were used, equal variances not assumed, to ascertain differences between groups. In contrast, for dichotomous measures, Fisher's Exact Test was implemented. This test is used when the sample size is small, and the assumptions of the chi-squared test are not met. The full violent crime model $\\mathrm { N } { = } 1 2 9 6$ ) will be presented first, followed by separate analysis for robbery incidents.\
\
# Full Violent Crime Model\
\
For the two outcome measures, a significantly greater proportion of cases were cleared within the RTCC-assisted sample $\\mathrm { N } = 2 3 4$ , 36 percent) compared to the control sample, with only 198 cleared cases $( 3 1 % )$ . Alternatively, RTCC-assisted cases had a significantly longer time to clearance, with an average of 43 days to case clearance $\\mathrm { S D } =$ 112.4) compared to the control sample average of just 19 days $\\mathrm { ( S D = 5 4 . 5 ) }$ ). Among the measures used in the analysis to adjust for victim and crime investigation characteristics, there were several significant differences between the two samples. Among victim characteristics, both victim age and whether the victim was black were significantly different, although, in frequency, the differences were small. The RTCC-assisted sample had an average victim age of 36 $\\mathrm { S D } = 1 4 . 4 ) $ , while the control sample was slightly older, with an average victim age of 38 $\\mathrm { S D } = 1 5 . 8 ) $ ). Among the RTCC sample, roughly half of the victims were black $\\mathbf { \\eta } \_ { \\mathrm { n } } = 3 2 2$ ), while 46 percent of the control sample victims were black $( \\mathtt { n } = 2 9 0 )$ ).\
\
Within the measures comprising human evidence, three of the five were significantly different between the two groups. There were statistically significant greater proportions of lineups $( \\mathrm { n } = 1 4 5 \\mathrm { v s . n } = 1 0 9 )$ and flyers $\\mathbf { n } = 2 1 3$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 6 3$ ) used, and the presence of external CCTV evidence $( \\mathtt { n } = 3 1 4$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 7 9$ ) within RTCC-assisted cases compared to the control sample. There were no significant differences in the presence of victim and witness interviews. For crime scene-based evidence, five of the six measures were significantly different between the samples. There was significantly more firearm present $\\mathbf { \\check { n } } = 3 3 7$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 2 1 8$ ) and vehicles used $\\mathrm { n } = 3 7 7$ vs. ${ \\bf n } = 2 1 0$ ) in the commission of a crime, and accordingly, greater use of NIBIN ballistics tracing ${ \\bf \\langle n = 1 9 3 }$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 5 7 $ ), processing of gunshot residue $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\mathrm { n } = 8 0$ vs. $\\mathrm { n } = 3 1$ ), and greater use of the CSI unit $( \\mathtt { n } = 3 5 0$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 2 4 6$ ). Lastly, there were also significantly greater numbers of search warrants $( { \\mathfrak { n } } =$ 51 vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 1 5$ ) and cell warrants ( $\\mathrm { ~ ~ n ~ } = 3 9$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 1 2$ ) acquired, and more officers utilized (mean $= 4 . 0 6$ vs. mean $= 3 . 8 2$ ) on RTCC-assisted cases compared to the control sample.\
\
# Robberies\
\
Once isolated from the full violent crime model, a significantly greater proportion of robbery cases were cleared within the RTCC-assisted sample $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } = 8 9 }$ , 38 percent) compared to the control sample, with only 64 cleared cases $( 2 8 % )$ . Alternatively, RTCCassisted cases had a significantly longer time to clearance, with an average of 51 days to case clearance $\\mathrm { ( S D = } 1 2 6 . 4 )$ compared to the control sample average of just 22 days $\\mathrm { S D } =$ 49.1).\

# Case Processing\
\
Module network analysis helped to determine the influence of RTCC technologies on the case processing of robbery incidents. As in the previous chapter, the network modules here were constructed with an EBICglasso estimation procedure with a nonparanormal transformation The analyzed data was drawn from the expanded data set derived from the RTCC log and the stratified randomly drawn control sample as discussed in the previous “data” section, with a total sample size of 1296 included within the analysis. The data was coded to represent whether various evidential sources were used in the investigation of the violent crime incident regardless of whether usable evidence was obtained from the evidence sources.\
\
These evidence sources were grouped into four types and included MRTCC technologies (CCTV, LPR, social media, facial recognition, and ShotSpotter), crime scene evidence (NIBIN, GSR, CSI, Latents/DNA), human-based evidence (victim interviews, witness interviews, media flyers, lineups, and external CCTV) and warrant based evidence (search warrants, cell warrants).15 An additional crime type variable was intentionally coded to represent the investigation of robbery incidents, with the aim of examining the role of police technology in the processing of these crimes. Victim characteristics and other investigative solvability factors were not included as they were outside of the scope of the analysis.\
\
The findings from this network indicate a few things. First, the network of RTCCsupported cases and the randomly drawn control cases had similar levels of sparsity within the network, where the RTCC-assisted sample had a sparsity measure of .449 compared to that of .333 in the control sample (see Table 9). Despite the greater number of evidential nodes present in the MRTCC network ${ \\bf \\langle n = 1 7 }$ ), the level of connectedness with other evidential nodes remains about the same as in the control sample $( \\mathtt { n } = 1 2 )$ ).\
\

Notably, these sparsity measures are not an improvement over the homicide and aggravated assault model (.441 and .303, respectively), however they still imply that, overall, the integration of RTCC technologies into case processing has been proportionate to other evidential nodes and again this contradicts previous research that suggest police agencies are resistant to incorporating innovative technologies into traditional practices and structures (Ioimo & Aronson, 2004; Manning, 2003; Manning, 2008). Instead, it shows that robbery units, which tend to have a heavier case load than homicide units (Cook et al., 2019) have also become acquainted with RTCC technologies as a mechanism for improving case processing efficiency.\
\

Figure 7. RTCC-assisted module network analysis for robbery incidents\
\
Further, as evidenced in Figure 7. it is apparent that RTCC technologies fulfill a comparable function to conventional evidential sources in the context of robbery investigations. This observation is corroborated by the fact that each of the RTCC evidence nodes depicted in Figure 7 demonstrates a significant number of associations with other customary sources of evidence throughout the network as a whole. Two of the RTCC evidence sources, however, appear to have greater influence on the investigation of the particular crime of interest, as compared to other violent crime incidents. According to the weight matrix (Table 9), the gunshot detection system, ShotSpotter (influence of .049) and the LPR (.028) appear to have stronger connections to the node representing robbery incidents. Here, the more traditional nodes that work alongside the RTCC technologies and influence the processing of robbery incidents include the following: suspect information flyers (.015), CSI (-.010) and NIBIN (-.034). These findings are perhaps as expected, as approximately $40 %$ of the incidents within the isolated robbery sample either utilized a firearm or had a firearm present during the commission of the crime. Moreover, the increased reliance on suspect information flyers in this model may be due to the tendency of robberies to have more investigative information than other crime types.\
\
Robbery incidents often involve face-to-face interactions between the perpetrator and the victim, which can provide more potential evidence for investigators to work with. For example, eyewitnesses may be able to provide descriptions of the perpetrator, the vehicle they used, or the direction they fled after the crime, all of which could subsequently involve images or sketches to be produced. The negative direction of the nodes does not represent a negative relationship as typically reported and observed within a regression model, but rather it depicts that 1) their connection to the node relied on the presence of other connections, and 2) the presence or absence of these measures would not affect the strength of the other connections.\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/115306c8f240da86ae8140525b0cdbb667117935c9f5ae346d35473302bb3700.jpg)\
\
Figure 8. Non-RTCC-assisted module network analysis for robbery incidents\
\
Within the control sample, RTCC technologies were removed from the network plot to determine which sources outside the scope of the RTCC maintained strength within the investigative process regardless of the integration of the unit (see Figure 8). Here, we see that human and crime scene evidence sources played the greatest role in the investigation. As seen in the weight matrix (Table 9) the dissemination of a public information flyer (.026) had the greatest utility within the investigation of robberies. Three other nodes had a notable connection with the crime of interest: that is, NIBIN (-.059), suspect lineup (-.006), and CSI (-.017). This indicates that these measures' association with the node was reliant on the presence of other connections, and their absence would not weaken the other connections' strength.\
\
# Case Clearance\
\
Table 10 presents the analysis results aimed at examining whether RTCC technologies influenced the case clearance of robbery incidents. Although Table 8. shows that RTCC-assisted robbery incidents were cleared more frequently than the control sample, these numbers alone do not consider the differences in the case processing of these crimes. Therefore, to accurately determine the impact of RTCC technologies on clearance rates for these incidents, the multivariate logistic regression analysis controlled for such differences.\
\
Here, missing data was identified in 52 total cases and deleted using the listwise deletion method. Specifically, $3 . 5 %$ $( { \\bf n } = 4 6 )$ of all cases had missing victim gender information and $3 . 9 %$ $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } } = 5 0$ ) had missing victim race information. Moreover, 2 cases or . $2 %$ of the sample had missing data regarding whether the CSI unit was deployed to the scene of a crime. As for victim age, fifty-five incidents were missing data, but this information was transformed and replaced with the series mean.\
\
The Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients were statistically significant in all models, with the exception of Model One, indicating good model fit. Within the significant models Nagelkerke pseudo R-square indicated an increasing ability of the models to predict the model variance ranging from a low of . $7 %$ in Model Two to a high of twenty-four percent in Model Six. All independent measures used in these models were examined for multicollinearity using the variance inflation factor (VIF) with no multicollinearity revealed. All VIF factors ranged from a low of 1.032 to the highest at 2.062.16\

Model One only included robbery. Here, we see that without the presence of any other covariates, the odds of these crimes being cleared, as compared to all other crimes included in the model, were not significant. In Model Two, all violent crimes that received RTCC-support, were included within the model to determine the influence of technology on case clearance. This variable established significance within the model, indicating approximately $3 6 %$ increased odds of case clearance (£ .05). Here, we see that robbery incidents improve slightly in likelihood of case clearance, but do not reach levels of significance.\
\
Robbery incidents remained insignificant in Model Three, in which victim characteristics were subsequently introduced. Of all covariates included within the model, RTCC-assisted violent crimes and whether the victim was male also proved to be significantly related to whether a case was cleared. Here, violent crime cases receiving RTCC technology support being $3 8 %$ more likely to be cleared than those without RTCC support and male victims resulted in $34 %$ lower odds of case clearance. Human evidence sources were added to Model Four. Within this model, violent crime RTCC-assisted cases maintained significance $47 %$ more likely to be cleared), as did whether the victim was a male, which still resulted in roughly $34 %$ lower odds of case clearance. All human evidence sources proved to be of statistical significance, with suspect lineups being the most impactful. In fact, when a lineup was present, the odds of the case being cleared were five times greater than when there was no lineup, while controlling for other factors $( p = \\leq$ .001). Here, external, or private CCTV footage resulted in $58 %$ decreased odds of case clearance.\
\
Model Five incorporated evidence from the crime scene, revealing that incidents of robbery remained insignificant. Further, cases involving RTCC-assistance had a $47 %$ greater likelihood of being cleared $( p = \\leq . 0 5 )$ . Of the victim characteristics, a male victim resulted in $2 6 %$ lower odds of case clearance $( p = \\leq . 0 5$ ). Among the five human evidence elements, only four remained significant, with suspect lineups having the greatest impact on case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } { = } 5 . 5 5 5$ ; $p \ = \\leq \ . 0 0 1$ ). External CCTV, once again, depicted $56 %$ lesser odds of case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } { = } . 4 4 4 ; p = \\leq . 0 0 1 )$ ). As for crime scene variables, the use of a firearm and vehicle in the commission of the crime and was significant, revealing $4 8 %$ and $2 6 %$ lower odds of case clearance, respectively.\
\
In Model Six, all variables, including those related to warrant evidence and manpower, were included in the final analysis. Here, the crimes of interest remained insignificant. A male victim sustained statistical significance, revealing $2 7 %$ lower odds of case clearance $( p = \\leq . 0 5$ ). Among all the variables, the support of RTCC technology, witness interviews, suspect lineups, public information flyers, and search warrants significantly increased the odds of case clearance. RTCC-assisted violent crime cases had $64 %$ better odds of case clearance, and the use of a suspect lineup remained the strongest indicator in the model $( \\mathrm { O R } = 5 . 4 2 9 $ ; $p = \\leq . 0 0 1$ ). External CCTV presence $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 4 0 4 ; p$ $= \\leq . 0 0 1$ ), the use of a firearm $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 5 0 9 $ ; $p = \\leq . 0 0 1$ ), and the use of a vehicle $\\mathrm { ( O R = }$ . $5 0 9 ; p = \\le . 0 0 1 \\rangle$ ) significantly decreased the likelihood of a case being cleared, controlling for all other solvability factors. Within the warrant and manpower group, both search and cellphone warrants significantly increased the odds of case clearance two-fold $\\mathrm { ( O R ~ = ~ }$ 2.237) and $( \\mathrm { O R } = 2 . 5 3 4 ) $ .\
\
# Time to Case Clearance\
\
The analyses to determine whether RTCC-assisted cases improved the time that it took to clear cases are reported in Figures 9 and 10, and Table 11. The simple frequency of days to case clearance reported in Table 8 suggests that RTCC assistance did not improve the speed at which arrests were made in robbery incidents. However, this does not account for the various victim characteristics and case processing characteristics that could influence the speed at which cases are cleared which are outside the scope of RTCC influence.\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/0b283e752eb121a9d0b0457ba54b290ff4784d41bf924906f3df6e350c428d16.jpg)\
\
Figure 9. Cumulative rate of case clearance RTCC-assisted robberies vs. control sample\
\
It may be possible that when accounting for fundamental differences that exist within different crime cases, the difference in the time that it takes to clear cases is adjusted. To determine this, first, a Kaplan-Meier survival analysis was performed to identify the trajectories in the time- to-case clearance within the two samples. Here, only the treatment and control groups for aggravated assaults and homicides were included within the analysis. The total number of cleared cases between the two samples was 153, of which 89 were cleared in the RTCC-assisted sample, and 63 in the control. The outcome of that analysis is reported in Figure 10. This analysis does not adjust for case characteristics and only plots the proportion of cases cleared within each sample across the period of days present within the observation period. Here we see a clear difference in the trajectory of RTCC-assisted cases and the control sample, with control sample cases being cleared at a significant faster rate than the sample assisted by RTCC technologies. The Mantel-Cox Log Rank comparison further indicates that these trends were significantly different ( c2 $= 6 . 2 8 3 , 1 \\mathrm { d f } , p = . 0 1 2 ) .$\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/bd31b7262cb2847e9d7d4135d57522cc25af82b795cd2bbe434cad91773a3211.jpg)\
\
Figure 10. Cumulative rate of case clearance for RTCC-assisted robberies and control sample adjusted at mean for case characteristics.\
\
To account for differences in victim and case processing characteristics between the two samples a Cox Proportional Hazard regression model was fitted. The trend line comparison of the model is presented in Figure 10 and the measures included in that model and coefficients are reported in Table 11. Each of the victim characteristics, human evidence, crime scene evidence, warrants, and manpower measures were included to account for their influence on the time taken to clear cases. As visible in Figure 10, after controlling for case characteristics the trend lines indicating the occurrence of case clearance over time overlap and largely exhibit the same trajectory as the previous model.\
\
With this adjustment, there are no apparent differences between the RTCC-assisted cases and the control sample in the time-to-case clearance trajectories. This was also evident in the coefficients (see Table 11). Holding constant the influence of all other measures there was no significant difference in the days to case clearance between the RTCC-assisted robbery cases and the control sample $( \\mathrm { B } = \_ { - } 1 7 6 , \\mathrm { S E } = . 1 9 2 , p = . 3 6 0$ , n.s.). The findings here suggest that the implementation of RTCC information technologies in the investigation of robbery cases has not reduced the amount of time that it takes to clear cases, but once case level circumstances are taken into consideration, the use of RTCC technologies does not prolong the time that it takes to clear these cases either.\
\
Among the remaining measures in the analysis, two were significantly related to the time that it took to clear cases. For victim characteristics, the only significant predictor was whether the victim was male. When the victim was male the case had significantly greater odds $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 9 3 1 $ ) of being cleared before female victims. Among human evidence measures, whether a public information flyer was used was associated with 41 percent (OR $= . 5 9 3 )$ lower odds of being cleared before those cases without a flyer, and the same finding held true for the use of a suspect lineup in cases $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 6 3 9 $ ) which resulted in longer clearance rates. The model Chi-Square coefficient was statistically significant indicating a good model fit ( $\\chi ^ { 2 } = 4 4 . 4 4 5$ , 18 df, $p = \\leq . 0 0 1 $ .\
\

\
A supplemental analysis was conducted to further augment the results of the former nested regression model (Table 12). The research question of interest was: “Do RTCCassisted robbery crime incidents have greater rates of case clearance compared to homicide and aggravated assault cases that did not receive RTCC support?” Here, only RTCCassisted robbery incidents were used in the model and compared to those that did not receive RTCC-support. Within this model, missing value analysis resulted in the following missing variables: victim sex 18 incidents $( 3 . 9 % )$ ; victim race 18 incidents $( 3 . 9 % )$ , and CSI 2 incidents $( . 2 % )$ . Data was deleted using the listwise deletion method, resulting in 448 total incidents remaining in the analysis, or 18 removed incidents. As for victim age, 19 incidents were missing data, but this information was transformed and replaced with the series mean. The mean average is presented in Table 8.\

In Model One, RTCC-assisted cases were associated with 76 percent greater odds $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 7 5 9 ) $ ) of being cleared compared to the control cases . In Model Two, while holding victim characteristics constant, those odds improved. RTCC-assisted robbery cases exhibited 77 percent greater odds $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 4 8 2 )$ ) of being cleared compared to control cases while accounting for victim age, gender, and race.\
\
In Model Three the odds of case clearance decreased slightly with RTCC-assisted cases exhibiting 72 percent greater odds $\\mathrm { \\langle O R = 1 } . 7 1 5 $ ) of being cleared compared to control cases while accounting for the presence of the human-based forms of evidence. Here, the dissemination of a public information flyer was also associated with improved likelihood of case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 8 7 3 ) $ ) as was the use of a suspect lineup, which resulted in case clearance to be 3 times more likely. External CCTV was associated with a decreased likelihood of case clearance, with cases that involved the extraction of private CCTV footage resulting in $47 %$ lesser odds of being cleared.\
\
In Model Four crime scene evidence was accounted for and again we see significantly greater odds of RTCC robbery cases being cleared compared control cases with an improvement of 88 percent $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 8 7 8 ) $ . The use of public information flyers (2.073) and suspect lineups $( \\mathrm { O R } = 3 . 9 7 3 $ ) remained significant. Within this model, the presence of external CCTV and a firearm during the commission of a crime was associated with $42 %$ and $47 %$ lower odds of clearance. Finally, in Model Five, after accounting for the influence of warrants and the number of officers used in the case, RTCC-assisted cases continued to exhibit 87 percent greater odds $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 8 6 9 ) $ of being cleared compared to cases that did not receive RTCC support. Additionally, where dissemination of a public information flyers $\\mathrm { \\mathrm { O R } } { = } 2 . 0 1 6$ ) and use of suspect lineup $( \\mathrm { O R } = 4 . 4 9 9 ) \_ { \\mathrm { \\Omega } }$ ) and search warrants $( \\mathrm { O R } = 5 . 9 5 7 ,$ ) resulted in increased odds of case clearance, the presence of external/private CCTV footage and firearm resulted in decreased odds ( $47 %$ and $52 %$ , respectively).\

# Chapter Six: The Influence of RTCC Technologies on the Case Outcomes of Property Crimes\
\
# Introduction\
\
The primary objective of this chapter is to examine the impact of police technologies on outcome measures of cases that involve burglaries and larceny thefts. Specifically, the following study aims to assess whether the use of RTCC technologies influence the case clearance of these cases as compared to violent crimes, what role they play in the investigative processing of these cases, and whether the time to clearance of cases that receive RTCC support are cleared at a faster rate. The research questions guiding this study are therefore three-fold: (1) “What is the influence of RTCC technologies on the clearance of burglary and larceny theft incidents compared to all other violent crimes?” (2) “To what extent have RTCC technologies been integrated into the case processing of this particular crime type?” and (3) “Does RTCC support improve the time that it takes to clear these cases as compared to violent crime RTCC-support cases?”\
\
This chapter will follow a similar outline to the preceding chapter. It will commence by presenting a literature review pertaining to the examination of burglary and larceny theft and the utilization of technology in their investigation. A subsequent section will detail the analytical approach taken and provide an overview of the data collection process and the measures involved within the analysis. A discussion of the findings, limitations and policy implications for this and all subsequent studies will be located in chapter 8.\
\
# Review of Literature\
\
There are two pertinent bodies of research related to an examination of property crime case clearance as it concerns this assessment. The first relates to the factors found to influence the solvability of burglary and larceny incidents, and the second pertains to the role of technology in the investigation of these crimes. Following a review of each, a brief overview of evaluations associated with real-time crime centers will be presented to further orient the study.\
\
# Factors Known to Impact Case Clearance\
\
As per the latest data published by the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), the occurrence of property crimes in the United States outweighs that of violent crimes. The agency's report for the year 2019 reveals an alarming rate of 2,110 incidents of property crimes per 100,000 individuals, while the cases of violent crimes were relatively lower, standing at 379 per 100,000 people (Gramlich, 2020). The most prevalent form of property crime in 2019 was larceny or theft, which constituted a significant portion of all property crimes. Furthermore, the occurrence of burglary and motor vehicle theft was also notably high. These figures illustrate that Americans are more vulnerable to property crimes compared to the incidence of violent offenses. With that being said, it is important to acknowledge that these crimes have varying degrees of severity and can have diverse impacts on the victims, such as financial loss, emotional trauma, and property damage (Lockwood, 2014).\
\
Regarding the clearance of property crimes, they remain remarkably low in comparison to all violent crime types. According to Li and Lartey (2022), the national clearance rates for thefts were slightly higher than those for burglary in 2020, at $1 5 %$ and $14 %$ , respectively. It can be argued that clearance rates for property crimes may be lower than those for violent crimes due to a complex interplay of various factors. For instance, some scholars, including Friedmanl, Grawert, and Cullen (2016), have pointed to caselevel characteristics that may hinder improved clearance rates, such as the availability of forensic evidence and witnesses. Therefore, property crimes tend to be less visible and more challenging to detect and investigate, leading to lower clearance rates. Conversely, violent crimes such as murder or assault may receive more attention and resources from law enforcement agencies, resulting in higher clearance rates due to their overtly violent and easily recognizable nature.\
\
As mentioned earlier, the scarcity of research in the field of property crimes can be traced back to challenges associated with data collection, such as underreporting and inconsistencies in definitions and police practices, which were identified by Avdija and Akgul (2021), Merry and Harsent (2018), and Steffensmeier, Harris, and Painter-Davis (2015). For example, it can be difficult to differentiate between various types of burglaries, such as residential, commercial, non-occupied dwelling, and occupied dwelling burglaries, since they may be classified under the same police signal in a database. Moreover, the distinction between theft and burglary is often a fine line, making it challenging to determine which category an offense falls under. These issues can significantly impact the accuracy of data collection, resulting in limited knowledge and understanding of property crimes.\
\
Society also tends to allocate more resources to studying violent crimes, particularly homicides, as they are often perceived as more severe (Braga and Dusseault, 2018; Cook et al., 2019). This prioritization has resulted in relatively few studies being conducted on property crimes. However, it is important to note that property crimes have a significant economic and social impact, as they can cause financial loss, damage property, and harm individuals' sense of security. Therefore, there is a pressing need for further research in this field to better understand the underlying factors contributing to property crimes and develop effective prevention strategies.\
\
Recently, scholars have worked diligently to bolster this area of research. Notably, Roth (2017) has contributed to this pursuit by conducting a study that aimed to augment the limited literature available on the subject. The research analyzed a sample of cities drawn from the 110 largest cities in the United States. Roth's approach mirrors that of other scholars, such as Eitle, Stolzenberg, and D'Alessio (2007), Jang et al. (2008), and Roberts (2008). The study revealed significant variations in property crime clearance rates across the cities examined, with some cities having higher clearance rates than others. The city's size, population density, and the police department's budget were identified as critical factors influencing clearance rates. Workload also emerged as a particularly influential predictor of burglary and theft clearance rates, in which a higher workload resulted in less cleared incidents. With regard to demographic and social disorganization variables, only the non-white population was found to be significant for all three offenses in the full models. This finding contrasts with that of Jang et al. (2008), who found a positive association between the proportion of black residents and clearance rates for theft and vehicle theft, but not for burglary.\
\
In a later study, Roth (2018) took a similar approach and explored factors that influence property crime clearance across smaller jurisdictions. The study used multilevel modeling and data from the Uniform Crime Reporting Program to investigate predictors of clearance rates for burglary, larceny, and vehicle theft in agencies serving populations of 50,000 individuals or fewer. The results of the study indicated that police resources, community cohesion, and socioeconomic status all play a significant role in property crime clearance rates. Specifically, increased police resources, higher community cohesion, and higher socioeconomic status were associated with higher clearance rates. Surprisingly, the study found that case workload was positively associated with larceny clearance rates. Moreover, the only variable that influenced the clearance of burglaries was whether the suspect was a juvenile, which made it more challenging for investigators to solve the case.\
\
Lockwood’s (2014) investigation into the determinants of crime clearance focused specifically on burglary incidents, and yielded support for a range of perspectives, including non-discretionary, discretionary, and broken windows approaches. The author estimated the effects of predictors at multiple levels of aggregation by analyzing burglary incident data and measures of crime and community context aggregated to Philadelphia block groups. The results demonstrated that non-discretionary factors, such as the day and time of the crime, discretionary factors, such as the race and gender of the complainant, and broken windows enforcement, specifically low-serious crime enforcement, were significantly associated with increased clearance rates. Specifically, the analysis showed that burglaries reported earlier in the week, at night, with white and male complainants, and in areas with higher levels of broken windows police enforcement are more likely to be cleared by arrest.\
\
With respect to gender, Roth and King (2019) conducted an investigation into the determinants of larceny and burglary arrest rates for both males and females, building upon prior research on the relationship between gender and property offending (Steffensmeier & Haynie, 2000; Steffensmeier & Streifel, 1992). Using a macro-level approach and gender-disaggregated predictors, the results indicated that male and female arrest rates were comparably influenced by factors associated with disadvantage, such as unemployment, but these factors had a stronger influence on female arrest rates.\
\
Although underutilized in the case of property crimes, forensic DNA analysis has been acknowledged as a valuable tool in the detection and resolution of burglary cases. The process of residential burglary typically follows a defined sequence of steps, beginning with the formation of a motive, followed by target selection, entry into the premises, a comprehensive search of the property, procurement of desired items, exit from the location, and disposal of any unwanted items (Swanson, Chamelin, and Territo, 2002; Wright and Decker, 1994). At each stage, DNA evidence can potentially be uncovered, such as at the point of entry, the tools or other items left at the scene, and unclaimed but handled property.\
\
According to Roman et al. (2008), the processing of DNA evidence in property crime cases results in over twice the number of identified suspects, twice the number of arrested suspects, and more than double the number of accepted prosecution cases compared to conventional investigations. Paine and Ariel (2013) have also found that footprints, fingerprints, and DNA were more prevalent in resolved than unresolved cases, which aligns with the findings of other researchers (Coupe and Griffiths, 2000; Bond, 2007), but not others (Burrows et al., 2005).\
\
Moreover, it has been unveiled that crimes associated with the prompt apprehension of suspects and the collection of suspect data are more likely to be classified as cleared, particularly concerning non-residential burglary, in contrast to ongoing offenses (Coupe and Kaur, 2005). Furthermore, Pelfrey and Hanna's (2011) research indicates that the likelihood of resolving business burglary cases is positively correlated with the degree of knowledge gathered about a suspect, with the identification of a name being particularly advantageous. Various other factors have also been identified as contributing to the likelihood of solving such offenses, including the occurrence of a witnessed offense (Donnellan, 2011; Paine, 2012), the commission of the offense during daylight hours (Coupe and Girling, 2001; Coupe and Blake, 2006), and the availability of investigative resources (Coupe and Griffiths, 2000).\
\
Impact of Technology on Property Crime Case Clearance\
\
As previously discussed in the earlier chapters, there has been an abundance of scholarly research examining the correlation between closed-circuit television (CCTV) systems and the efficacy of crime reduction or resolution. However, these studies have adopted a comprehensive approach by exploring a broad range of criminal activities, rather than limiting their scope exclusively to property-related offenses. For instance, Gerell's (2021) investigation of CCTV deployment in impoverished neighborhoods during a brief period presents compelling findings. The study revealed that the installation of CCTV had a statistically significant impact on reducing violent crime rates in the short term but failed to reduce property crimes, and there was no substantial impact on crime clearance rates for property crimes. This study emphasizes the relevance of contextual factors, such as camera placement, community participation, and camera quality, in determining the effectiveness of CCTV as a crime prevention tool in impoverished neighborhoods.\
\
Coupe and Kaur's (2005) research on the efficacy of alarms and closed-circuit television in detecting non-residential burglaries demonstrated a significant increase in clearance rates for these types of crimes when they occurred within the field of vision of a CCTV-monitored location. Additionally, Morgan and Dowling's (2019) investigation into the impact of CCTV footage on clearance rates for crimes within the South Wales rail network revealed a noteworthy rise of $7 %$ and $8 %$ in clearance rates for theft, burglary, and property damage, respectively, when investigators obtained CCTV footage. Similarly, Jung and Wheeler's (2023) assessment of CCTV installations and clearance of all reported crimes within the jurisdiction of the Dallas Police Department revealed that the deployment of CCTV resulted in a $2 %$ increase in crime clearance, although only in cases of theft.\
\
The integration of license plate reader technologies with CCTV has also gained significant traction in recent times to bolster crime-fighting efforts, as noted by La Vigne et al. (2011). Nonetheless, only one study, which is extensively detailed in chapter five, has comprehensively evaluated the impact of LPRs on property crime. In this regard, Koper and Lum (2019) investigated the impact of widespread LPR deployment on criminal investigations and determined that it enhanced the clearance rates of robbery cases and vehicular component-related thefts. However, further research is imperative to fully comprehend the utility of LPR in property crime investigations.\
\
# Impact of Real-Time Crime Centers\
\
To date, only one assessment has been conducted on real-time crime centers concerning property crime incidents. This particular evaluation did not examine crime clearance rates but was exclusively focused on identifying crime reduction effects. The evaluation of Chicago's Strategic Decision Support Centers found a statistically significant reduction in burglary incidents across all districts and models evaluated, while significant reduction in larceny thefts was observed only in a few districts and models (Hollywood et al., 2019).\
\
Although the data for this dissertation was collected as part of the MRTCC evaluation, the purpose of the initial valuation was to assess the impact of the investigative unit on the case clearance of violent crime, whereas property crimes did not receive attention (Guerette et al., 2022). Nevertheless, it is important to acknowledge that the technologies implemented within of RTCCs resulted in a statistically improved clearance rate for violent crimes as a whole.\
\
# Current Study\
\
This research represents an attempt to investigate the influence of real-time crime on property crime case processing, clearance, and time to clearance. In the context of this study, property crimes are defined as incidents of burglary and larceny theft. Given the comparatively limited sample size, burglary incidents encompass instances of residential, occupied, and commercial burglaries. Larceny thefts exclude theft of motor vehicles but may incorporate theft of vehicle components. It is noteworthy that property crimes are prevalent offenses in the United States, but they are also notoriously difficult to solve. The formulated hypotheses in this study draw mainly on the two prior national evaluations of RTCCs. They are also informed by my experience as an embedded criminologist within the RTCC for several years, which has generated valuable anecdotal evidence that underpins the assumptions of this investigation.\
\
\[H1\] Compared to all other forms of violent crime, property crime incidents will have a higher probability of case clearance, controlling for the support of RTCC technologies.\
\
\[H2\] RTCC-technologies will be integrated into the case processing of property crimes as compared to other violent crimes.\
\
\[H3\] RTCC-assisted property crime incidents will experience longer time to case clearance rates as a result of increased investigative effort.\
\
# Analytical Approach\
\
This study employed four distinct analytical techniques and two forms of analytical software programs to examine the influence of RTCC technologies on the case clearance of property crimes. First, a set of nested multivariate logistic regression models were employed to determine the likelihood of case clearance for property crimes compared to all violent crime incidents and controlling for RTCC support. The multivariate model was adjusted for various solvability factors or case characteristics that could potentially impact the clearance probability of cases outside the scope of the technologies employed within the RTCC unit. Second, module network analysis was used to empirically examine the extent to which RTCC technologies influence the investigative process of this understudied category of crime.\
\
Kaplan Meier survival analysis was used to determine differences in the time to case clearance for RTCC-assisted property crime cases compared to the control sample and finally, due to the small sample size associated with property crimes, Cox regression with hazard model outputs was used to determine differences in the time to case clearance for RTCC-assisted property crime cases compared all other forms of crime assisted by RTCC technologies. To conduct the logistic regression and survival analyses, SPSS was utilized, whereas an open-source online program, JASP was used to perform modular network analysis.\
\
# Data and Measures\
\
The analysis relied on data from the expanded RTCC incident log analytical database which contained additional measures of violent crime incident characteristics and sources of evidence used by responding officers and detectives. For a detailed overview of the data collection process, please refer back to the “Data and Measures” section in chapter four. Here, property crimes are of interest (burglaries and larcenies). Within the following analyses, all but two of measures were coded dichotomously, with 1 indicating it was present within the investigation of the case and 0 indicating its absence. Two measures, victim age and the number of officers responding, were coded as continuous measures. There were two dependent measures in the outcome analysis, whether the case was cleared by arrest (coded as 1 indicating an arrest was made and 0 one was not) and the number of days from the time the incident occurred to the time the arrest was made. Days to arrest was coded as a continuous measure.\
\
# Findings\
\
Table 13 presents a comparative overview of the measures employed in the analyses to evaluate the influence of RTCC technologies on the case processing and outcomes of property crime incidents, as compared to other forms of violent crimes.17 Here the other forms of violent crime included within the model consist of homicides $\\mathbf { \\Phi } ( \\mathbf { n } = 1 2 3$ ), aggravated assaults $( \\mathtt { n } = 4 3 6 )$ ), misdemeanor assaults $( \\mathtt { n } = 7 4 )$ ), domestic violence $( \\mathtt { n } = 9 0 )$ ), and sexual assaults incidents $( \\mathtt { n } = 5 6 )$ ). For all continuous measures in the table, independent samples t-tests were used, equal variances not assumed, to ascertain differences between groups. In contrast, for dichotomous measures, Fisher's Exact Test was implemented. This test is used when the sample size is small, and the assumptions of the chi-squared test are not met. The complete model of all crimes $\\mathrm { ( N = 1 } 5 3 4 )$ ) will be presented first, followed by separate analyses for robbery incidents.\
\
# Full Crime Model\
\
For the two outcome measures, a significantly greater proportion of cases were cleared within the RTCC-assisted sample $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\mathrm { n } = 2 6 1$ , 34 percent) compared to the control sample, with only 210 cleared cases $( 2 7 % )$ . Alternatively, RTCC-assisted cases had a significantly longer time to clearance, with an average of 45 days to case clearance $\\mathrm { S D } =$ 110.6) compared to the control sample average of just 20 days $\\mathrm { ( S D = 5 8 . 8 ) }$ ). Among the measures used in the analysis to adjust for victim and crime investigation characteristics, there were several significant differences between the two samples. Among victim characteristics, both victim age and whether the victim was male were significantly different, although, in frequency, the differences were small. The RTCC-assisted sample had an average victim age of 36 $\\mathrm { \\mathop { S D } } = 1 3 . 6 ) $ , while the control sample was slightly older, with an average victim age of 38 ( $\\mathrm { S D } = 1 5 . 5 ) $ ). Among the RTCC sample, sixty-two percent were male $( \\mathtt { n } = 4 7 7$ ), while 59 percent of the control sample victims were male $( \\mathtt { n } = 4 5 4 )$ ). This constitutes a change from the violent crime models in the previous chapters, in which whether the victim was black was significantly different, suggesting that many of the property crime victims in this sample were of African American descent.\
\
Within the measures comprising human evidence, four of the five were significantly different between the two groups. There were statistically significant greater proportions of witness interviews $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } } = 2 5 0$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 2 1 0 $ ), flyers $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } } = 2 5 9$ vs. $\\mathbf { n } = 7 2$ ) disseminated, lineups $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\mathrm { \\acute { n } = 1 } 5 7$ vs. $\\mathtt { n } = 1 1 4$ ) conducted , as well as increased presence of external CCTV evidence $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } = 4 0 9 }$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 1 1 5$ ) within RTCC-assisted cases compared to the control sample. There were no significant differences in the presence of victim interviews. For crime scene-based evidence, all six measures were significantly different between the samples. There was significantly more firearm present $\\mathrm { n } = 3 4 7$ vs. ${ \\tt n } = 2 1 8$ ) and vehicles used $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } } = 4 1 3$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 2 1 9$ ) in the commission of a crime, and accordingly, greater use of NIBIN ballistics tracing $\\mathrm { \\Phi } \_ { \\mathrm { n } } = 1 9 5$ vs. $\\mathtt { n } = 5 7$ ), processing of gunshot residue $\\mathrm { ~ ~ \\omega ~ } \_ { \\mathrm { n } } = 8 0$ vs. $\\mathbf { n } = 3 1$ ), and greater use of the CSI unit $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \_ { \\mathrm { n } } = 3 8 1$ vs. $n = 2 8 5$ ) in the RTCCassisted sample. Latent/DNA prints were collected more within the control sample $( { \\mathfrak { n } } =$ 214 vs. $\\mathtt { n } = 1 7 8$ ). Lastly, there were also significantly greater numbers of search warrants $( \\mathrm { n } = 5 2 \\mathrm { v s . } \\mathrm { n } = 1 5 )$ ) and cell warrants $\\left( \\mathrm { n } = 3 9 \\mathrm { v s . } \\mathrm { n } = 1 3 \\right)$ acquired, and more officers utilized $( \\mathrm { M } = 3 . 8 1 $ vs. $\\mathbf { M } = 3 . 5 8$ ) on RTCC-assisted cases compared to the control sample.\
\
# Burglaries\
\
Once isolated from the full violent crime model, a significantly greater proportion of burglary cases were cleared within the RTCC-assisted sample $( \\mathtt { n } = 2 1$ , 24 percent) compared to the control sample, with only 11 cleared cases $( 1 3 % )$ . Alternatively, RTCCassisted cases had a significantly longer time to clearance, with an average of 69 days to case clearance $\\mathrm { ( S D = 1 0 4 . 5 ) }$ compared to the control sample average of just 44 days $\\mathrm { S D } =$ 114.2). Among the measures used in the analysis to adjust for victim and crime solvability characteristics, there were only a handful of significant differences between the two samples. These differences fell within the human evidence and crime scene evidence categories.\

\
# Larceny Thefts\
\
For larceny thefts, there were only two significant differences between the treatment and control groups. Those differences were also apparent within the human and crime scene evidence groups, with a significantly greater proportion of external CCTV presence $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\mathrm { n } = 2 0$ vs. $\\mathfrak { n } = 8$ ), and more vehicles used $\\mathrm { \\acute { n } } = 1 1$ vs. ${ \\mathfrak { n } } = 2$ ) within the RTCCassisted group. There were no differences in outcomes measures, with six larceny thefts being cleared within the RTCC-sample, and one within the control group. Moreover, on average it took the RTCC 28 days to clear larceny theft incidents, whereas in the control sample it only took four. However, this difference was not significant as the sample here was relatively small.\
\
# Case Processing\
\
Module network analysis helped to determine the influence of RTCC technologies on the case processing of property crimes. Network modules here were constructed with an EBICglasso estimation procedure with a nonparanormal transformation18 The analyzed data was drawn from the expanded data set derived from the RTCC log and the stratified randomly drawn control sample as discussed in the previous “Data and Measures” section, with a total sample size of 1534 included within the analysis. The data was coded to represent whether various evidential sources were used in the investigation of the violent crime incident regardless of whether usable evidence was obtained from the evidence sources.\
\
These evidence sources were grouped into four types and included MRTCC technologies (CCTV, LPR, social media, facial recognition, and ShotSpotter), crime scene evidence (NIBIN, GSR, CSI, Latents/DNA), human-based evidence (victim interviews, witness interviews, media flyers, lineups, and external CCTV) and warrant based evidence (search warrants, cell warrants).19 An additional crime type variable was intentionally coded to represent the investigation of property crimes, with the aim of examining the role of police technology in the processing of these crimes. As with all other network analyses performed in this dissertation, victim characteristics and other investigative solvability factors were not included as they were outside of the scope of the analysis.\
\

The findings from this network indicate a few things. First, the network of RTCCsupported cases and the randomly drawn control cases had similar levels of sparsity within the network, where the RTCC-assisted sample had a sparsity measure of .118 compared to that of .121 in the control sample. Despite the greater number of evidential nodes present in the MRTCC network $( \\mathtt { n } = 1 7 \_ { . }$ ), the level of connectedness with other evidential nodes remains about the same as in the control sample $\\mathbf { \\tilde { n } = 1 } 2$ ). Notably, these sparsity measures are much lower than those of the violent crimes assessed in chapter four and chapter five, which is an improvement in the model and indicates a much more connected network.\
\
