Figure 11. RTCC-assisted module network analysis for property crime incidents\
\
In Figure 11, it is apparent that RTCC technologies play a significant role in investigating property crime incidents20. These incidents are more central in the module network than other types of crime that have been examined. Among the RTCC evidence sources, two of them have a greater impact on investigating the particular crime in question in the model. The weight matrix (Table 14) indicates that facial recognition software technology (with an influence of .341) and the license plate reader system (with an influence of .107) are strongly linked to the property crime node. CCTV (.003), social media searches (-.064), and ShotSpotter (-.007) also maintain some form of connection to the property node, albeit weaker.\
\
Here, as perhaps expected, the more traditional nodes that work alongside the RTCC technologies with the highest influence the processing of property crime incidents include external/private CCTV (.452), latent prints/DNA (.342), and victim interviews (.219). Suspect lineups (-.649) also appear to be connected to the property node; however, the negative relationship indicates that it is highly contingent on the presence of other nodes.\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/be511925aec14e4730bbe4792f3caab4901fe10472d69866c431de112bd6dfb3.jpg)\
\
Figure 12. Non-RTCC-assisted module network analysis for property crime incidents\
\
To determine which sources outside the scope of RTCC technologies maintained strength in the investigative process, the network plot within the control sample was created by removing the RTCC technologies. The results showed that human and crime scene evidence sources played a significant role in the investigation. According to the weight matrix (Table 14), external CCTV (.691) has the highest connection to the property node, followed by victim interviews (.418), and collection of latent prints or DNA evidence (.362). The negative direction of most of the remaining nodes does not indicate a negative relationship as typically reported and observed within a regression model. Instead, it shows that 1) their connection to the node relied on the presence of other connections, and 2) the presence or absence of these measures would not affect the strength of the other connections.\
\
# Case Clearance\
\
Table 15 presents the analysis results aimed at examining whether RTCC technologies influenced the case clearance of property incidents. Although Table 13. shows that RTCC-assisted burglary and larceny theft incidents were cleared more frequently than the control sample, these numbers alone do not consider the differences in the case processing of these crimes. Therefore, to accurately determine the influence of RTCC technologies on clearance rates for these incidents, the multivariate logistic regression analysis controlled for such differences.\
\
Here, missing data was identified in 140 total cases and deleted using the listwise deletion method. Specifically, $8 . 1 %$ $( { \\bf n } = 1 2 5 )$ ) of all cases had missing victim gender information and $9 . 0 %$ $\\mathrm { \\Delta } \\ln = 1 3 8 \\mathrm { \\Delta }$ ) had missing victim race information. Moreover, 2 cases or . $1 %$ of the sample had missing data regarding whether the CSI unit was deployed to the scene of a crime. As for victim age, 136 incidents contained missing data, but this information was transformed and replaced with the series mean.\
\
The Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients were statistically significant in all models, indicating good model fit. Within the significant models Nagelkerke pseudo Rsquare indicated an increasing ability of the models to predict the model variance ranging from a low of . $7 %$ in Model Two to a high of twenty-four percent in Model Six. All independent measures used in these models were examined for multicollinearity using the variance inflation factor (VIF) with no multicollinearity revealed. All VIF factors ranged from a low of 1.085 to the highest at 1.979.21\
\
Model One only included property crimes. Here, we see that without the presence of any other covariates, the odds of burglary and larceny thefts being cleared, as compared to all other violent crimes included in the model, were statistically significantly lower. Specifically, property crimes had roughly 66 percent lower odds of case clearance. In Model Two, all crimes that received RTCC-support, were included within the model to determine the influence of technology on case clearance. This variable established significance within the model, indicating approximately $42 %$ increased odds of case clearance $( \\le . 0 1 )$ Here, we see that property crime incidents remain at $6 6 %$ significantly lowers odds of case clearance, thus RTCC-support was not influential in clearing these crimes.\
\

Property crime incidents remained significant in Model Three, in which victim characteristics were subsequently introduced. Of all covariates included within the model, RTCC-assisted violent crimes and whether the victim was male also proved to be significantly related to whether a case was cleared. Here, incidents receiving RTCC technology support were $4 6 %$ more likely to be cleared than those without RTCC support and male victims resulted in $34 %$ lower odds of case clearance. Human evidence sources were added to Model Four. Within this model, incidents that received RTCC support maintained significance ( $50 %$ more likely to be cleared), as did whether the victim was a male, which still resulted in roughly $23 %$ lower odds of case clearance. All human evidence sources proved to be of statistical significance, with suspect lineups being the most impactful. In fact, when a lineup was present, the odds of the case being cleared were five times greater than when there was no lineup, while controlling for other factors $( p = \\leq$ .001). Here private or external CCTV was associated with $5 5 %$ decreased odds of case clearance.\
\
Model Five incorporated evidence from the crime scene, revealing that incidents of property crime remained significant. The odds improved slightly, but property crimes were still $64 %$ less likely to be cleared than all other violent crimes included within the model. Further, cases involving RTCC-assistance had a $6 8 %$ greater likelihood of being cleared $( p = \\leq . 0 0 1 )$ . Of the victim characteristics, a male victim resulted in $2 5 %$ lower odds of case clearance. Among the five human evidence elements, only four remained significant, with suspect lineups having the greatest impact on case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } = 5 . 9 5 8 ; p = \\leq . 0 0 1 )$ . External CCTV, once again, depicted lesser odds of case clearance $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 4 7 5 ; p = \\leq . 0 0 1 )$ .\
\
As for crime scene variables, the use of a firearm in the commission of the crime and was significant, revealing $4 8 %$ lower odds of case clearance.\
\
In Model Six, all variables, including those related to warrant evidence and manpower, were included in the final analysis. Here, the property crimes also remained insignificant, depicting 58 percent lower odds of case clearance than violent crime. A male victim sustained statistical significance, revealing $2 5 %$ lower odds of case clearance $( p =$ $\\leq . 0 5 )$ . Among all the variables, the support of RTCC technology, witness interviews, suspect lineups, public information flyers, and search warrants significantly increased the odds of case clearance. RTCC-assisted incidents had $67 %$ better odds of case clearance, and the use of a suspect lineup remained the strongest indicator in the model $( \\mathrm { O R } = 5 . 7 7 5 $ ; $p = \\leq . 0 0 1 $ ), followed by the dissemination of public information flyers $( \\mathrm { O R } = 2 . 0 4 0 ; p =$ $\\le . 0 0 1 )$ . External CCTV presence $( \\mathrm { O R } { = } . 4 3 0 ; p = \\leq . 0 0 1 ) $ , the use of a firearm $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 5 0 2 $ ; $p = \\leq . 0 0 1$ ), and the use of a vehicle $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 5 0 9 $ ; $p = \\leq . 0 5$ ) significantly decreased the likelihood of a case being cleared, controlling for all other solvability factors and case characteristics. Within the warrant and manpower group, both search and cellphone warrants significantly increased the odds of case clearance two-fold $( \\mathrm { O R } = 2 . 2 3 7 ,$ ) and (OR $= 2 . 5 3 4 )$ .\
\
# Time to Case Clearance\
\
The analyses to determine whether RTCC-assisted cases improved the time that it took to clear property crime incidents are reported in Figures 13 and 14, and Table 16. The simple frequency of days to case clearance reported in Table 13 suggests that RTCC assistance did not improve the speed at which arrests were made in burglary or larceny theft incidents. However, this does not account for the various victim characteristics and case processing characteristics that could influence the speed at which cases are cleared which are outside the scope of RTCC influence.\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/1c10f2940a0de3035d23a6ae7c1fab3993ff7e0c7e80ca11857311ad56066d69.jpg)\
\
Figure 13. Cumulative rate of case clearance RTCC-assisted property crimes\
\
First, a Kaplan-Meier survival analysis was performed to identify the trajectories in the time- to-case clearance within the two samples. Here, only the treatment and control groups for property crime incidents were included within the analysis. The total number of cleared cases between the two samples was 39, of which 27 were cleared in the RTCCassisted sample, and 12 in the control. The outcome of that analysis is reported in Figure 13. This analysis does not adjust for case characteristics and only plots the proportion of cases cleared within each sample across the period of days present within the observation period. Here we do not see a clear difference in the trajectory of RTCC-assisted cases and the control sample, with control sample cases being cleared at a similar compared to the sample assisted by RTCC technologies. The Mantel-Cox Log Rank comparison further indicates that these trends were not significantly different ( $\\chi 2 = . 8 6 1 ,$ 1 df, $p = . 3 5 3 )$ .\
\
![](https://digitalcommons.fiu.edu/nanna/record/14169/files/images/52b22b422e0cd67ea1b2afb2db33a797b8d9f4166ea4e40097a495e1653b69d5.jpg)\
\
Figure 14. Cumulative rate of case clearance for RTCC-assisted property crimes vs. other forms of violent crime adjusted at mean for case characteristics\
\
To account for differences in victim and case processing characteristics between the two samples a Cox Proportional Hazard regression model was fitted. Initially, this analysis was conducted solely on property crime incidents due to their distinct characteristics. However, the sample size was too small, and the resulting Chi-Square coefficient did not attain statistical significance, indicating a suboptimal model fit. As a result, all cleared crimes were incorporated in the analysis and further stratified by technology support to examine whether the time to clearance varied for RTCC-assisted property crimes in comparison to other forms of RTCC-assisted violent crimes. The trend line comparison of the model is presented in Figure 14 and the measures included in that model and coefficients are reported in Table 18. Each of the victim characteristics, human evidence, crime scene evidence, warrants, and manpower measures were included to account for their influence on the time taken to clear cases. As visible in Figure 14, after controlling for case characteristics the trend lines indicating the occurrence of case clearance over time overlap.\
\
With this adjustment, there are no apparent differences between the RTCC-assisted cases and the control sample in the time-to-case clearance trajectories. This was also evident in the coefficients (see Table 16). Holding constant the influence of all other measures there was no significant difference in the days to case clearance between the RTCC-assisted property crime cases and the control sample $( \\mathrm { B } = . . 1 3 0 , \\mathrm { S E } = . 8 7 8 , p =$ .591, n.s.). The findings here suggest that the implementation of RTCC information technologies in the investigation of property incidents has not reduced the amount of time that it takes to clear cases as compared to violent crime RTCC-assisted cases, even when considering case level circumstances.\
\
Among the remaining measures in the analysis, five were significantly related to the time that it took to clear cases. For victim characteristics, the only significant predictor was whether the victim was male. When the victim was male the case had significantly higher odds $( \\mathrm { O R } = 1 . 4 8 4 ) \_ { \ }$ ) of being cleared before female victims. Among human evidence measures, whether a public information flyer was disseminated was associated with 40 percent $\\left( \\mathrm { O R } = . 6 0 1 \\right) ^ { \\cdot }$ ) lower odds of being cleared before those cases without a flyer. Within the crime scene evidence characteristics, when a firearm or vehicle was used, the odds of faster case clearance was lower as compared to incidents that did not have these variables ( $8 6 %$ and $89 %$ , respectively). Finally, in cases that a cell phone warrant was obtained, it also took significantly longer to clear a case $( \\mathrm { O R } = . 5 8 9 $ ). The -2 log-likelihood and model\
\
Chi-Square coefficient were statistically significant indicating a good model fit $( \\chi ^ { 2 } =$ 70.060, 17 df, $p = \\leq . 0 0 1 $ .\
\

# Chapter Seven: Public Perceptions Associated with Police Use and Deployment of Technology\
\
# Introduction\
\
The primary aim of this chapter was to investigate how the public perceives police technology through the use of randomized controlled trial research design. The study sought to evaluate public acceptance of technology that is relevant to the operational decision-making processes of law enforcement agencies equipped with real-time crime center units. To this end, survey participants were assigned to differing treatment groups, thereby allowing for a systematic assessment of their attitudes toward such technologies.\
\
The present study pursued two principal objectives: firstly, to ascertain if the tone of narratives and the extent of disseminated public messaging and information pertaining to police technology use and deployment could enhance public acceptance of these technologies, and secondly, to identify the factors that exert the most significant impact on public approval of police technology, such as respondent characteristics and experiences. To attain these objectives, the study was framed by two key research questions: (1) “What is the influence of the messaging strategy employed to communicate information on the use and deployment of police technology in communities on public approval?” and (2) “Which of the three tested concepts - police legitimacy, transparency, or effectiveness - exerts the most substantial influence on obtaining public approval of police technology?”\
\
The chapter begins with a review of the relevant literature concerning the public's attitudes towards police technology. This is followed by a section outlining the analytical methodology employed, including a detailed overview of the data collection procedure and the analytical measures implemented. In chapter eight, the limitations of this study and policy implications will be presented.\
\
# Review of Literature\
\
The dynamic between police officers and the communities they serve has been characterized by a growing sense of tension and fragmentation. This trend is indicative of a broader societal shift, as traditional law enforcement practices are being questioned for their legitimacy and effectiveness. This phenomenon has been fueled in part by high-profile cases of police brutality, particularly those involving the killings of African Americans, which have prompted some of the largest protests in American history, as well as widespread calls for systemic change in law enforcement practices (Buchanan et al., 2020). The root causes of this erosion of trust and sense of alienation are not confined to any one specific region, but rather represent a pervasive phenomenon with significant implications for the future of policing.\
\
Moreover, Gallup undertakes extensive annual surveys encompassing all 50 states of the United States, with the aim of comprehending the degree of confidence that Americans place in various institutions throughout their nation. Over the course of the past few decades, spanning back to 1993, the police have consistently garnered a noteworthy level of trust from the American populace in comparison to other establishments (Gallup, 2023). Throughout the period spanning 1993 to 2018, public confidence in the police exhibited fluctuations, with the lowest recorded levels occurring in 1993 and 2015 (both registering at $52 %$ ), while the pinnacle of trust was observed in 2004, boasting an impressive $64 %$ . Overall, the police have commanded considerable respect, maintaining an average level of trust at a commendable $5 7 %$ over this timespan. However, more recent findings extracted from Gallup's poll reveal a downward trajectory in the confidence bestowed upon the police. Specifically, in 2020, less than half of the American population, a mere $4 8 %$ , expressed possessing "a great deal" or "quite a lot" of faith in the police (Gallup, 2023). This occurrence signifies the first instance since 1993 in which confidence in the police has descended below the critical threshold of $50 %$ .\
\
Nonetheless, the integration of technological advancements, such as body-worn cameras, has frequently been proposed as a strategy to harness the digital era and foster positive rapport between law enforcement and society (Lum, Stoltz, Koper &Scherer, 2019). However, it is paradoxical that many instances of police misconduct and aggressive enforcement actions have been documented through video surveillance facilitated by the implicated law enforcement agency or by private citizens utilizing their smartphones or social media platforms (Kochel & Skogan, 2021). Thus, establishing the status of public approval of police technologies has been multifaceted and complex, as the promise of enhanced public safety with limited intrusion on personal privacy is welcomed by civilians, whereas contrasting views on surveillance technology are influenced by their trust in government and police agencies (Pavoe & Esposti, 2012).\
\
However, based on the findings of Saulnier and Thompson's (2016) research, it appears that the degree to which police technology is embraced by the public depends on the intended purpose of the technology or the justification for its implementation. Further, in situations where individuals are not fully informed about the specific goals of the technology or where there is no clear victim, concerns about the underlying motives behind surveillance may be heightened (Sakiyama, 2016). Legitimacy, in conjunction with trust, can also serve as a criterion by which individuals assess whether the police are justified in expanding the deployment of technological tools. Existing literature suggests that perceiving law enforcement agencies as fair and equitable entities fosters endorsement of behaviors that align with established normative boundaries of policing (Gerber & Jackson, 2017). Whereas accountability and transparency are then seen as levers that promote elements of police legitimacy and trust. Nevertheless, in light of the current scrutiny faced by law enforcement agencies and the recent movement to defund these organizations, gaining public approval has become a crucial priority for police departments nationwide.\
\
# Public Perception of Technology\
\
Bragias, Hine, and Fleet (2021) recently conducted a study exploring the public's attitudes towards the utilization of facial recognition technology (FRT) by law enforcement agencies. The researchers focused on analyzing comments posted on YouTube, recognizing both the potential benefits of FRT for enhancing police efficiency and safety, as well as the challenges of striking a balance between security and public support. The study analyzed a diverse dataset of 460 videos pertaining to police use of FRT from November 2017 to March 2021. Employing sentiment analysis, the videos were categorized as positive, neutral, or negative, with only neutral videos being included to minimize biases, resulting in a set of 71 videos created by various individuals, local media, and television recordings.\
\
The study's findings revealed that a substantial majority $( 7 5 % )$ of the comments expressed negative sentiments, while only $1 5 %$ were positive and $9 %$ neutral, indicating a predominantly unfavorable perception of police FRT usage. The public voiced apprehension regarding the accuracy of the technology, given that several private companies are developing FRTs with varying degrees of success. Additionally, concerns were raised about potential abuses of power and authority by both the state and police in using FRT, as well as the erosion or violation of individual rights and freedoms, indicating discontent with both state governance and private enterprise. To generate support for FRT, the researchers proposed addressing these concerns and highlighting its potential benefits for criminal investigations through public education campaigns outlining the technology's usage, trials, and accompanying legislation.\
\
Even more recently, a national survey of public support for FRT and associated privacy and safety concerns was carried out by Miethe et al., (2023). This study utilized a sample generated through Qualtrics' online panel of survey participants and was conducted in March 2022, with 612 U.S. adults participating. The results of their study showed variability in support across different policing contexts, with $70 %$ of respondents supporting FRT use overall. Support was also found to be higher in high-profile policing activities, than in public-order situations with the highest support for identifying terrorists and the lowest for identifying jaywalkers. Additionally, the impact of privacy concerns on FRT support was found to depend on an individual's level of trust in the police. For example, among those with low trust, higher privacy concerns are associated with lower support for FRT.\
\
On a similar note, but from an international perspective, Bradford et al. (2020) utilized an RCT vignette approach survey data from 1,092 Londoners collected between 23 May and 4 June 2018 to investigate their perspectives on the Metropolitan Police Service's (MPS) gathering and retention of personal data, particularly in relation to Live\
\
Facial Recognition (LFR).22 The findings are very relevant to the current study. Here the scholars found that there was support for LFR was higher when used to identify potential terrorists or those wanted for serious violent crimes, but lower for minor crimes and nuisance behavior.\
\
Additionally, the study uncovered some variation in views across different sociodemographic categories, with older individuals and white individuals more likely to view police use of LFR as acceptable. Concern about crime and political orientation were also significantly associated with legitimacy, whereas there was little variation in views by gender, country of birth, social class, or crime victimization. The study's models employing different measures of acceptability also revealed a weaker association between privacy concerns and acceptability when participants were prompted to consider specific crimes, indicating that privacy concerns become relatively less important for many individuals in such cases.\
\
Scholars have also recently employed similar analytical strategies to determine public approval of drone usage. For example, Heen, Lieverman & Miethe (2018) investigated the relationship between individual attitudes towards police legitimacy and effectiveness, and the support for police UAV use. The researchers hypothesized that greater legitimacy and effectiveness would lead to increased acceptance of proactive policing measures, while the use of UAVs for reactive policing activities would receive high levels of support regardless of attitudes towards police legitimacy. To test this hypothesis, a national survey was conducted using Qualtrics and Mechanical Turk, resulting in a sample of 481 adult respondents collected in May 2015.\
\
The results of the study showed that support for UAV use was generally higher in voluntary citizen-initiated contexts, which are reactive in nature, while support was lower for proactive police-initiated interactions. Furthermore, victimization and previous contact with police were found to influence support for UAV use in both proactive and reactive applications. African Americans, who generally had lower confidence and trust in the police, were also less likely to support UAV usage compared to White individuals.\
\
Merola, Lum & Murphy (2018) further implemented a survey-based experiment to understand community perceptions following the adoption of a new policy by an agency, specifically focusing on the use of LPRs by the police. The experiment was conducted in Fairfax County, Virginia, with a sample size of 405 participants. Two versions of the survey were created, with the control group receiving questions about police perceptions first and LPR functions second, while the experimental group encountered the sections in reverse order. The scholars found that roughly $90 %$ of respondents were uncertain if their local police department used LPR, indicating limited exposure to LPR in their communities and a lack of awareness regarding its implementation. Further, the experiment revealed that when participants were given LPR information, their trust in the police declined compared to the control group.\
\
# Current Study\
\
The primary aim of the research was to investigate the attitudes of the general populace towards the utilization and deployment of technologies commonly housed within real-time crime center units. To accomplish this objective, a randomized experimental methodology was employed, where treatment groups were provided with a personalized vignette that portrayed a descriptive scenario of police employment of technology. The present investigation was made feasible thanks to the financial backing of the Association of Doctoral Programs in Criminology and Criminal Justice ($1,000) and the Kimberly and Dorothea Green Fellowship for Advanced Doctoral Students ($1,000). These funds were employed to acquire targeted survey responses via the market research provider, Pollfish.23 The earlier section of the study highlighted pertinent scholarly works, which offered some empirical backing for the following hypotheses postulated.\
\
\[H1\] The treatment group depicting police use and deployment of police technology in a positive manner will be associated with improved levels of public approval of technology.\
\
\[H2\] The treatment group depicting police use and deployment of police technology in a negative manner will be associated with heightened levels of public approval of technology.\
\
\[H3\] The treatment groups receiving only a neutral description of the technologies commonly used and deployed by police agencies will be associated with reduced levels of public approval.\
\
\[H4\] The most apparent difference in pre and post stimuli scores will be observed between the control and positive treatment group.\
\
\[H5\] Of all three concepts tested, perceptions of police legitimacy will have the most significant impact on the approval of police use and deployment of police technology.\
\
# Analytical Approach\
\
The study employed various statistical analyses to address the research questions. Internal consistency or reliability of the survey items was assessed using Cronbach's alpha coefficient. Descriptive statistics were then presented using analysis of variance and Fisher's Exact Test to determine significant differences between the control group and treatment groups. One-way ANOVA, along with a Bonferroni post hoc group comparison, was conducted to compare police legitimacy, transparency, and effectiveness post-test scores. The study also analyzed police technology approval pre and post-test scores and used a difference in differences in test. Finally, ordinal regression analysis was performed to identify predictors of increased public approval for police technology use and deployment.\
\
To conduct this RCT experimental vignette study, Pollfish was selected over other survey marketing tools for a several reasons. Firstly, Pollfish refrains from dispensing cash or airline incentive points, unlike most other platforms, which are prone to generate static and cumulative incentives, ultimately resulting in the emergence of “professional surveytakers” (Andrade, 2022). These individuals possess the skills to circumvent screening questions and are motivated to complete as many surveys as possible without comprehending the underlying rationale questions.\
\
Instead, Pollfish engages directly with app developers, who can formulate a distinct non-cash incentive to encourage genuine consumers to participate in surveys without the risk of inducing them to become habitual survey-takers. For example, a news app may offer a premium article, while a fitness app may provide a free yoga lesson. The third-party app partners undergo a meticulous vetting process, and their compensation is contingent upon furnishing responses from individuals who fit the targeting criteria specified by the researcher and satisfy the quality controls. This approach diverges from the conventional methodology of impaneling respondents, which incurs a steep cost and results in the elimination of dubious responses.\
\
Additionally, over the past few decades, the realm of market research has undergone a significant transformation with the evolution of techniques utilized, which now spans from traditional random digit dialing to online panels and river sampling. This shift has been driven by a confluence of factors, including technological advancements, shifts in consumer behavior, and the identification of deficiencies in existing methodologies. Hence, Pollfish uses organic sampling built on a new delivery framework called Random Device Engagement, which is considered the future of market research by academics and researchers (Rothschild & Konitzer, 2018).\
\
Pollfish has also developed a proprietary algorithm that uses machine learning technology to detect and remove fraudulent or suspicious responses at the question level. By leveraging artificial intelligence, the platform can immediately eliminate insincere responses, ensuring the integrity and reliability of the collected data. Further, in this study, survey takers were randomly assigned to a treatment or control group. As most survey platforms do not offer the ability to test more than two concepts, the A/B testing feature provided within the Pollfish dashboard was particularly advantageous. This feature enables a researcher to evaluate multiple concepts simultaneously, making it a valuable tool for experimental research.\
\
# Survey Construction\
\
The survey comprised a total of 29 questions. The first question asked for the participant's consent to participate (as per Pollfish protocol) and the second question was presented in a descriptive manner, explaining the purpose of the survey (as per the approved FIU IRB protocol). Participants were informed that they could exit the survey at any point in time (see Appendix C for survey instrument). The remaining questions within the survey were close-ended, single-response questions. Certain demographic questions, including age, education, income, employment, and U.S. census region were provided by the survey marketing tool free of cost. Other respondent characteristics were acquired through the survey instrument itself, such as the gender of the participant and their political affiliation. These questions were later recoded into dichotomous measures.\
\
Respondent experiences were also collected by inquiring as to whether they were a victim of a crime or had an interaction with a police officer in the past three years, whether they have ever been arrested for a crime, and their overall perception of crime in their neighborhood. These questions were included as, in some cases, they are known to influence perception of police practices (Koster, Van der Leun & Kunst, 2020). The responses to these questions were then transformed into dichotomous measures, where "yes" was assigned a value of 1, while "no" was assigned a value of 0, with the exception of the question relating to neighborhood perception, which was recorded on an ordinal scale.\
\
All remaining questions were presented using a Likert scale, where a score of one (1) represented "strongly disagree” and a score of five (5) represented "strongly agree." The pretest included several questions, but one question in particular, which was the dependent variable for the ordinal regression model, was of primary interest. This question asked participants to rate their approval of police technology on a scale of one (1) to ten (10), with one indicating no approval and ten indicating high approval. 24 The same question was also asked as a post-test after the stimuli were administered.\
\
Further, the survey participants were assigned to one of four groups through random assignment to meet the criteria for a randomized controlled trial and stratified by age, gender, and race. The control group was not exposed to any stimuli but was instructed to proceed with answering the post-test survey questions. The three treatment groups consisted of the positive stimuli group, the negative stimuli group, and neutral stimuli group. Overall, the intent was to observe the tone and extent of messaging needed to educate the public and achieve levels of acceptance with respect to the deployment of police technologies within communities. A description of each stimulus is provided in Appendix D.\
\
# Positive Stimuli\
\
The principal aim of the positive stimuli was to present a favorable perception to survey respondents concerning the utilization of technology by law enforcement agencies as a means to alleviate public concerns. In this context, the treatment group was introduced to the technical resources available within RTCCs and the measures taken by police authorities to ensure the ethical and community-oriented use of these investigative tools. Additionally, a specific example was provided to demonstrate how these technological resources enhance crime prevention, victim support, and overall clearance rates.\
\
In terms of policy considerations, the purpose of the positive depiction was to illustrate that community members are willing to adopt law enforcement technology, provided they are informed of the relevant protocols and the manifold ways in which such technologies confer benefits upon them. Consequently, an agency that prioritizes transparency in its utilization and dissemination of technology is likely to achieve greater acceptance of such technology within its jurisdiction.\
\
# Negative Stimuli\
\
Conversely, the negative treatment group was established to present survey respondents with an unfavorable outlook on the application of police technology. This was implemented to assess the impact of this information on their overall perception of police practices in relation to the use and implementation of such technologies. While participants were already familiar with the existence of these technologies in RTCCs, the scenario portrayed their usage and implementation in an unfavorable light. Moreover, the description emphasized that these technologies were not advantageous to the community and emphasized the fact that their effectiveness as investigative tools was unproven.\
\
The proposition put forth posits that a pessimistic portrayal of police technology or insufficient disclosure regarding its implementation can impede the general populace's view of the usefulness of these tools, thereby gradually eroding their trust in the validity and efficacy of police procedures. If this supposition were to materialize, it would underscore the crucial importance of conducting research grounded in empirical evidence and disseminating this knowledge transparently to the public.\
\
# Neutral Stimuli\
\
The purpose of the neutral stimuli group was to provide a succinct explanation of the technologies commonly utilized in RTCCs. This group can be viewed as representing "treatment as usual," as this treatment is already present, to some degree, in practice (Freedland et al., 2011). Consequently, participants received a summary of the functions of CCTV cameras, license plate readers, gunshot detection systems, and facial recognition software. While some survey respondents may have had prior knowledge of these technologies, the results of this vignette would reflect the perspectives of these individuals without any positive or negative influence or manipulation.\
\
Intuitively, two outcome scenarios could arise. Firstly, participants may recognize the usefulness of individual technologies commonly employed in an RTCC setup, leading to greater comprehension and higher acceptance of these technologies. Alternatively, some individuals may associate systems such as facial recognition and LPRs with privacy issues, resulting in less positive opinions. Nevertheless, further analysis of the outcomes related to this vignette could be valuable in guiding law enforcement efforts to improve transparency.\
\
# Sample Size\
\
A power analysis tool and sample size calculator were used to estimate the minimum sample size required for the experiment, given a desired significance level, effect size, and statistical power. According to the G-power software tool for a small to medium effect size of .20, a sample of 321 participants is recommended to optimally detect an effect of the independent variables. Moreover, according to Raosoft, a sample size of 377 offers a margin of error of $+ / - 5 %$ at the $9 5 %$ confidence level. To ensure the attainment of the desired sample size, accounting for the possibility of invalid responses, a total sample size of 400 was deemed appropriate here.\
\
While increasing the sample size to 500 would improve the reliability by only $0 . 5 %$ , the cost of obtaining 100 additional responses may not justify the marginal benefit. Industry experts in survey marketing also contend that sample sizes beyond 400 often result in diminishing returns on investment (Kuhn, 2018). Further, to ensure the representativeness of the survey results, the sample was stratified based on age, race, and gender. Stratification is an effective method for minimizing bias in the data and obtaining a more precise and reliable estimate of the opinions and behaviors of different demographic groups, which is essential for informed decision-making and developing targeted interventions (Dunn & Shultis, 2023).\
\
# Survey Dissemination\
\
The survey was distributed via Pollfish in April of 2023 and results were published within one day of the dissemination. The survey began with an initial screening question that served as a form of consent, requiring participants to confirm that they were at least eighteen years old and willing to proceed with the survey despite its sensitive nature. Pollfish classified the survey as sensitive due to its questions regarding participants' interactions with law enforcement officials and their arrest history within the past three years. The average completion time for the survey was eight minutes, and participant engagement was remarkable, with a response rate of $9 8 %$ . Initially, 392 out of 400 participants proceeded to answer the survey questions after passing the screening process.\
\
An additional eight individuals were selected through Pollfish's random sampling procedure to achieve the target sample size of 400 participants.\
\
Of the total responses, 55 were eliminated from subsequent analyses for having “don’t know” responses, bringing the final count to 345. Accordingly, seventeen respondents were removed from the control group, thirteen from treatment group one, nine from treatment group two, and sixteen from treatment group three (see Table 17). The decision to incorporate the “don’t know” option in the survey was informed by the extant literature, which suggests that when a survey lacks a “don't know” (DK) option, respondents feel compelled to make a decision regarding the concept in question (Denman et al., 2018). Furthermore, individuals lacking sufficient knowledge on a topic may select an answer that does not align with their true beliefs or opt to skip the question altogether.\
\
# Reliability Testing\
\
Prior to data analysis, reliability testing was conducted to ensure precision and accuracy in the measurement of the primary concepts of interest (refer to Table 18). The reliability evaluation was carried out using Cronbach's alpha coefficient, a statistical tool employed to determine the internal consistency and dependability of a set of survey items. The primary objective of this technique is to ascertain whether a group of items consistently gauges the same fundamental construct. The resulting alpha score provides a standardized indication of agreement among the items, ranging from 0 to 1, with higher values signifying greater coherence between the items. Consequently, high values of Cronbach's alpha suggest that respondents' answers across multiple questions are consistent, whereas low values indicate that the construct being measured lack reliability (Tavakol & Dennick, 2011).\
\

# Findings\
\
The following section presents the findings of the study. However, prior to identifying any differences between the treatment groups and the control group, a one-way ANOVA was utilized, along with Fisher's Exact Test to determine any significant differences between multiple groups of data. Ensuring that the demographic characteristics of all treatment and control groups are comparable is paramount, as it assists in guaranteeing that any observed variations between the groups are exclusively attributable to the studied treatment and not any extraneous factors such as age, gender, ethnicity, or socioeconomic status.\
\
When groups are not matched for demographic characteristics, it becomes challenging to differentiate between the effects of the treatment and other factors, and observed differences may be due to chance or biases (Stuart, 2010). Matching groups based on demographic characteristics can minimize the impact of these confounding factors and enhance the study's validity and reliability. Furthermore, having comparable groups is vital for generalizing the study results to the broader population. If groups are not matched for demographic characteristics, the findings may not be applicable to the larger population, or they may only be relevant to a specific subset of the population.\
\
However, as depicted in Table 19, there were no significant differences between the groups in terms of demographic characteristics. This means that the groups were comparable in terms of factors such as age, gender, income level, victim experiences and crime perception of their neighborhood. Here, eleven of the thirteen measures were coded dichotomously, meaning that they were either present (coded as 1) or absent (coded as 0). The age variable was measured as a continuous variable, while crime perception of neighborhoods was measured on a scale from 0 to 9, with higher scores indicating higher perceptions of crime in the neighborhood.\
\
# Impact of Stimuli\
\
The present study aimed to investigate the divergent effects of various treatment types using analyses of variance with post hoc Bonferroni corrections (See Appendix E). Generally speaking, statistical analyses rely on two techniques to manage the family-wise error rate in multiple comparisons, namely Bonferroni corrections and Tukey's HSD (honest significant difference) test. The selection of either approach hinges upon the research inquiry and the attributes of the data. Bonferroni correction displays greater efficacy when the number of comparisons is minimal, while Tukey's HSD test exhibits more strength when assessing numerous means (Lee & Lee, 2018). Thus, for the purpose of this analysis, Bonferroni correction was employed. All four groups were subjected to between-group comparisons. Homogeneity of variance was tested using Levene's test, and significant results were observed for all ANOVAs. Accordingly, Welch's F was repor\

\
The Welch test is commonly used when the assumption of equal variances between two groups and equal sample sizes is violated, a common occurrence in real-world applications (Lakens, 2019). The test is designed to be more robust than the standard t-test when the variances of the two groups are different. The null hypothesis in the Welch test is that there is no significant difference between the means of the two groups, while the alternative hypothesis is that there is a significant difference. The test statistic is calculated as the difference between the means of the two groups divided by the standard error of the difference.\
\
Here it is imperative to acknowledge that the Likert scale, due to its inherent design, follows a pattern whereby a lower mean score is indicative of a stronger negative sentiment towards the variable under investigation. In this particular context, the mean is calculated by summing up the responses to the three questions that pertain to the variable of interest. The specific questions used in each analysis are listed in the reliability testing section. It is noteworthy that the highest achievable score on this scale is 15, which signifies an overall view that the police force is deemed legitimate, transparent, and effective. Conversely, a lower score suggests that the participants perceive the police force as being illegitimate, not transparent, and ineffective in their operational practices. As evidenced in Figure 15., the highest mean scores were achieved for the effectiveness measure, followed closely by legitimacy and transparency.\
\

Legitimacy\
\
The police legitimacy mean score of the control group, which did not undergo any intervention, was recorded at 10.41 $\\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 9 8$ ). Treatment group one, which was exposed to a positive vignette, had a higher mean score of 10.92 $\\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 7 1$ ) in comparison to treatment group two, which received a negative vignette, and had a mean score of 10.25 $( \\mathrm { S D } = 3 . 1 2 )$ . The group that received neutral information achieved the highest mean score of 11.12 $\\mathrm { \ S D } = 2 . 6 5$ ). Despite Levene's test of homogeneity failing to reveal any significant differences in variances $( p = . 2 6 7 )$ , Welch's test was still conducted $( p = . 1 5 6 )$ , and a post hoc analysis was performed to visualize the distinctions in mean scores among the groups. Transparency\
\
The mean score associated with technology transparency for the control group, without an intervention was 9.42 $\\mathrm { \\Delta S D } = 2 . 5 9$ ). The group that received the positive vignette had a higher mean response score $( \\mathrm { M } = 1 0 . 6 9 $ , $\\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 3 3$ ) compared to both the negative treatment group $( \\mathrm { M } = 9 . 5 9 , \\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 9 9 )$ and the neutral treatment group $( \\mathrm { M } = 1 0 . 3 0 , \\mathrm { S D } =$ 2.55). Levene’s test of homogeneity suggested significant differences between the variances $( p \ : = \ : . 0 0 5 )$ , leading to the reporting of Welch’s test $( p \ : = . 0 0 3 )$ that indicated significant differences between the means of the compared groups. This suggests that the difference in means is likely due to the different treatment groups.\
\
To identify where the differences lie, a Bonferroni test was conducted. Results indicated a significant difference between the control group and the positive treatment group with a mean difference of 1.278, revealing that the control group perceived police as significantly less transparent. Furthermore, a significant difference was observed between the positive treatment group and negative treatment group $( \\mathrm { M } = 1 . 0 9 6 )$ ), suggesting that those receiving a positive vignette perceived police as more transparent compared to those who received less favorable information.\
\
# Effectiveness\
\
The technology effectiveness mean score for the control group, without intervention, was 11.11 $\\mathrm { ( S D } = 2 . 2 6 )$ . Among the treatment groups, the positive vignette group obtained the highest response mean $( \\mathrm { M } = 1 1 . 4 7 , \\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 5 0 )$ , surpassing the negative treatment group $( \\mathrm { M } = 1 0 . 4 6$ , $\\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 7 3$ ) and the neutral information group $( \\mathrm { M } = 1 1 . 3 7$ , SD $= 2 . 6 1 $ ). Levene's test of homogeneity also revealed significant differences between the variances $( p = < . 0 4 1 )$ ). As a result, Welch's test was reported $( p = < . 0 5 0 )$ ), indicating that the means of the groups were significantly different from each other. The post hoc analysis indicated that the positive treatment group differed significantly from the negative treatment group in terms of technology effectiveness, with a mean difference of 1.1010.\
\
This finding suggests that the negative group perceived the police to be significantly less effective in their use and. No other significant differences were observed.\
\
Approval of Police Technology Pre-Post Test\
\
Table 20 displays the mean pre-test and post-test scores for police technology approval in each group. To determine the significance of the differences, a paired samples t-test was conducted. The control group had a pre-test mean score of 5.40 $\\mathrm { S D } = 2 . 0 7 $ ) and a slightly lower post-test mean score of 5.29 $\\mathrm { \\Delta S D } = 1 . 9 8 \\mathrm { \\Omega }$ ). In contrast, the positive treatment group had a higher pre-test mean score of 5.44 $\\mathrm { { S D } } = 2 . 5 0 $ ) and a further increased posttest mean score of 5.64 $\\mathrm { ( S D = 2 } . 3 8 )$ ). The negative treatment group had a pre-test mean score of 5.30 $\\mathrm { \\Delta C D } = 2 . 3 6 \\mathrm { \\Omega }$ and a slightly lower post-test mean score of 5.26 $\\mathrm { \\Delta S D } = 2 . 3 6 $ ). The paired samples t-test showed no significant difference between the pre-test and posttest scores in these groups. However, the neutral treatment group had a significant increase in post-test scores compared to the pre-test means, with scores of 5.61 and 5.93, respectively. The effect size for the difference between the groups was calculated using Cohen’s d, resulting in a small effect size or no improvement in the post-test results.\
\
A follow-up analysis was conducted to evaluate whether the differences in pre-post scores among all the treatment groups were significantly different from the pre- and posttest difference in the control sample. The difference-in-difference approach was employed to identify significant variations in outcomes between the treatment and control groups during the pre- and post-treatment phases. This was accomplished by computing the differences in the pre-test and post-test scores of all treatment groups and control group and comparing the differences using an independent samples t-test. The findings revealed significant differences between the mean score differences of the pre-test and post-test for the neutral treatment group and the control sample, indicating that participants who received an explanation of the technologies achieved significantly higher scores in terms of public approval of police technology.\

Predictors of Increased Public Approval of Technology\
\
Table 21 displays the results of the nested ordinal regression model used to analyze the predictors of increased public approval for the deployment and use of police technology. All independent measures used in these models were examined for multicollinearity using the variance inflation factor (VIF) with no multicollinearity revealed. All VIF factors ranged from a low of 1.082 to the highest at 3.162.26\
\
Model one included perceived legitimacy, transparency, and effectiveness of technology, as well as responses from survey participants in the positive, negative, and neutral treatment groups, with the control group as the reference category for the treatment group variables. The analysis revealed that greater levels of police legitimacy were positively associated with higher levels of police technology approval $( b = . 3 7 0 , \\mathrm { S . E . } =$ .067 $\\stackrel { 7 } { , } p = < . 0 0 1$ ). Similarly, the same finding held true for effectiveness, where individuals who perceived the police as more effective in their use and deployment of police technology were also more likely to have higher scores on approval of police technology $( b = . 4 7 7 , \\mathrm { S . E . } = . 0 7 0 , p = < . 0 0 1 )$ . Police transparency did not achieve significance in model one, nor did any of the individual treatment group included within the model, although the neutral treatment group was close to achieving statistical significance. Here, the Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients were statistically significant, indicating good model fit. Moreover, the model accounted for roughly $5 5 %$ of the variation in perceptions of public approval of technology.\
\
Model two incorporated respondent characteristics and experiences. Here we see that the Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients were also statistically significant, indicating a good model fit, and an improvement over the previous model. Police legitimacy and effectiveness remained significant indicating that higher scores were associated with heightened approval of police use and deployment of technology. Transparency scores and treatment groups remained insignificant in this analysis, while only respondent age showed significance among all other variables included in the model. The results indicated that for every one unit increase in age, there was a predicted increase in the log odds of falling at a higher level of police technology approval. The Chi-square and -2 log-likelihood coefficients remained statistically significant, indicating a good model fit. Furthermore, the model explained an improved $56 %$ of the variation in the perceptions of public approval.\
